source_text:
Electrical risks have the potential to cause death and serious injury through direct or indirect contact with electricity. The most common risks are:

 Electric shock causing injury or death, if contact is made with energised parts.   
 Fire, arcing or explosion causing burns, for example when moisture or dust enter electrical equipment, or an explosive atmosphere is present.   
 Toxic gases causing illness or death.

matches.target_title: 147 Risk management
matches.target_text:
A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must manage risks to health and safety associated with electrical risks at the workplace, in accordance with Part 3.1. 

Example 

Electrical risks associated with the design, construction, installation, protection, maintenance and testing of electrical equipment and electrical installations at a workplace. 

Note 

WHS Act—section 19 (see regulation 9).

compliance.reasoning: The texts are functionally related because the PROVISION imposes a duty on the PCBU to manage risks to health and safety associated with electrical risks at the workplace (in accordance with Part 3.1). The POLICY text identifies common electrical risks (shock, fire/arcing/explosion, toxic gases) but does not state any risk management requirements or controls, does not reference compliance with Part 3.1, and does not address managing risks across the lifecycle activities noted in the PROVISION (design, construction, installation, protection, maintenance, testing). Therefore, the POLICY does not fulfill the obligation to manage electrical risks; it is descriptive rather than procedural/controls-based.
compliance.remediation: Update the policy to include a risk management framework aligned to Part 3.1: require hazard identification for electrical risks; risk assessment where needed; implementation of controls using the hierarchy of control (e.g., de-energisation/lockout-tagout, RCDs, isolation, guarding, exclusion zones, competent persons, inspection/testing, maintenance schedules); procedures for work on/near energised parts; controls for moisture/dust/explosive atmospheres and toxic gas exposure; roles/responsibilities (PCBU, supervisors, workers), training/competency, consultation, recordkeeping, and monitoring/review of control effectiveness. Explicitly reference the applicable WHS Act/Regulation provisions and ensure coverage of design/installation/maintenance/testing activities.

matches.target_title: 158 Preliminary steps
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure the following before electrical work on energised electrical equipment commences: 

(a) a risk assessment is conducted in relation to the proposed electrical work;   
(b) the area where the electrical work is to be carried out is clear of obstructions so as to allow for easy access and exit;   
(c) the point at which the electrical equipment can be disconnected or isolated from its electricity supply is: (i) clearly marked or labelled; and (ii) clear of obstructions so as to allow for easy access and exit by the worker who is to carry out the electrical work or any other competent person; and (iii) capable of being operated quickly;   
(d) the person authorises the electrical work after consulting with the person with management or control of the workplace. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(a), the risk assessment must be: 

(a) conducted by a competent person; and (b) recorded. 

(3) Subregulation (1)(c) does not apply to electrical work on electrical equipment if: 

(a) the work is to be carried out on the supply side of the main switch on the main switchboard for the equipment; and   
(b) the point at which the equipment can be disconnected from its electricity supply is not reasonably accessible from the work location.

compliance.reasoning: The POLICY text identifies key electrical risks (shock, fire/arcing/explosion, toxic gases) but does not set out the specific preconditions required before commencing electrical work on energised equipment. The PROVISION requires, before such work starts: (a) a risk assessment (by a competent person and recorded), (b) the work area to be clear of obstructions for access/exit, (c) the isolation/disconnection point to be clearly marked, unobstructed, and quickly operable (subject to a limited exception), and (d) formal authorisation after consultation with the person with management/control of the workplace. Because the POLICY only describes hazards and does not address these procedural/administrative controls, it does not demonstrate compliance with the PROVISION.
compliance.remediation: Update the policy/procedure for energised electrical work to explicitly require: (1) a recorded risk assessment conducted by a competent person before work starts; (2) verification that the work area is clear of obstructions to allow easy access and exit; (3) identification of the isolation/disconnection point and confirmation it is clearly marked/labelled, unobstructed, and can be operated quickly (and document when the supply-side/main switch exception applies); and (4) a documented authorisation step after consultation with the person with management or control of the workplace.

matches.target_title: 160 Contact with equipment being worked on
matches.target_text:
A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that, while electrical work is being carried out on energised electrical equipment, all persons are prevented from creating an electrical risk by inadvertently making contact with an exposed energised component of the equipment. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning: The POLICY text describes general electrical risks (shock, fire/arcing/explosion, toxic gases) but does not state any control requirement to ensure that, during energized electrical work, persons are prevented from inadvertently contacting exposed energized components. The PROVISION imposes a specific duty on the business/operator to implement measures that prevent inadvertent contact with exposed energized parts while energized work is performed. Because the POLICY lacks this specific preventive obligation or equivalent controls, it does not demonstrate compliance with the PROVISION.
compliance.remediation: Update the policy to include explicit requirements and controls for energized electrical work, including: prohibiting or strictly controlling work on energized equipment; requiring isolation/de-energization unless justified; implementing barriers/guards/insulation and exclusion zones to prevent inadvertent contact with exposed energized components; lockout/tagout and verification steps; use of competent/authorized workers, supervision, and permits; mandatory PPE and insulated tools; and procedures for managing access by other persons in the area.

matches.target_title: 166 Duty of person conducting a business or undertaking
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must ensure, so far as is reasonably practicable, that no person, plant or thing at the workplace comes within an unsafe distance of an overhead or underground electric line. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) If it is not reasonably practicable to ensure the safe distance of a person, plant or thing from an overhead or underground electric line, the person conducting the business or undertaking at the workplace must ensure that:
(a) a risk assessment is conducted in relation to the proposed work; and 

(b) control measures implemented are consistent with: 

(i) the risk assessment; and 

(ii) if an electricity supply authority is responsible for the electric line, any requirements of the authority. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

Notes 

1 [Relevant electricity safety legislation] will also apply to the person conducting the business or undertaking.

compliance.reasoning: The POLICY text identifies general electrical risks (shock, fire/arcing/explosion, toxic gases) but does not set out any specific controls or obligations regarding maintaining safe distances from overhead/underground electric lines, nor does it require a risk assessment and implementation of controls when safe distances are not reasonably practicable. The PROVISION imposes concrete duties on a PCBU to (1) ensure no person/plant/thing comes within an unsafe distance of electric lines so far as reasonably practicable, and (2) if that is not reasonably practicable, conduct a risk assessment and implement controls consistent with the assessment and any electricity authority requirements. Because the POLICY lacks these required measures, it does not demonstrate compliance with the PROVISION.
compliance.remediation: Update the policy (or add a specific procedure) to: (1) require maintaining prescribed/authority-defined safe approach distances from overhead and underground electric lines for people, plant and loads; (2) define how unsafe distance is determined (e.g., by voltage and electricity supply authority guidance) and require consultation/confirmation with the electricity supply authority where applicable; (3) mandate a documented risk assessment before work near electric lines when safe distances cannot be maintained; (4) specify required control measures (e.g., de-energisation/isolation, permits, spotters, exclusion zones, barriers, signage, equipment height limits, route planning, use of non-conductive tools, supervision and training) aligned to the risk assessment; and (5) include monitoring, review, and recordkeeping requirements to evidence implementation.

matches.target_title: 155 Duty to determine whether equipment is energised
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that, before electrical work is carried out on electrical equipment, the equipment is tested by a competent person to determine whether or not it is energised. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

Note 

Regulation 157 allows electrical testing to be carried out on electrical equipment for the purposes of this regulation. Regulation 161 sets out how the testing is to be carried out. 

(2) The person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that: 

(a) each exposed part is treated as energised until it is isolated and determined not to be energised; and   
(b) each high-voltage exposed part is earthed after being deenergised. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning: The POLICY text identifies electrical risks (shock, fire/arcing/explosion, toxic gases) but does not state the specific mandatory controls required by the PROVISION: (1) testing electrical equipment by a competent person before electrical work to determine if it is energised; (2)(a) treating exposed parts as energised until isolated and confirmed de-energised; and (2)(b) earthing each high-voltage exposed part after de-energising. Because these procedural obligations are not included, the policy does not demonstrate compliance with the provision.
compliance.remediation: Update the policy/procedure to explicitly require: (1) pre-work testing of electrical equipment by a competent person to confirm whether it is energised; (2) treating all exposed parts as energised until isolated and verified de-energised; (3) earthing high-voltage exposed parts after de-energising; and reference/align with the applicable testing and method requirements (e.g., the regulations governing electrical testing and how it must be performed).

matches.target_title: 144 Meaning of electrical equipment
matches.target_text:
(1) In this Part, electrical equipment means any apparatus, appliance, cable, conductor, fitting, insulator, material, meter or wire that: 

(a) is used for controlling, generating, supplying, transforming or transmitting electricity at a voltage greater than extra-low voltage; or   
(b) is operated by electricity at a voltage greater than extra-low voltage; or   
(c) is part of an electrical installation located in an area in which the atmosphere presents a risk to health and safety from fire or explosion; or   
(d) is, or is part of, an active impressed current cathodic protection system within the meaning of AS 2832.1:2015 (Cathodic protection of metals—Pipes and cables). 

(2) In this Part, electrical equipment does not include any apparatus, appliance, cable, conductor, fitting, insulator, material, meter or wire that is part of a vehicle that is a motor car or motorcycle if: 

(a) the equipment is part of a unit of the vehicle that provides propulsion for the vehicle; or   
(b) the electricity source for the equipment is a unit of the vehicle that provides propulsion for the vehicle. 

Note 

See the jurisdictional note in the Appendix.

compliance.reasoning: The POLICY text describes electrical risks (shock, fire/arcing/explosion, toxic gases) but does not define what constitutes “electrical equipment” or set scope boundaries. The PROVISION text is a definitional clause that specifies what is included/excluded as “electrical equipment” (e.g., > extra-low voltage equipment, equipment in explosive atmospheres, cathodic protection systems, and exclusions for certain vehicle equipment). For compliance, the policy should align its scope/definitions with the provision so that the risk controls clearly apply to the regulated set of electrical equipment. Because the policy lacks this definition/scope alignment, it does not fully satisfy the provision’s definitional requirements needed for consistent compliance application.
compliance.remediation: Update the policy to include (or explicitly reference) the regulatory definition of “electrical equipment,” including: (1) inclusion criteria (items used/operated > extra-low voltage; equipment in fire/explosion-risk atmospheres; impressed current cathodic protection systems per AS 2832.1:2015) and (2) the stated exclusion for certain motor car/motorcycle propulsion-related equipment. Ensure the policy’s risk statements and any controls apply to the defined scope.

matches.target_title: 150 Inspection and testing of electrical equipment
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must ensure that electrical equipment is regularly inspected and tested by a competent person if the electrical equipment is: 

(a) supplied with electricity through an electrical socket outlet; and   
(b) used in an environment in which the normal use of electrical equipment exposes the equipment to operating conditions that are likely to result in damage to the equipment or a reduction in its expected life span, including conditions that involve exposure to moisture, heat, vibration, mechanical damage, corrosive chemicals or dust. 

Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty. 

(2) In the case of electrical equipment that is new and unused at the workplace, the person conducting the business or undertaking: 

(a) is not required to comply with subregulation (1); and (b) must ensure that the equipment is inspected for obvious damage before being used. 

Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty. 

Note 

However, electrical equipment that is unsafe must not be used (see regulation 149). 

(3) The person must ensure that a record of any testing carried out under subregulation (1) is kept until the electrical equipment is: 

(a) next tested; or (b) permanently removed from the workplace or disposed of. 


Maximum penalty: tier I monetary penalty. 

(4) The record of testing: 

(a) must specify the following: (i) the name of the person who carried out the testing; (ii) the date of the testing; (iii) the outcome of the testing; (iv) the date on which the next testing must be carried out; and   
(b) may be in the form of a tag attached to the electrical equipment tested.

compliance.reasoning: The POLICY text identifies key electrical risks (shock, fire/arcing/explosion, toxic gases) but does not set out the specific controls required by the PROVISION. The PROVISION imposes enforceable duties to (1) regularly inspect and test certain socket-supplied electrical equipment used in harsh/damaging environments by a competent person, (2) inspect new/unused equipment for obvious damage before use, and (3) keep specified testing records until next test or removal/disposal, including required record fields. The POLICY contains no commitments or procedures for inspection/testing frequency, competency requirements, pre-use inspection for new equipment, recordkeeping, or tagging/record content. Therefore, it does not demonstrate compliance with the PROVISION’s obligations.
compliance.remediation: Update the policy/procedures to explicitly require: (1) regular inspection and testing by a competent person for socket-outlet supplied equipment used in environments with moisture/heat/vibration/mechanical damage/corrosive chemicals/dust; (2) pre-use inspection for obvious damage for new/unused equipment; (3) prohibition on use of unsafe equipment; and (4) retention of testing records until next test or removal/disposal, with records including tester name, test date, outcome, and next test due date (e.g., via equipment tags). Define responsibility, scope, and testing intervals consistent with applicable WHS guidance/standards.

matches.target_title: 156 De-energised equipment must not be inadvertently re-energised
matches.target_text:
A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical equipment that has been de-energised to allow electrical work to be carried out on it is not inadvertently re-energised while the work is being carried out. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning: The POLICY text identifies key electrical risks (shock, fire/arcing/explosion, toxic gases) but does not include any control or requirement to prevent inadvertent re-energisation of de-energised electrical equipment during electrical work. The PROVISION imposes a specific duty on a PCBU to ensure de-energised equipment is not inadvertently re-energised while work is being carried out. Because the POLICY does not address this obligation (e.g., lockout/tagout, isolation verification, access control), it does not demonstrate compliance with the PROVISION.
compliance.remediation: Update the policy/procedure to explicitly require controls preventing inadvertent re-energisation during electrical work, such as: documented isolation and de-energisation steps; lockout/tagout (LOTO) with personal locks/tags; verification of isolation (test for dead) before work; control of keys and access to isolators/switchboards; signage and barriers; permit-to-work/energisation authorization process; and training and supervision requirements.

matches.target_title: 157 Electrical work on energised electrical equipment—when permitted
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical work on energised electrical equipment is not carried out unless: 

(a) it is necessary in the interests of health and safety that the electrical work is carried out on the equipment while the equipment is energised; or Example 

It may be necessary that life-saving equipment remain energised and operating while electrical work is carried out on the equipment.   
(b) it is necessary that the electrical equipment to be worked on is energised in order for the work to be carried out properly; or   
(c) it is necessary for the purposes of testing required under regulation 155; or   
(d) there is no reasonable alternative means of carrying out the work. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) The electrical work that may be carried out under subregulation (1)(a), (b) and (d) may include testing of the energised electrical equipment.

compliance.reasoning: The POLICY text describes electrical risks (shock, fire/arcing/explosion, toxic gases) but does not set any controls or requirements about when energised electrical work may be performed. The PROVISION imposes a specific duty: energised electrical work must not be carried out unless one of the listed necessity conditions applies (health and safety necessity, work requires energisation, required testing, or no reasonable alternative). Because the POLICY does not prohibit energised work by default, does not require de-energisation/isolations, and does not specify the allowable exceptions, it does not demonstrate compliance with the PROVISION.
compliance.remediation: Update the policy to include a clear rule that electrical work on energised equipment must not be carried out unless one of the permitted conditions applies (as per (1)(a)-(d)), and explicitly require de-energisation/isolation as the default. Add a documented decision/approval process to justify energised work under the allowed exceptions (including testing), and reference the applicable testing requirement (regulation 155) where relevant.

matches.target_title: 164 Use of socket outlets in hostile operating environment
matches.target_text:
(1) This regulation applies in the following circumstances: 

(a) electrical equipment is used in an environment in which the normal use of electrical equipment exposes the equipment to operating conditions that are likely to result in damage to the equipment or a reduction in its expected life span, including conditions that involve exposure to moisture, heat, vibration, mechanical damage, corrosive chemicals or dust; 

(b) electrical equipment is moved between different locations in circumstances where damage to the equipment or to a flexible electricity supply cord is reasonably likely; 

(c) electrical equipment is frequently moved during its normal use; 

(d) electrical equipment forms part of, or is used in connection with, an amusement device. 

(2) In a circumstance set out in subregulation (1), a person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must ensure, so far as is reasonably practicable, that any electrical risk associated with the supply of electricity to the electrical equipment through a socket outlet is minimised by the use of an appropriate residual current device. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(3) Without limiting subregulation (2), the residual current device must have a tripping current that does not exceed 30 milliamps if electricity is supplied to the equipment through a socket outlet not exceeding 20 amps. 

(4) Subregulation (2) does not apply if the supply of electricity to the electrical equipment: 

(a) does not exceed 50 volts alternating current; or 

(b) is direct current; or 

(c) is provided through an isolating transformer that provides at least an equivalent level of protection; or 

(d) is provided from a non-earthed socket outlet supplied by an isolated winding portable generator that provides at least an equivalent level of protection.

compliance.reasoning: The POLICY identifies key electrical risks (electric shock, fire/arcing/explosion from moisture/dust/explosive atmospheres, and toxic gases). The PROVISION imposes a specific control requirement in certain higher-risk usage conditions (e.g., exposure to moisture, heat, vibration, mechanical damage, corrosive chemicals or dust; frequent movement; amusement devices): the PCBU must, so far as reasonably practicable, minimise electrical risk from socket-outlet supply by using an appropriate residual current device (RCD), with a ≤30 mA trip current for socket outlets ≤20 A, unless an explicit exemption applies (≤50 VAC, DC, isolating transformer equivalent protection, or isolated generator equivalent protection). The POLICY text is descriptive and does not state any requirement to use RCDs, specify trip ratings, define applicability conditions, or address the exemptions. Therefore, it does not demonstrate fulfillment of the PROVISION’s obligations and is not sufficient for compliance.
compliance.remediation: Update the policy/procedure to explicitly require use of an appropriate RCD for socket-outlet supplied equipment when used in the listed conditions (moisture/dust/corrosive chemicals/heat/vibration/mechanical damage, moved between locations, frequently moved, amusement devices), specify ≤30 mA trip current for outlets ≤20 A, and document when exemptions apply (≤50 VAC, DC, isolating transformer, isolated generator) including how equivalent protection is verified.

source_text:
Section 146 of the WHS Regulation defines “electrical work” as:

 connecting electricity supply wiring to electrical equipment or disconnecting electrical supply wiring from electrical equipment; or   
 installing, removing, adding, testing, replacing, repairing, altering or maintaining electrical equipment or an electrical installation.

matches.target_title: 146 Meaning of electrical work

compliance.reasoning: The POLICY text states that Section 146 defines “electrical work” and lists only the two inclusion limbs (connecting/disconnecting supply wiring; and installing/removing/adding/testing/replacing/repairing/altering/maintaining electrical equipment or an electrical installation). The PROVISION text includes those same inclusion limbs in subsection (1), but also adds a set of explicit exclusions in subsection (2) (e.g., plug-and-socket connection, non-electrical component work without electrical risk, replacing fuses/light bulbs, manufacturing process work, certain duct/conduit work under conditions, mounting equipment not related to connection, supervised assistance, and eligibility work). Because the POLICY purports to define the term but omits the exclusions that materially narrow the scope, it is not fully aligned with the PROVISION’s definition as used in that Part and could misstate compliance scope.
compliance.remediation: Update the POLICY definition to incorporate (or explicitly reference) the subsection (2) exclusions/notes from the PROVISION, or clarify that the POLICY is a partial summary and provide a direct cross-reference to the full regulatory definition and exclusions applicable in that Part.

matches.target_title: 144 Meaning of electrical equipment
matches.target_text:
(1) In this Part, electrical equipment means any apparatus, appliance, cable, conductor, fitting, insulator, material, meter or wire that: 

(a) is used for controlling, generating, supplying, transforming or transmitting electricity at a voltage greater than extra-low voltage; or   
(b) is operated by electricity at a voltage greater than extra-low voltage; or   
(c) is part of an electrical installation located in an area in which the atmosphere presents a risk to health and safety from fire or explosion; or   
(d) is, or is part of, an active impressed current cathodic protection system within the meaning of AS 2832.1:2015 (Cathodic protection of metals—Pipes and cables). 

(2) In this Part, electrical equipment does not include any apparatus, appliance, cable, conductor, fitting, insulator, material, meter or wire that is part of a vehicle that is a motor car or motorcycle if: 

(a) the equipment is part of a unit of the vehicle that provides propulsion for the vehicle; or   
(b) the electricity source for the equipment is a unit of the vehicle that provides propulsion for the vehicle. 

Note 

See the jurisdictional note in the Appendix.

compliance.reasoning: The POLICY defines “electrical work” by reference to activities performed on “electrical equipment” or an “electrical installation.” The PROVISION defines what counts as “electrical equipment” (and exclusions) for the relevant Part. This is functionally related because the scope of “electrical work” depends on what is considered “electrical equipment.” However, the PROVISION does not address the full scope needed to apply the POLICY definition: it does not define “electrical work,” does not define “electrical installation,” and introduces scope limitations/exclusions (e.g., vehicle motor car/motorcycle propulsion-related equipment) that are not reflected in the POLICY text. Therefore, using the PROVISION alone would not fully satisfy or align with the POLICY’s definitional requirements for determining what activities constitute “electrical work.”
compliance.remediation: Augment the definitional mapping so that: (1) “electrical equipment” used in applying the POLICY is explicitly tied to the PROVISION definition (including exclusions), and (2) obtain/include the applicable definition of “electrical installation” and any other relevant definitional provisions in the same regulatory context to ensure the full scope of “electrical work” can be determined consistently.

matches.target_title: 145 Meaning of electrical installation
matches.target_text:
(1) In this Part, electrical installation means a group of items of electrical equipment that: 

(a) are permanently electrically connected together; and (b) can be supplied with electricity from the works of an electricity supply authority or from a generating source. 

(2) An item of electrical equipment may be part of more than 1 electrical installation. 

(3) In subregulation (1)(a): 

(a) an item of electrical equipment connected to electricity by a plug and socket outlet is not permanently electrically connected; and   
(b) connection achieved through using works of an electricity supply authority is not a consideration in determining whether or not electrical equipment is electrically connected.

compliance.reasoning: The POLICY defines the scope of “electrical work,” which includes activities performed on “electrical equipment” or an “electrical installation.” The PROVISION defines what constitutes an “electrical installation” (including what counts as permanently connected and exclusions like plug-and-socket connections). This definition is directly relevant to determining whether particular activities fall within the POLICY’s definition of “electrical work,” but it does not itself impose an obligation or requirement that can be complied with or violated. Therefore, a pass/fail compliance determination is not applicable.

matches.target_title: 158 Preliminary steps
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure the following before electrical work on energised electrical equipment commences: 

(a) a risk assessment is conducted in relation to the proposed electrical work;   
(b) the area where the electrical work is to be carried out is clear of obstructions so as to allow for easy access and exit;   
(c) the point at which the electrical equipment can be disconnected or isolated from its electricity supply is: (i) clearly marked or labelled; and (ii) clear of obstructions so as to allow for easy access and exit by the worker who is to carry out the electrical work or any other competent person; and (iii) capable of being operated quickly;   
(d) the person authorises the electrical work after consulting with the person with management or control of the workplace. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(a), the risk assessment must be: 

(a) conducted by a competent person; and (b) recorded. 

(3) Subregulation (1)(c) does not apply to electrical work on electrical equipment if: 

(a) the work is to be carried out on the supply side of the main switch on the main switchboard for the equipment; and   
(b) the point at which the equipment can be disconnected from its electricity supply is not reasonably accessible from the work location.

compliance.reasoning: The POLICY TEXT defines the scope of what constitutes “electrical work” (e.g., connecting/disconnecting supply wiring; installing, testing, repairing, maintaining electrical equipment/installations). The PROVISION TEXT imposes specific preconditions that a PCBU must ensure before “electrical work on energised electrical equipment” commences (risk assessment by a competent person and recorded; clear access/egress; isolation/disconnection point marked, unobstructed, quickly operable; authorisation after consultation). Because the provision applies to a subset of the defined activity (electrical work, when performed on energised equipment), they are functionally related and a compliance check is necessary. However, the POLICY TEXT contains only a definition and does not include or evidence the required controls/steps mandated by the PROVISION TEXT. Therefore, based on the provided texts alone, the policy does not demonstrate compliance with the provision’s requirements.
compliance.remediation: Update the policy/procedure for electrical work on energised electrical equipment to explicitly require, before work starts: (1) a recorded risk assessment conducted by a competent person; (2) the work area is cleared to allow easy access and exit; (3) the isolation/disconnection point is clearly marked/labelled, unobstructed for access/exit, and quickly operable (or document the specific exception conditions where subreg (3) applies); and (4) formal authorisation after consultation with the person with management/control of the workplace. Implement supporting forms/checklists and training to evidence these steps.

matches.target_title: 147 Risk management
matches.target_text:
A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must manage risks to health and safety associated with electrical risks at the workplace, in accordance with Part 3.1. 

Example 

Electrical risks associated with the design, construction, installation, protection, maintenance and testing of electrical equipment and electrical installations at a workplace. 

Note 

WHS Act—section 19 (see regulation 9).

compliance.reasoning: The POLICY TEXT defines the scope of what constitutes “electrical work” (activities involving connecting/disconnecting supply wiring and installing/testing/maintaining electrical equipment or installations). The PROVISION TEXT imposes a duty on a PCBU to manage risks to health and safety associated with electrical risks at the workplace. These are functionally related because the definition of electrical work identifies activities that commonly create electrical risks and therefore fall within the risk-management duty. However, the POLICY TEXT only provides a definition and does not state any requirement to manage risks in accordance with Part 3.1 (or otherwise describe a risk management process). Therefore, it does not demonstrate compliance with the PROVISION’s obligation to manage electrical risks.
compliance.remediation: Add policy requirements that the PCBU must manage electrical risks arising from electrical work/electrical equipment and installations in accordance with WHS Regulation Part 3.1, including hazard identification, risk assessment (as required), implementation of control measures (e.g., elimination/minimisation), and ongoing review/maintenance/testing arrangements.

matches.target_title: 149 Unsafe electrical equipment
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must ensure that any unsafe electrical equipment at the workplace: 

(a) is disconnected (or isolated) from its electricity supply; and (b) once disconnected (or isolated): 

(i) is not reconnected until it is repaired or tested and found to be safe; or (ii) is replaced or permanently removed from use. 

Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty. 

(2) For the purposes of this regulation, electrical equipment or a component of electrical equipment is unsafe if there are reasonable grounds for believing it to be unsafe.

compliance.reasoning: The POLICY defines what constitutes “electrical work” (e.g., disconnecting supply wiring, testing, repairing, maintaining electrical equipment). The PROVISION imposes a duty on the PCBU to ensure unsafe electrical equipment is disconnected/isolated and not reconnected until repaired or tested and found safe, or replaced/removed. Because the required actions (disconnecting/isolating, repairing, testing) fall within the POLICY’s definition of electrical work, the POLICY is functionally related to the PROVISION and a compliance check is necessary. However, the POLICY text is only definitional and does not include the PROVISION’s required controls (mandatory disconnection/isolation, prohibition on reconnection until repair/test confirms safety, or replacement/removal). Therefore, the POLICY does not demonstrate compliance with the PROVISION’s obligations.
compliance.remediation: Add explicit requirements/procedures that: (1) unsafe electrical equipment must be immediately disconnected/isolated; (2) it must not be reconnected until repaired or tested and verified safe; (3) alternatively it must be replaced or permanently removed from use; and (4) define how “reasonable grounds” for believing equipment is unsafe will be identified and documented.

matches.target_title: 157 Electrical work on energised electrical equipment—when permitted
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical work on energised electrical equipment is not carried out unless: 

(a) it is necessary in the interests of health and safety that the electrical work is carried out on the equipment while the equipment is energised; or Example 

It may be necessary that life-saving equipment remain energised and operating while electrical work is carried out on the equipment.   
(b) it is necessary that the electrical equipment to be worked on is energised in order for the work to be carried out properly; or   
(c) it is necessary for the purposes of testing required under regulation 155; or   
(d) there is no reasonable alternative means of carrying out the work. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) The electrical work that may be carried out under subregulation (1)(a), (b) and (d) may include testing of the energised electrical equipment.

compliance.reasoning: The POLICY TEXT defines the scope of what constitutes “electrical work” (e.g., connecting/disconnecting supply wiring; installing, testing, repairing, maintaining electrical equipment/installations). The PROVISION TEXT imposes a duty on a PCBU to ensure that electrical work on energised electrical equipment is not carried out unless specific necessity-based exceptions apply. Because the provision’s obligation applies specifically to “electrical work,” the definition is functionally related and necessary to determine whether an activity falls within scope and triggers the prohibition/exception test. However, the POLICY TEXT is definitional and does not state controls, procedures, or evidence about when/why energised work is performed, so compliance with the PROVISION cannot be determined from the provided POLICY alone.
compliance.remediation: Add/implement an energised electrical work control procedure that: (1) prohibits energised electrical work by default; (2) requires documented justification that one of (a)-(d) applies before work proceeds; (3) explicitly covers testing allowances under (2); (4) requires verification that de-energised alternatives were assessed and found not reasonably practicable; and (5) retains records (risk assessment/permit/authorisation) demonstrating the applicable exception for each energised task.

matches.target_title: 152 Application of Division 4
matches.target_text:
This Division does not apply to work carried out by or on behalf of an electricity supply authority on the electrical equipment, including electric line-associated equipment, controlled or operated by the authority to transform, transmit or supply electricity. 

Note 

See the jurisdictional note in the Appendix.

compliance.reasoning: The POLICY defines the scope of “electrical work” (activities involving connecting/disconnecting supply wiring and installing/testing/repairing/maintaining electrical equipment or installations). The PROVISION establishes a scope exclusion for a Division: it does not apply to work carried out by or on behalf of an electricity supply authority on electrical equipment it controls/operates for transforming, transmitting, or supplying electricity. This directly relates to the POLICY-defined activities because such authority work would otherwise fall within “electrical work,” but the PROVISION clarifies that the Division’s requirements are not applicable in that context. Therefore, the texts are consistent and the PROVISION is compliant with the POLICY definition by appropriately carving out an applicability exception rather than contradicting the definition.

matches.target_title: 155 Duty to determine whether equipment is energised
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that, before electrical work is carried out on electrical equipment, the equipment is tested by a competent person to determine whether or not it is energised. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

Note 

Regulation 157 allows electrical testing to be carried out on electrical equipment for the purposes of this regulation. Regulation 161 sets out how the testing is to be carried out. 

(2) The person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that: 

(a) each exposed part is treated as energised until it is isolated and determined not to be energised; and   
(b) each high-voltage exposed part is earthed after being deenergised. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning: The POLICY TEXT defines what constitutes “electrical work” (including testing, repairing, altering or maintaining electrical equipment/installation). The PROVISION TEXT imposes specific safety obligations that must be met before electrical work is carried out on electrical equipment: (1) the equipment must be tested by a competent person to determine whether it is energised; and (2) exposed parts must be treated as energised until isolated and proven de-energised, and high-voltage exposed parts must be earthed after de-energisation. The POLICY TEXT contains only a definition and does not include these required pre-work testing and exposed-part handling controls. Therefore, based on the provided texts, the defined scope of electrical work is not accompanied by the required compliance obligations, so it is not compliant with the PROVISION requirements.
compliance.remediation: Update the policy/controls for all activities that fall within the definition of “electrical work” to require: (1) pre-work testing of electrical equipment by a competent person to confirm energisation status (performed in accordance with the applicable testing requirements referenced in the regulation); (2) treating all exposed parts as energised until isolated and confirmed de-energised; and (3) earthing high-voltage exposed parts after de-energisation. Document procedures, competency requirements, and verification records (e.g., test results, isolation/earthing checklists).

matches.target_title: 161 How the work is to be carried out
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical work on energised electrical equipment is carried out: 

(a) by a competent person who has tools, testing equipment and personal protective equipment that: 

(i) are suitable for the work; and (ii) have been properly tested; and (iii) are maintained in good working order; and 

(b) in accordance with a safe work method statement prepared for the work; and   
(c) subject to subregulation (5), with a safety observer present who has the competence and qualifications specified in subregulation (4). 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) The person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure, so far as is reasonably practicable, that the person who carries out the electrical work uses the tools, testing equipment and personal protective equipment properly. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(3) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(b), the safe work method statement must: 

(a) identify the electrical work; and   
(b) specify hazards associated with that electrical work and risks associated with those hazards; and   
(c) describe the measures to be implemented to control the risks; and   
(d) describe how the risk control measures are to be implemented, monitored and reviewed. 

(4) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(c): 

(a) the safety observer must be competent: 

(b) the safety observer must have been assessed in the previous 12 months as competent to rescue and resuscitate a person. 

(5) A safety observer is not required if: 

(a) the work consists only of testing; and   
(b) the person conducting the business or undertaking has conducted a risk assessment under regulation 158(1)(a) that shows that there is no serious risk associated with the proposed work.

compliance.reasoning: The POLICY text defines the scope of what constitutes “electrical work” (e.g., connecting/disconnecting supply wiring; installing, testing, repairing, maintaining electrical equipment/installations). The PROVISION text imposes specific duties on a PCBU when that electrical work is performed on energised electrical equipment (competent person with suitable/tested/maintained tools and PPE; SWMS; safety observer unless exempt; ensuring proper use of equipment; SWMS content requirements; safety observer competency and recent rescue/resuscitation assessment; exemption conditions). These requirements are consistent with and applicable to the defined category of “electrical work,” and the PROVISION adds operational controls rather than conflicting with the definition. No inconsistency or gap is evident between the definition and the imposed obligations.

source_text:
Electrical work does not include work on electrical equipment that is operated by electricity at extra-low voltage except electrical equipment that:

 is part of an electrical installation located in an area in which the atmosphere presents a risk to health and safety from fire or explosion; or  is, or is part of an active impressed current cathodic protection system.

Appendix 1 What is not electrical work in CSIRO within the Electrical Safety Procedure lists the activities that are considered electrical work under Section 146 of the WHS Regulations.

matches.target_title: 146 Meaning of electrical work

compliance.reasoning: The texts are functionally related because both define the scope of “electrical work” and exclusions, which determines when WHS electrical-work controls and licensing/supervision requirements apply. The POLICY states that electrical work does not include work on extra-low voltage equipment except in two specific cases (hazardous atmospheres; active ICCP systems) and refers to an internal appendix for activities considered electrical work under WHS Regs s146. The PROVISION defines electrical work broadly (connecting/disconnecting supply wiring; installing/testing/repairing/maintaining electrical equipment/installations) and then lists multiple explicit exclusions (plug-and-socket connection, non-electrical component work without electrical risk, simple replacements like fuses/bulbs, manufacturing process work, certain duct/conduit work under conditions, mounting/fixing not related to connection, supervised assisting, and supervised training work). The POLICY’s exclusion set is materially incomplete and framed differently: it focuses on extra-low voltage equipment and two exceptions, but does not incorporate or clearly align with the PROVISION’s broader definition and enumerated exclusions. As written, the POLICY could misclassify work as “electrical work” or “not electrical work” compared to the PROVISION, creating compliance risk unless Appendix 1 fully and accurately reproduces the regulatory definition/exclusions—which is not demonstrated in the POLICY text provided.
compliance.remediation: Amend the POLICY to explicitly align with the PROVISION’s definition of “electrical work” and its exclusions (a)–(h), either by incorporating them verbatim or by an unambiguous cross-reference to the applicable WHS Regulation clause. Ensure the extra-low voltage statement is consistent with the regulatory framework (i.e., treat it as an additional internal control only if it does not narrow or contradict the statutory definition). Update Appendix 1 to map each internal activity to the corresponding provision (definition or exclusion) and state that where there is any inconsistency, the WHS Regulations prevail.

matches.target_title: 144 Meaning of electrical equipment
matches.target_text:
(1) In this Part, electrical equipment means any apparatus, appliance, cable, conductor, fitting, insulator, material, meter or wire that: 

(a) is used for controlling, generating, supplying, transforming or transmitting electricity at a voltage greater than extra-low voltage; or   
(b) is operated by electricity at a voltage greater than extra-low voltage; or   
(c) is part of an electrical installation located in an area in which the atmosphere presents a risk to health and safety from fire or explosion; or   
(d) is, or is part of, an active impressed current cathodic protection system within the meaning of AS 2832.1:2015 (Cathodic protection of metals—Pipes and cables). 

(2) In this Part, electrical equipment does not include any apparatus, appliance, cable, conductor, fitting, insulator, material, meter or wire that is part of a vehicle that is a motor car or motorcycle if: 

(a) the equipment is part of a unit of the vehicle that provides propulsion for the vehicle; or   
(b) the electricity source for the equipment is a unit of the vehicle that provides propulsion for the vehicle. 

Note 

See the jurisdictional note in the Appendix.

compliance.reasoning: The texts are functionally related because the POLICY is defining the scope of “electrical work” by reference to electrical equipment operated at extra-low voltage and carving out exceptions that match parts (c) and (d) of the PROVISION’s definition of “electrical equipment.” However, the POLICY is not compliant with the PROVISION because it omits key elements of the statutory definition: the PROVISION defines electrical equipment broadly to include items used for controlling/generating/supplying/transforming/transmitting electricity at > extra-low voltage (1)(a), operated at > extra-low voltage (1)(b), and also includes specific inclusions (1)(c) and (1)(d). The POLICY only addresses the extra-low-voltage-operated category and its exceptions, and does not reflect the broader > extra-low voltage categories or the vehicle exclusion in (2). This creates a mismatch/under-specification that could lead to incorrect scoping of what is treated as electrical equipment/electrical work under the regulatory framework.
compliance.remediation: Update the POLICY (or explicitly cross-reference the WHS Regulations definition) to align with the full definition of “electrical equipment,” including: (1)(a) and (1)(b) categories (> extra-low voltage used for/operated by electricity), and consider whether to incorporate or explicitly note the vehicle exclusion in (2). Clarify that the extra-low-voltage carve-out is only one aspect of the overall definition/scope and ensure Appendix 1 references the complete regulatory criteria.

matches.target_title: 152 Application of Division 4
matches.target_text:
This Division does not apply to work carried out by or on behalf of an electricity supply authority on the electrical equipment, including electric line-associated equipment, controlled or operated by the authority to transform, transmit or supply electricity. 

Note 

See the jurisdictional note in the Appendix.

matches.target_title: 157 Electrical work on energised electrical equipment—when permitted
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical work on energised electrical equipment is not carried out unless: 

(a) it is necessary in the interests of health and safety that the electrical work is carried out on the equipment while the equipment is energised; or Example 

It may be necessary that life-saving equipment remain energised and operating while electrical work is carried out on the equipment.   
(b) it is necessary that the electrical equipment to be worked on is energised in order for the work to be carried out properly; or   
(c) it is necessary for the purposes of testing required under regulation 155; or   
(d) there is no reasonable alternative means of carrying out the work. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) The electrical work that may be carried out under subregulation (1)(a), (b) and (d) may include testing of the energised electrical equipment.

matches.target_title: 145 Meaning of electrical installation
matches.target_text:
(1) In this Part, electrical installation means a group of items of electrical equipment that: 

(a) are permanently electrically connected together; and (b) can be supplied with electricity from the works of an electricity supply authority or from a generating source. 

(2) An item of electrical equipment may be part of more than 1 electrical installation. 

(3) In subregulation (1)(a): 

(a) an item of electrical equipment connected to electricity by a plug and socket outlet is not permanently electrically connected; and   
(b) connection achieved through using works of an electricity supply authority is not a consideration in determining whether or not electrical equipment is electrically connected.

compliance.reasoning: The POLICY defines what is and is not “electrical work” (including exceptions for extra-low voltage equipment) and references WHS Regulations s146. The PROVISION defines “electrical installation” and clarifies what counts as permanently electrically connected (e.g., plug-and-socket is not permanent). Because the POLICY’s exclusion hinges on whether equipment is “part of an electrical installation” in certain hazardous areas, the regulatory definition of “electrical installation” is directly relevant and should be reflected to ensure correct classification. The POLICY does not incorporate or align with the PROVISION’s criteria for what constitutes an electrical installation (permanent electrical connection; plug-and-socket exclusion; supply authority connection not determinative). This omission can lead to misclassification of equipment as part of an installation (or not), affecting whether work is treated as “electrical work” under the stated exceptions.
compliance.remediation: Update the POLICY (or the referenced Appendix 1) to explicitly adopt/reflect the WHS regulatory definition of “electrical installation,” including: (1) the requirement for items to be permanently electrically connected together; (2) clarification that plug-and-socket connected equipment is not permanently connected; and (3) that connection via supply authority works is not determinative. Add guidance/examples for classifying extra-low voltage equipment in hazardous atmospheres based on this definition.

matches.target_title: 161 How the work is to be carried out
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical work on energised electrical equipment is carried out: 

(a) by a competent person who has tools, testing equipment and personal protective equipment that: 

(i) are suitable for the work; and (ii) have been properly tested; and (iii) are maintained in good working order; and 

(b) in accordance with a safe work method statement prepared for the work; and   
(c) subject to subregulation (5), with a safety observer present who has the competence and qualifications specified in subregulation (4). 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) The person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure, so far as is reasonably practicable, that the person who carries out the electrical work uses the tools, testing equipment and personal protective equipment properly. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(3) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(b), the safe work method statement must: 

(a) identify the electrical work; and   
(b) specify hazards associated with that electrical work and risks associated with those hazards; and   
(c) describe the measures to be implemented to control the risks; and   
(d) describe how the risk control measures are to be implemented, monitored and reviewed. 

(4) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(c): 

(a) the safety observer must be competent: 

(b) the safety observer must have been assessed in the previous 12 months as competent to rescue and resuscitate a person. 

(5) A safety observer is not required if: 

(a) the work consists only of testing; and   
(b) the person conducting the business or undertaking has conducted a risk assessment under regulation 158(1)(a) that shows that there is no serious risk associated with the proposed work.

compliance.reasoning: The provision imposes specific duties on a PCBU when electrical work is performed on energised electrical equipment (competent person with suitable/tested/maintained tools and PPE; SWMS content requirements; safety observer requirements and limited exception). The policy text instead defines what is (and is not) “electrical work” at extra-low voltage and points to an appendix listing activities considered electrical work. It does not address the required controls for energised electrical work (competency, SWMS requirements, safety observer, tool/PPE testing/maintenance and proper use). Therefore, while the texts are functionally related (both concern the scope and management of electrical work), the policy excerpt does not demonstrate compliance with the provision’s operational requirements.
compliance.remediation: Update the Electrical Safety Procedure (and/or the referenced Appendix) to explicitly require that energised electrical work is only performed: (1) by a competent person with suitable, tested and maintained tools/testing equipment/PPE; (2) under a SWMS that meets the provision’s minimum content (identify work, hazards/risks, controls, implementation/monitoring/review); (3) with a competent safety observer assessed within the last 12 months for rescue/resuscitation, unless the testing-only + risk assessment no-serious-risk exception applies; and (4) include a requirement that the PCBU ensures, so far as reasonably practicable, proper use of tools/testing equipment/PPE.

matches.target_title: 158 Preliminary steps
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure the following before electrical work on energised electrical equipment commences: 

(a) a risk assessment is conducted in relation to the proposed electrical work;   
(b) the area where the electrical work is to be carried out is clear of obstructions so as to allow for easy access and exit;   
(c) the point at which the electrical equipment can be disconnected or isolated from its electricity supply is: (i) clearly marked or labelled; and (ii) clear of obstructions so as to allow for easy access and exit by the worker who is to carry out the electrical work or any other competent person; and (iii) capable of being operated quickly;   
(d) the person authorises the electrical work after consulting with the person with management or control of the workplace. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(a), the risk assessment must be: 

(a) conducted by a competent person; and (b) recorded. 

(3) Subregulation (1)(c) does not apply to electrical work on electrical equipment if: 

(a) the work is to be carried out on the supply side of the main switch on the main switchboard for the equipment; and   
(b) the point at which the equipment can be disconnected from its electricity supply is not reasonably accessible from the work location.

compliance.reasoning: The POLICY text defines what is and is not considered “electrical work” (particularly excluding most extra-low voltage work, with specific exceptions) and references an internal appendix listing activities considered electrical work. The PROVISION text imposes specific mandatory preconditions that a PCBU must ensure before commencing electrical work on energised electrical equipment (risk assessment, access/egress, isolation point marking/access/operability, and authorisation/consultation; plus competency and recording requirements for the risk assessment). Because the POLICY is part of an Electrical Safety Procedure and addresses the scope of “electrical work,” it is functionally related to the regulatory obligations governing how certain electrical work (energised work) must be controlled. However, the POLICY does not state or incorporate the required controls for energised electrical work (risk assessment conducted by a competent person and recorded; ensuring clear access/egress; isolation/disconnection point requirements; authorisation after consultation), so it does not demonstrate compliance with the PROVISION.
compliance.remediation: Amend the Electrical Safety Procedure (and/or the referenced Appendix 1) to explicitly require, before any electrical work on energised electrical equipment commences: (1) a recorded risk assessment conducted by a competent person; (2) the work area is clear of obstructions for access/exit; (3) the disconnection/isolation point is clearly marked/labelled, unobstructed, and operable quickly (including addressing the subregulation (3) exception where applicable); and (4) formal authorisation of the energised work after consultation with the person with management/control of the workplace. Include roles/responsibilities (PCBU, supervisor, competent person), documentation/recordkeeping requirements, and a process/checklist to evidence these steps.

matches.target_title: 147 Risk management
matches.target_text:
A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must manage risks to health and safety associated with electrical risks at the workplace, in accordance with Part 3.1. 

Example 

Electrical risks associated with the design, construction, installation, protection, maintenance and testing of electrical equipment and electrical installations at a workplace. 

Note 

WHS Act—section 19 (see regulation 9).

compliance.reasoning: The provision imposes a duty on the PCBU to manage risks to health and safety associated with electrical risks at the workplace (in accordance with Part 3.1), covering electrical equipment and installations across their lifecycle (design, installation, maintenance, testing, etc.). The policy text instead focuses on defining what is/is not “electrical work,” carving out extra-low voltage equipment except in specific high-risk contexts, and refers to an appendix listing activities considered electrical work. This definitional scope does not state or demonstrate a risk management process or controls for managing electrical risks as required by the provision. Therefore, the policy does not adequately address the obligation to manage electrical risks.
compliance.remediation: Amend the policy/procedure to explicitly require management of electrical risks in accordance with WHS Part 3.1, including: hazard identification for electrical risks; risk assessment where required; implementation of controls using the hierarchy of controls (e.g., isolation, RCDs, lockout/tagout, competent persons, inspection/testing regimes); maintenance and testing requirements for electrical equipment/installations; procedures for high-risk areas (fire/explosion atmospheres, cathodic protection systems); roles/responsibilities (PCBU, supervisors, workers); training/competency requirements; and monitoring/review of control effectiveness. Cross-reference the “what is/is not electrical work” definitions as scope guidance, but do not rely on them as the primary compliance mechanism.

matches.target_title: 155 Duty to determine whether equipment is energised
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that, before electrical work is carried out on electrical equipment, the equipment is tested by a competent person to determine whether or not it is energised. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

Note 

Regulation 157 allows electrical testing to be carried out on electrical equipment for the purposes of this regulation. Regulation 161 sets out how the testing is to be carried out. 

(2) The person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that: 

(a) each exposed part is treated as energised until it is isolated and determined not to be energised; and   
(b) each high-voltage exposed part is earthed after being deenergised. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning: The provision imposes a duty on the PCBU to ensure that before electrical work is carried out on electrical equipment, the equipment is tested by a competent person to determine whether it is energised, and that exposed parts are treated as energised until isolated and proven de-energised (and high-voltage exposed parts earthed after de-energising). The policy text instead defines what is and is not 'electrical work' (particularly excluding most extra-low voltage equipment except in hazardous atmospheres or cathodic protection systems) and references an appendix listing activities considered electrical work. It does not state any requirement to test equipment before work, to treat exposed parts as energised until proven otherwise, or to earth high-voltage exposed parts after de-energising. Therefore, it does not fulfill the operational safety obligations in the provision for situations where electrical work is performed.
compliance.remediation: Amend the Electrical Safety Procedure/policy to explicitly require that, before any electrical work on electrical equipment, a competent person tests to determine whether the equipment is energised; require exposed parts to be treated as energised until isolated and confirmed de-energised; and require high-voltage exposed parts to be earthed after de-energising. Cross-reference the relevant WHS Regulations (including testing and method requirements) and ensure the requirements apply to all work that meets the procedure’s definition of 'electrical work' (including the listed exceptions).

matches.target_title: 148 Electrical equipment and electrical installations to which this Division applies
matches.target_text: In this Division, a reference to electrical equipment or an electrical installation in relation to a person conducting a business or undertaking is a reference to electrical equipment or an electrical installation that is under the person's management or control.

compliance.reasoning: The provision defines the scope of what counts as “electrical equipment” or an “electrical installation” for a PCBU by limiting it to equipment/installations under the person’s management or control. The policy defines what is and is not “electrical work” (including exceptions for extra-low voltage equipment) and references an internal appendix for activities considered electrical work. However, the policy does not incorporate or acknowledge the management/control limitation when referring to electrical equipment or installations (e.g., it does not clarify that the policy’s classifications apply to equipment/installations under CSIRO’s management or control). This creates a potential scope mismatch where the policy could be interpreted as applying to equipment/installations not under CSIRO control, or failing to clearly tie obligations to the legally relevant subset.
compliance.remediation: Amend the policy to explicitly state that references to electrical equipment and electrical installations (and the classification of activities as electrical work) apply to equipment/installations under CSIRO’s management or control (or otherwise specify how CSIRO treats shared/third-party controlled equipment). Update Appendix 1 and any related definitions to align with this scope statement.

source_text:
Electrical Safety Procedure Section 1 – HSE Risk Management

Prior to undertaking any electrical work or activities involving a possible electrical hazard that has the potential to cause injury or property/infrastructure damage an Activity Risk Assessment must be completed. A Safe Work Instruction (SWI) must be completed for recurring electrical work and energised electrical work.

Section 147 of the WHS Regulation specifies that an employer “must manage risks to health and safety associated with electrical risks at the workplace”. This includes electrical risks associated with the design, construction, installation, protection, maintenance and testing of electrical equipment and electrical installations at a workplace.

Section 2 of the Code of Practice 2018 – Managing electrical risks in the workplace provides the following guidance that may assist when completing an Activity Risk Assessment (ARA):

 different hazards associated with electrical equipment and installations;   
 considerations when assessing the nature and severity of risks associated with electrical hazards and electrical work; and   
 considerations when identifying and implementing control measures to eliminate or reduce the risks with associated with electrical hazards.

Refer to Section 7 of this guideline for information on risk assessment consideration for energised electrical work.

matches.target_title: 147 Risk management
matches.target_text:
A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must manage risks to health and safety associated with electrical risks at the workplace, in accordance with Part 3.1. 

Example 

Electrical risks associated with the design, construction, installation, protection, maintenance and testing of electrical equipment and electrical installations at a workplace. 

Note 

WHS Act—section 19 (see regulation 9).

compliance.reasoning: The POLICY text is functionally related to the PROVISION because it explicitly references the same WHS Regulation requirement (Section 147) that a duty holder must manage risks to health and safety associated with electrical risks at the workplace. The POLICY operationalizes this obligation by requiring an Activity Risk Assessment prior to electrical work involving potential electrical hazards and requiring Safe Work Instructions for recurring and energised electrical work. This aligns with the PROVISION’s requirement to manage electrical risks (including those arising from design, construction, installation, protection, maintenance and testing). While the POLICY does not explicitly cite 'Part 3.1', its mandated risk assessment and control-planning mechanisms are consistent with managing risks in accordance with general risk management duties.

matches.target_title: 158 Preliminary steps
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure the following before electrical work on energised electrical equipment commences: 

(a) a risk assessment is conducted in relation to the proposed electrical work;   
(b) the area where the electrical work is to be carried out is clear of obstructions so as to allow for easy access and exit;   
(c) the point at which the electrical equipment can be disconnected or isolated from its electricity supply is: (i) clearly marked or labelled; and (ii) clear of obstructions so as to allow for easy access and exit by the worker who is to carry out the electrical work or any other competent person; and (iii) capable of being operated quickly;   
(d) the person authorises the electrical work after consulting with the person with management or control of the workplace. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(a), the risk assessment must be: 

(a) conducted by a competent person; and (b) recorded. 

(3) Subregulation (1)(c) does not apply to electrical work on electrical equipment if: 

(a) the work is to be carried out on the supply side of the main switch on the main switchboard for the equipment; and   
(b) the point at which the equipment can be disconnected from its electricity supply is not reasonably accessible from the work location.

compliance.reasoning: The POLICY is functionally related to the PROVISION because it sets requirements for risk management prior to electrical work, including energised electrical work, which aligns with the PROVISION’s preconditions for commencing energised electrical work. However, the POLICY only requires an Activity Risk Assessment (and SWI for recurring/energised work) and provides general guidance references. It does not explicitly require that, before energised electrical work commences: (1) the work area is clear of obstructions for easy access/exit; (2) the isolation/disconnection point is clearly marked/labelled, unobstructed, and operable quickly; (3) the work is authorised after consultation with the person with management/control of the workplace; (4) the risk assessment is conducted by a competent person and recorded. Therefore, the POLICY does not fully cover the specific mandatory preconditions in the PROVISION.
compliance.remediation: Update the Electrical Safety Procedure/SWI requirements for energised electrical work to explicitly mandate, before work starts: (a) a risk assessment conducted by a competent person and recorded; (b) the work area is cleared of obstructions to allow easy access and exit; (c) the isolation/disconnection point is clearly marked/labelled, unobstructed, and capable of quick operation (and address the stated exception where applicable); (d) formal authorisation of the energised work after consultation with the person with management/control of the workplace, with documentation of the consultation/authorisation.

matches.target_title: 161 How the work is to be carried out
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical work on energised electrical equipment is carried out: 

(a) by a competent person who has tools, testing equipment and personal protective equipment that: 

(i) are suitable for the work; and (ii) have been properly tested; and (iii) are maintained in good working order; and 

(b) in accordance with a safe work method statement prepared for the work; and   
(c) subject to subregulation (5), with a safety observer present who has the competence and qualifications specified in subregulation (4). 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) The person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure, so far as is reasonably practicable, that the person who carries out the electrical work uses the tools, testing equipment and personal protective equipment properly. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(3) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(b), the safe work method statement must: 

(a) identify the electrical work; and   
(b) specify hazards associated with that electrical work and risks associated with those hazards; and   
(c) describe the measures to be implemented to control the risks; and   
(d) describe how the risk control measures are to be implemented, monitored and reviewed. 

(4) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(c): 

(a) the safety observer must be competent: 

(b) the safety observer must have been assessed in the previous 12 months as competent to rescue and resuscitate a person. 

(5) A safety observer is not required if: 

(a) the work consists only of testing; and   
(b) the person conducting the business or undertaking has conducted a risk assessment under regulation 158(1)(a) that shows that there is no serious risk associated with the proposed work.

compliance.reasoning: The policy addresses managing electrical risks via Activity Risk Assessments and requires a Safe Work Instruction (SWI) for recurring and energised electrical work, which is functionally related to the provision requiring specific controls for energised electrical work (competent person with suitable/tested/maintained tools/PPE, a safe work method statement (SWMS) with defined content, and a safety observer unless an exception applies). However, the policy does not explicitly require: (1) energised work to be carried out in accordance with a SWMS (it references SWI/ARA instead), (2) the mandatory SWMS content elements (identify work, hazards/risks, controls, implementation/monitoring/review), (3) a safety observer with specified competence and annual rescue/resuscitation assessment, (4) ensuring tools/testing equipment/PPE are suitable, properly tested, maintained, and used properly, or (5) the specific exception conditions for when a safety observer is not required (testing-only plus a risk assessment showing no serious risk). Therefore it does not fully meet the provision’s requirements for energised electrical work.
compliance.remediation: Update the Electrical Safety Procedure to explicitly require that energised electrical work is performed under a SWMS (or ensure the SWI is equivalent and explicitly designated as the SWMS) that includes all required elements (work identification; hazards/risks; control measures; implementation, monitoring and review). Add requirements that energised work is performed by a competent person using tools/testing equipment/PPE that are suitable, properly tested, and maintained, and that the PCBU ensures (so far as reasonably practicable) proper use of such equipment. Include a requirement for a safety observer for energised work, specifying competence and documented assessment within the previous 12 months for rescue and resuscitation, and document the limited exception (testing-only plus a risk assessment demonstrating no serious risk).

matches.target_title: 155 Duty to determine whether equipment is energised
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that, before electrical work is carried out on electrical equipment, the equipment is tested by a competent person to determine whether or not it is energised. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

Note 

Regulation 157 allows electrical testing to be carried out on electrical equipment for the purposes of this regulation. Regulation 161 sets out how the testing is to be carried out. 

(2) The person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that: 

(a) each exposed part is treated as energised until it is isolated and determined not to be energised; and   
(b) each high-voltage exposed part is earthed after being deenergised. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning: The provision imposes specific pre-work electrical safety obligations: (1) before electrical work is carried out, the equipment must be tested by a competent person to determine whether it is energised; and (2) exposed parts must be treated as energised until isolated and proven de-energised, and high-voltage exposed parts must be earthed after de-energisation. The policy text focuses on completing an Activity Risk Assessment and Safe Work Instructions for recurring and energised electrical work, and generally references managing electrical risks under WHS Regulation s147 and guidance from the Code of Practice. However, it does not explicitly require pre-work testing by a competent person to confirm de-energised status, does not state the 'treat as energised until isolated and proven de-energised' rule, and does not require earthing of high-voltage exposed parts after de-energisation. Therefore, the policy does not fully implement the specific controls mandated by the provision.
compliance.remediation: Amend the Electrical Safety Procedure/SWI requirements to explicitly mandate: (1) testing of electrical equipment by a competent person before electrical work to determine if it is energised (with reference to the applicable testing method requirements); (2) a rule that all exposed parts are treated as energised until isolated and verified de-energised; and (3) for high-voltage exposed parts, earthing after de-energisation.
