Ensure these steps are embedded as mandatory controls in ARAs/SWIs and supported by competency, documentation, and verification requirements.

matches.target_title: 157 Electrical work on energised electrical equipment—when permitted
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical work on energised electrical equipment is not carried out unless: 

(a) it is necessary in the interests of health and safety that the electrical work is carried out on the equipment while the equipment is energised; or Example 

It may be necessary that life-saving equipment remain energised and operating while electrical work is carried out on the equipment.   
(b) it is necessary that the electrical equipment to be worked on is energised in order for the work to be carried out properly; or   
(c) it is necessary for the purposes of testing required under regulation 155; or   
(d) there is no reasonable alternative means of carrying out the work. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) The electrical work that may be carried out under subregulation (1)(a), (b) and (d) may include testing of the energised electrical equipment.

compliance.reasoning: The provision requires that energised electrical work must not be carried out unless one of the listed necessity conditions applies (health and safety necessity, necessary for proper work, necessary for testing under reg 155, or no reasonable alternative). The policy text requires an Activity Risk Assessment for electrical hazards and an SWI for recurring and energised electrical work, and references general risk-management duties and guidance. However, it does not explicitly prohibit energised work by default, does not require verification/documentation that one of the specific necessity conditions is met before proceeding, and does not reference the specific exceptions framework (including testing under reg 155). Therefore, the policy does not fully implement the provision’s threshold requirement for when energised work is permitted.
compliance.remediation: Amend the procedure/SWI requirements to explicitly state that energised electrical work is prohibited unless one of the WHS Regulation exceptions applies, and require the ARA/SWI to document which exception is relied upon (including where applicable testing under reg 155) and why no reasonable alternative exists. Add an approval/authorisation step for energised work confirming the exception and controls.

matches.target_title: 150 Inspection and testing of electrical equipment
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must ensure that electrical equipment is regularly inspected and tested by a competent person if the electrical equipment is: 

(a) supplied with electricity through an electrical socket outlet; and   
(b) used in an environment in which the normal use of electrical equipment exposes the equipment to operating conditions that are likely to result in damage to the equipment or a reduction in its expected life span, including conditions that involve exposure to moisture, heat, vibration, mechanical damage, corrosive chemicals or dust. 

Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty. 

(2) In the case of electrical equipment that is new and unused at the workplace, the person conducting the business or undertaking: 

(a) is not required to comply with subregulation (1); and (b) must ensure that the equipment is inspected for obvious damage before being used. 

Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty. 

Note 

However, electrical equipment that is unsafe must not be used (see regulation 149). 

(3) The person must ensure that a record of any testing carried out under subregulation (1) is kept until the electrical equipment is: 

(a) next tested; or (b) permanently removed from the workplace or disposed of. 


Maximum penalty: tier I monetary penalty. 

(4) The record of testing: 

(a) must specify the following: (i) the name of the person who carried out the testing; (ii) the date of the testing; (iii) the outcome of the testing; (iv) the date on which the next testing must be carried out; and   
(b) may be in the form of a tag attached to the electrical equipment tested.

compliance.reasoning: The POLICY establishes a general requirement to manage electrical risks via Activity Risk Assessments and Safe Work Instructions, referencing WHS Regulation s147 (general duty to manage electrical risks). The PROVISION imposes a specific, prescriptive obligation to (1) regularly inspect and test certain socket-outlet supplied electrical equipment used in harsh/damaging environments, (2) inspect new/unused equipment for obvious damage before use, and (3) keep detailed testing records until next test or disposal. The POLICY text provided does not state any requirement for regular inspection/testing of such equipment, does not define inspection/testing intervals or competency requirements, and does not require keeping testing records with the specified minimum content. Therefore, the POLICY does not demonstrate coverage of the PROVISION’s specific inspection/testing and recordkeeping obligations.
compliance.remediation: Update the Electrical Safety Procedure (or add a linked procedure) to explicitly require: (1) regular inspection and testing by a competent person for socket-outlet supplied equipment used in environments likely to cause damage (moisture/heat/vibration/mechanical damage/corrosives/dust), including defined triggers/scope and a schedule or reference to an applicable testing regime; (2) pre-use inspection for obvious damage for new and unused equipment; (3) retention of testing records until next test or disposal/removal; and (4) record content requirements (tester name, test date, outcome, next test due date) and acceptable formats (e.g., tagging plus central register).

matches.target_title: 160 Contact with equipment being worked on
matches.target_text:
A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that, while electrical work is being carried out on energised electrical equipment, all persons are prevented from creating an electrical risk by inadvertently making contact with an exposed energised component of the equipment. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning: The provision imposes a specific duty during energised electrical work: the PCBU must ensure all persons are prevented from creating an electrical risk by inadvertently contacting an exposed energised component. The policy text is generally about completing an Activity Risk Assessment and Safe Work Instructions for recurring and energised electrical work, and references broader WHS duties and guidance. However, it does not explicitly require preventing inadvertent contact with exposed energised components (e.g., through barriers, insulation, exclusion zones, guarding, access control, or similar controls) while energised work is being performed. Therefore, the policy does not clearly implement the specific preventive requirement in the provision.
compliance.remediation: Amend the Electrical Safety Procedure/SWI requirements for energised electrical work to explicitly mandate controls that prevent inadvertent contact with exposed energised components (e.g., establish exclusion zones and access control, use insulating barriers/blankets/covers, guarding, signage, supervision/spotter where appropriate, and verification that exposed live parts are covered or isolated where practicable). Include clear responsibilities, minimum control standards, and verification steps within the ARA/SWI process.

matches.target_title: 162 Record keeping
matches.target_text:
(1) This regulation applies if a person conducting a business or undertaking prepares: 

(a) a risk assessment under regulation 158; or (b) a safe work method statement under regulation 161. 

(2) Subject to subregulation (3), the person must keep: 

(a) a copy of the risk assessment until at least 28 days after the work to which it relates is completed; and 

(b) a copy of the safe work method statement until the work to which it relates is completed. 

Maximum penalty: tier I monetary penalty. 

(3) If a notifiable incident occurs in connection with the work to which the assessment or statement relates, the person must keep the assessment or statement (as applicable) for at least 2 years after the incident occurs. Maximum penalty: tier I monetary penalty.   
(4) The person must ensure that, for the period for which the assessment or statement must be kept under this regulation, a copy is readily accessible to any worker engaged by the person to carry out electrical work to which the assessment or statement relates. Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty.   
(5) The person must ensure that, for the period for which the assessment or statement must be kept under this regulation, a copy is available for inspection under the Act. Maximum penalty: tier I monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning: The POLICY requires completing an Activity Risk Assessment (ARA) prior to electrical work and completing a Safe Work Instruction (SWI) for recurring and energised electrical work. The PROVISION imposes additional obligations when a risk assessment (under reg 158) or a safe work method statement (under reg 161) is prepared: retention periods (28 days post-completion for risk assessments; until completion for SWMS; 2 years after a notifiable incident), and accessibility/availability requirements (readily accessible to workers and available for inspection). The POLICY does not address document retention timeframes, extended retention after notifiable incidents, or requirements to keep copies readily accessible to workers and available for inspection. Therefore, while the POLICY covers preparation of risk assessments/instructions, it does not fulfill the PROVISION’s recordkeeping and access obligations.
compliance.remediation: Update the procedure to explicitly require: (1) keeping copies of electrical risk assessments for at least 28 days after the related work is completed; (2) keeping copies of SWMS (or the equivalent document used for high-risk electrical work) until the work is completed; (3) retaining the relevant assessment/statement for at least 2 years after any notifiable incident connected to the work; (4) ensuring copies are readily accessible to workers performing the electrical work during the retention period; and (5) ensuring copies are available for inspection under the Act. Also clarify whether SWI is intended to meet SWMS requirements under reg 161; if not, add a SWMS requirement where applicable.

matches.target_title: 166 Duty of person conducting a business or undertaking
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must ensure, so far as is reasonably practicable, that no person, plant or thing at the workplace comes within an unsafe distance of an overhead or underground electric line. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) If it is not reasonably practicable to ensure the safe distance of a person, plant or thing from an overhead or underground electric line, the person conducting the business or undertaking at the workplace must ensure that:
(a) a risk assessment is conducted in relation to the proposed work; and 

(b) control measures implemented are consistent with: 

(i) the risk assessment; and 

(ii) if an electricity supply authority is responsible for the electric line, any requirements of the authority. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

Notes 

1 [Relevant electricity safety legislation] will also apply to the person conducting the business or undertaking.

compliance.reasoning: The provision imposes a specific duty regarding work near overhead/underground electric lines: (1) ensure, so far as reasonably practicable, that no person/plant/thing comes within an unsafe distance; and (2) if maintaining safe distance is not reasonably practicable, conduct a risk assessment for the proposed work and implement controls consistent with that assessment and any electricity supply authority requirements. The policy text is generally about managing electrical risks via Activity Risk Assessments and Safe Work Instructions for recurring/energised electrical work, but it does not explicitly address electric line approach distances, the primary duty to maintain safe distance, the trigger condition when safe distance is not reasonably practicable, or the requirement to align controls with electricity supply authority requirements. Therefore, it does not fully cover the provision’s specific obligations.
compliance.remediation: Update the Electrical Safety Procedure to explicitly address work near overhead and underground electric lines by: (1) requiring identification of electric lines and establishment/maintenance of 'unsafe distance' exclusion zones (including defined approach distances or a requirement to obtain them from the relevant authority/standards); (2) stating that if safe distance cannot be maintained, a specific risk assessment for the proposed work must be conducted; (3) requiring implementation of controls consistent with the risk assessment; and (4) requiring compliance with any electricity supply authority requirements (e.g., consultation, permits, isolation/de-energisation, spotters, barriers). Ensure SWIs/ARAs include these elements where applicable.

matches.target_title: 149 Unsafe electrical equipment
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must ensure that any unsafe electrical equipment at the workplace: 

(a) is disconnected (or isolated) from its electricity supply; and (b) once disconnected (or isolated): 

(i) is not reconnected until it is repaired or tested and found to be safe; or (ii) is replaced or permanently removed from use. 

Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty. 

(2) For the purposes of this regulation, electrical equipment or a component of electrical equipment is unsafe if there are reasonable grounds for believing it to be unsafe.

compliance.reasoning: The provision imposes a specific operational duty: ensure any unsafe electrical equipment is disconnected/isolated and not reconnected until repaired/tested safe, or replaced/permanently removed. The policy text focuses on completing an Activity Risk Assessment and Safe Work Instructions for recurring/energised electrical work and generally references managing electrical risks, but it does not explicitly require identification of unsafe electrical equipment, mandatory disconnection/isolation, controls to prevent reconnection, or requirements for repair/testing/replacement/removal before reuse. Therefore, the policy does not demonstrably fulfill the provision’s specific obligations.
compliance.remediation: Amend the Electrical Safety Procedure to explicitly require that any electrical equipment reasonably believed to be unsafe is immediately disconnected/isolated from supply, tagged/secured to prevent reconnection, and only returned to service after repair and testing confirms it is safe, or else replaced or permanently removed from use. Define responsibilities, triggers/criteria for 'unsafe', and recordkeeping/verification steps.

source_text:
Electrical workers must be deemed competent to carry out electrical work.

Electrical work competencies are regulated by the states and territories, as a result your location will determine what types of competencies are required for the work you carry out.

Table 1 provides an overview of what licences are required in each state and territory for specific electrical work. If an electrical licence isn’t required, then competency must be demonstrated through the successful completion of a practical competency assessment as agreed by CSIRO.

Table 1 Electrical licence and competency assessment requirements by state/territory

matches.target_title: 146 Meaning of electrical work

compliance.reasoning: The provision defines what constitutes “electrical work” and explicitly lists exclusions (tasks that are not electrical work) and conditions for certain activities (e.g., supervised assistance, supervised conduit/duct work, eligibility work). The policy sets a blanket requirement that “electrical workers must be deemed competent to carry out electrical work” and ties competency to licensing or a practical competency assessment, but it does not align its scope to the provision’s definition/exclusions. Without incorporating the exclusions and supervision-based carve-outs, the policy may incorrectly require licensing/competency assessment for activities that the provision states are not “electrical work,” and it does not explicitly address the supervision conditions required for assistants/trainees and certain construction tasks.
compliance.remediation: Update the policy to (1) adopt or reference the provision’s definition of “electrical work,” (2) explicitly list or reference the exclusions (e.g., plug-in connection, non-electrical component work without electrical risk, replacing fuses/light bulbs, manufacturing-process assembly, etc.), and (3) specify supervision requirements for assistants/trainees and for duct/conduit/trough work as described in the provision. Ensure Table 1 and competency assessment triggers apply only to activities that meet the defined scope of “electrical work.”

matches.target_title: 161 How the work is to be carried out
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical work on energised electrical equipment is carried out: 

(a) by a competent person who has tools, testing equipment and personal protective equipment that: 

(i) are suitable for the work; and (ii) have been properly tested; and (iii) are maintained in good working order; and 

(b) in accordance with a safe work method statement prepared for the work; and   
(c) subject to subregulation (5), with a safety observer present who has the competence and qualifications specified in subregulation (4). 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) The person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure, so far as is reasonably practicable, that the person who carries out the electrical work uses the tools, testing equipment and personal protective equipment properly. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(3) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(b), the safe work method statement must: 

(a) identify the electrical work; and   
(b) specify hazards associated with that electrical work and risks associated with those hazards; and   
(c) describe the measures to be implemented to control the risks; and   
(d) describe how the risk control measures are to be implemented, monitored and reviewed. 

(4) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(c): 

(a) the safety observer must be competent: 

(b) the safety observer must have been assessed in the previous 12 months as competent to rescue and resuscitate a person. 

(5) A safety observer is not required if: 

(a) the work consists only of testing; and   
(b) the person conducting the business or undertaking has conducted a risk assessment under regulation 158(1)(a) that shows that there is no serious risk associated with the proposed work.

matches.target_title: 158 Preliminary steps
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure the following before electrical work on energised electrical equipment commences: 

(a) a risk assessment is conducted in relation to the proposed electrical work;   
(b) the area where the electrical work is to be carried out is clear of obstructions so as to allow for easy access and exit;   
(c) the point at which the electrical equipment can be disconnected or isolated from its electricity supply is: (i) clearly marked or labelled; and (ii) clear of obstructions so as to allow for easy access and exit by the worker who is to carry out the electrical work or any other competent person; and (iii) capable of being operated quickly;   
(d) the person authorises the electrical work after consulting with the person with management or control of the workplace. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(a), the risk assessment must be: 

(a) conducted by a competent person; and (b) recorded. 

(3) Subregulation (1)(c) does not apply to electrical work on electrical equipment if: 

(a) the work is to be carried out on the supply side of the main switch on the main switchboard for the equipment; and   
(b) the point at which the equipment can be disconnected from its electricity supply is not reasonably accessible from the work location.

matches.target_title: 95 Reassessment of competency of licence holder
matches.target_text:
The regulator may direct a licence holder to obtain a reassessment of the competency of the licence holder to carry out the high risk work covered by the licence if the regulator reasonably believes that the licence holder may not be competent to carry out that work. 

Examples 

1 The training or competency assessment of the licence holder did not meet the standard required to hold the licence.   
2 The regulator receives information that the licence holder has carried out high risk work incompetently. 

Subdivision 3 Amendment of licence document

(1) A person must not carry out any type of general diving work unless the person holds a certificate for general diving work, issued by a training organisation, that demonstrates that the person has acquired the relevant competencies for that type of general diving work.   
(2) This regulation does not apply in relation to incidental diving work or limited scientific diving work.   
(3) In subregulation (1), relevant competencies means the competencies specified in AS/NZS 2815 (Training and certification of occupational divers) that are relevant to the type of general diving work to which subregulation (1) applies.

(a) by a competent person; and (b) in accordance with:

(i) the recommendations of the designer or manufacturer or designer and manufacturer; or

(ii) if a maintenance manual for the device or ropeway has been prepared by a competent person, the requirements of the maintenance manual.

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

source_title: 4.2 Electrical work – who can do what?
source_text:
Electrical work, whether energised or de-energised, must only be carried out by a person who is deemed competent under applicable state/territory electrical legislation.

An electrical worker’s training and qualifications will impact the type of electrical work they can perform. Table 2 outlines what electrical work electrical workers in certain positions should be able to perform based on their qualifications and training.

compliance.reasoning: Error after 3 attempts: Connection error.

matches.target_title: 154 Electrical work on energised electrical equipment—prohibited
matches.target_text:
Subject to this Division, a person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical work is not carried out on electrical equipment while the equipment is energised. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning: Error after 3 attempts: Connection error.

matches.target_title: 160 Contact with equipment being worked on
matches.target_text:
A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that, while electrical work is being carried out on energised electrical equipment, all persons are prevented from creating an electrical risk by inadvertently making contact with an exposed energised component of the equipment. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning: The provision imposes a specific duty during energised electrical work: the PCBU must ensure all persons are prevented from creating an electrical risk by inadvertently contacting exposed energised components. The policy addresses only who may perform electrical work (competency/qualifications) and does not include controls or requirements to prevent inadvertent contact with exposed energised components while energised work is occurring. Therefore, the policy does not satisfy the provision’s risk-prevention obligation.
compliance.remediation: Amend the policy to include explicit requirements for energised electrical work to prevent inadvertent contact with exposed energised components (e.g., mandatory insulation/barriers/guards, exclusion zones and access control, lockable covers, use of insulating mats/tools/PPE as applicable, supervision/spotter where required, and procedures such as permits/SWMS and verification that exposed live parts are covered or isolated where practicable). Define responsibilities (PCBU, supervisor, workers) and verification/audit steps to ensure these controls are implemented during energised work.

matches.target_title: 152 Application of Division 4
matches.target_text:
This Division does not apply to work carried out by or on behalf of an electricity supply authority on the electrical equipment, including electric line-associated equipment, controlled or operated by the authority to transform, transmit or supply electricity. 

Note 

See the jurisdictional note in the Appendix.

compliance.reasoning: Both texts concern who may perform electrical work under legislation. However, the PROVISION creates a jurisdictional exemption for work done by/for an electricity supply authority on its controlled equipment, meaning the POLICY’s blanket requirement (only competent persons under applicable legislation) may not apply in those exempt circumstances. Without the full scope of the referenced “Division” and the applicable jurisdictional note, it cannot be determined whether the POLICY conflicts with, over-applies beyond, or appropriately defers to the exemption.
compliance.remediation: Clarify in the POLICY scope that requirements apply except where specific legislative exemptions apply (e.g., work by/on behalf of an electricity supply authority on authority-controlled supply equipment), and reference the relevant jurisdictional notes/appendix. Confirm alignment with each state/territory legislation and explicitly document how the exemption is handled in procedures and competency matrices (e.g., Table 2).

matches.target_title: 150 Inspection and testing of electrical equipment
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must ensure that electrical equipment is regularly inspected and tested by a competent person if the electrical equipment is: 

(a) supplied with electricity through an electrical socket outlet; and   
(b) used in an environment in which the normal use of electrical equipment exposes the equipment to operating conditions that are likely to result in damage to the equipment or a reduction in its expected life span, including conditions that involve exposure to moisture, heat, vibration, mechanical damage, corrosive chemicals or dust. 

Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty. 

(2) In the case of electrical equipment that is new and unused at the workplace, the person conducting the business or undertaking: 

(a) is not required to comply with subregulation (1); and (b) must ensure that the equipment is inspected for obvious damage before being used. 

Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty. 

Note 

However, electrical equipment that is unsafe must not be used (see regulation 149). 

(3) The person must ensure that a record of any testing carried out under subregulation (1) is kept until the electrical equipment is: 

(a) next tested; or (b) permanently removed from the workplace or disposed of. 


Maximum penalty: tier I monetary penalty. 

(4) The record of testing: 

(a) must specify the following: (i) the name of the person who carried out the testing; (ii) the date of the testing; (iii) the outcome of the testing; (iv) the date on which the next testing must be carried out; and   
(b) may be in the form of a tag attached to the electrical equipment tested.

compliance.reasoning: The POLICY requires that electrical work (energised or de-energised) is only carried out by a person deemed competent under applicable electrical legislation. The PROVISION imposes a duty on the PCBU to ensure electrical equipment is regularly inspected and tested by a competent person in specified environments, and to keep specified testing records. Inspection and testing of electrical equipment is a form of electrical work/activity requiring competence. While the POLICY addresses competence generally, it does not include the PROVISION’s specific obligations to (1) ensure regular inspection/testing for socket-outlet supplied equipment used in harsh/damaging environments, (2) inspect new/unused equipment for obvious damage before use, and (3) keep testing records with required details until next test/removal. Therefore, the POLICY does not fully satisfy the PROVISION’s requirements.
compliance.remediation: Update the policy/procedure to explicitly require: (1) a risk-based program for regular inspection and testing of socket-outlet supplied equipment used in environments likely to cause damage (moisture/heat/vibration/mechanical damage/corrosives/dust), performed by a competent person; (2) pre-use inspection for obvious damage for new/unused equipment; (3) retention of testing records until the next test or until the equipment is removed/disposed; and (4) records/tags to include tester name, test date, outcome, and next test due date.

matches.target_title: 147 Risk management
matches.target_text:
A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must manage risks to health and safety associated with electrical risks at the workplace, in accordance with Part 3.1. 

Example 

Electrical risks associated with the design, construction, installation, protection, maintenance and testing of electrical equipment and electrical installations at a workplace. 

Note 

WHS Act—section 19 (see regulation 9).

compliance.reasoning: The provision requires the PCBU to manage risks to health and safety associated with electrical risks at the workplace (a broad risk management duty). The policy text focuses narrowly on ensuring electrical work is performed only by competent persons under applicable electrical legislation and references training/qualification-based task allocation. While competency controls can form part of risk management, the policy does not state that the business will identify electrical hazards, assess risks, implement and maintain controls, or review controls in accordance with Part 3.1. It also does not cover the broader scope of electrical risks referenced in the provision (e.g., design, construction, installation, protection, maintenance, testing) beyond who may perform work. Therefore, the policy only partially addresses the obligation and is not sufficient to demonstrate compliance with the provision.
compliance.remediation: Amend the policy to explicitly require electrical risk management in accordance with WHS Part 3.1, including: hazard identification for electrical risks (including equipment/installations across design, installation, maintenance, testing); risk assessment where required; implementation of controls using the hierarchy of control (e.g., elimination, isolation, RCDs, lockout/tagout, de-energisation, permits, PPE); procedures for inspection, maintenance and testing; consultation, training and supervision; and periodic review/audit of controls and incident response.

source_text:
Electrical workers must complete a practical electrical competency assessment prior to carrying out electrical work if an appropriate electrical licence is not required by their local state/territory regulator. Table 1 Electrical licence and competency assessment requirements by state/territory identifies when an electrical competency assessment is required.

The electrical competency assessment evaluates whether an electrical worker has the necessary skills to carry out electrical work on non-fixed electrical equipment and can demonstrate safe work practices in line with the CSIRO Electrical Safety Procedure.

An electrical worker, if applicable, must successfully pass an electrical competency assessment as agreed by CSIRO within the past 5 years.

This assessment does not permit electrical workers to carry out any type of electrical work that requires an appropriate electrical licence within their local state or territory.

Refer to Appendix 2 of this guideline for the electrical competency assessment template that must be used.

matches.target_title: 161 How the work is to be carried out
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical work on energised electrical equipment is carried out: 

(a) by a competent person who has tools, testing equipment and personal protective equipment that: 

(i) are suitable for the work; and (ii) have been properly tested; and (iii) are maintained in good working order; and 

(b) in accordance with a safe work method statement prepared for the work; and   
(c) subject to subregulation (5), with a safety observer present who has the competence and qualifications specified in subregulation (4). 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) The person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure, so far as is reasonably practicable, that the person who carries out the electrical work uses the tools, testing equipment and personal protective equipment properly. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(3) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(b), the safe work method statement must: 

(a) identify the electrical work; and   
(b) specify hazards associated with that electrical work and risks associated with those hazards; and   
(c) describe the measures to be implemented to control the risks; and   
(d) describe how the risk control measures are to be implemented, monitored and reviewed. 

(4) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(c): 

(a) the safety observer must be competent: 

(b) the safety observer must have been assessed in the previous 12 months as competent to rescue and resuscitate a person. 

(5) A safety observer is not required if: 

(a) the work consists only of testing; and   
(b) the person conducting the business or undertaking has conducted a risk assessment under regulation 158(1)(a) that shows that there is no serious risk associated with the proposed work.

compliance.reasoning: The provision regulates how energised electrical work must be performed (competent person with suitable/tested/maintained tools and PPE; work done under a safe work method statement; safety observer present unless a testing-only exception applies with a supporting risk assessment; and safety observer rescue/resuscitation competency assessed within the previous 12 months). The policy addresses a practical electrical competency assessment for certain electrical workers (primarily for non-fixed equipment and where a licence is not required) and sets a 5-year recency requirement. It does not require or describe: (1) controls specific to energised electrical work, (2) ensuring suitable/tested/maintained tools, testing equipment and PPE, (3) preparation/content of a safe work method statement for energised work, (4) use of a safety observer and the observer’s required competencies, or (5) the 12-month rescue/resuscitation assessment requirement. Therefore, the policy does not fully implement the obligations in the provision and is not compliant as a control for this regulatory requirement.
compliance.remediation: Update the policy/procedure for energised electrical work to explicitly require: (1) energised work only by a competent person using tools/testing equipment/PPE that are suitable, properly tested, and maintained; (2) a safe work method statement meeting the provision’s content requirements; (3) a safety observer for energised work unless the testing-only exception applies, supported by a documented risk assessment; and (4) safety observer competence including rescue/resuscitation competency assessed within the previous 12 months. Align competency/assessment intervals to include the 12-month requirement for safety observers and define governance/recordkeeping to demonstrate compliance.

matches.target_title: 158 Preliminary steps
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure the following before electrical work on energised electrical equipment commences: 

(a) a risk assessment is conducted in relation to the proposed electrical work;   
(b) the area where the electrical work is to be carried out is clear of obstructions so as to allow for easy access and exit;   
(c) the point at which the electrical equipment can be disconnected or isolated from its electricity supply is: (i) clearly marked or labelled; and (ii) clear of obstructions so as to allow for easy access and exit by the worker who is to carry out the electrical work or any other competent person; and (iii) capable of being operated quickly;   
(d) the person authorises the electrical work after consulting with the person with management or control of the workplace. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(a), the risk assessment must be: 

(a) conducted by a competent person; and (b) recorded. 

(3) Subregulation (1)(c) does not apply to electrical work on electrical equipment if: 

(a) the work is to be carried out on the supply side of the main switch on the main switchboard for the equipment; and   
(b) the point at which the equipment can be disconnected from its electricity supply is not reasonably accessible from the work location.

matches.target_title: 146 Meaning of electrical work

compliance.reasoning: The provision defines what constitutes “electrical work” and lists exclusions (tasks that are not electrical work). The policy governs when CSIRO electrical workers must complete a practical electrical competency assessment before carrying out electrical work in jurisdictions where an electrical licence is not required, and it explicitly states the assessment does not authorise work that requires a licence. This is functionally related because the policy is an internal control intended to manage competence/safety for electrical work activities that may fall within the provision’s definition. The policy does not contradict the provision’s scope or exclusions and is stricter than the minimum legal definition by requiring an assessment in certain cases; it also preserves licensing requirements by stating it cannot substitute for a licence. Therefore, it is compliant with the provision.

matches.target_title: 155 Duty to determine whether equipment is energised
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that, before electrical work is carried out on electrical equipment, the equipment is tested by a competent person to determine whether or not it is energised. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

Note 

Regulation 157 allows electrical testing to be carried out on electrical equipment for the purposes of this regulation. Regulation 161 sets out how the testing is to be carried out. 

(2) The person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that: 

(a) each exposed part is treated as energised until it is isolated and determined not to be energised; and   
(b) each high-voltage exposed part is earthed after being deenergised. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning: The provision requires the PCBU to ensure that, before electrical work is carried out on electrical equipment, the equipment is tested by a competent person to determine whether it is energised, and that exposed parts are treated as energised until isolated and proven de-energised (and high-voltage exposed parts earthed after de-energised). The policy instead focuses on worker competency assessments and licensing/assessment requirements (including a 5-year recency requirement) for work on non-fixed electrical equipment. It does not state a requirement to test equipment for energisation before work, does not require treating exposed parts as energised until isolation and verification, and does not address earthing of high-voltage exposed parts after de-energisation. Therefore, the policy does not fulfill the specific procedural safety obligations in the provision.
compliance.remediation: Amend the policy/guideline to explicitly require that before any electrical work on electrical equipment, a competent person tests the equipment to confirm whether it is energised (in accordance with the applicable testing regulations/procedures). Add requirements that exposed parts must be treated as energised until isolated and verified de-energised, and that high-voltage exposed parts must be earthed after de-energisation. Define/align 'competent person' criteria and reference the required testing method/procedure.

matches.target_title: 150 Inspection and testing of electrical equipment
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must ensure that electrical equipment is regularly inspected and tested by a competent person if the electrical equipment is: 

(a) supplied with electricity through an electrical socket outlet; and   
(b) used in an environment in which the normal use of electrical equipment exposes the equipment to operating conditions that are likely to result in damage to the equipment or a reduction in its expected life span, including conditions that involve exposure to moisture, heat, vibration, mechanical damage, corrosive chemicals or dust. 

Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty. 

(2) In the case of electrical equipment that is new and unused at the workplace, the person conducting the business or undertaking: 

(a) is not required to comply with subregulation (1); and (b) must ensure that the equipment is inspected for obvious damage before being used. 

Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty. 

Note 

However, electrical equipment that is unsafe must not be used (see regulation 149). 

(3) The person must ensure that a record of any testing carried out under subregulation (1) is kept until the electrical equipment is: 

(a) next tested; or (b) permanently removed from the workplace or disposed of. 


Maximum penalty: tier I monetary penalty. 

(4) The record of testing: 

(a) must specify the following: (i) the name of the person who carried out the testing; (ii) the date of the testing; (iii) the outcome of the testing; (iv) the date on which the next testing must be carried out; and   
(b) may be in the form of a tag attached to the electrical equipment tested.

matches.target_title: 95 Reassessment of competency of licence holder
matches.target_text:
The regulator may direct a licence holder to obtain a reassessment of the competency of the licence holder to carry out the high risk work covered by the licence if the regulator reasonably believes that the licence holder may not be competent to carry out that work. 

Examples 

1 The training or competency assessment of the licence holder did not meet the standard required to hold the licence.   
2 The regulator receives information that the licence holder has carried out high risk work incompetently. 

Subdivision 3 Amendment of licence document

matches.target_title: 114 Accredited assessor must act in accordance with accreditation
matches.target_text:
(1) An accredited assessor must not conduct a competency assessment unless: 

(a) the competency assessment relates to a class of high risk work for which the assessor is accredited; and   
(b) the accredited assessor conducts the competency assessment for or on behalf of an RTO. 

(2) An accredited assessor must not issue a notice of satisfactory assessment unless the competency assessment relates to a class of high risk work for which the assessor is accredited. 

(3) An accredited assessor who conducts a competency assessment must do so in accordance with the conditions of accreditation imposed under regulation 121. 

(4) An accredited assessor who issues a notice of satisfactory assessment must do so in accordance with any conditions of accreditation imposed under regulation 121. 

(5) Subregulations (1) to (4) do not apply if the regulator is the accredited assessor. 

Note 

See section 43 of the Act.

matches.target_title: 157 Electrical work on energised electrical equipment—when permitted
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical work on energised electrical equipment is not carried out unless: 

(a) it is necessary in the interests of health and safety that the electrical work is carried out on the equipment while the equipment is energised; or Example 

It may be necessary that life-saving equipment remain energised and operating while electrical work is carried out on the equipment.   
(b) it is necessary that the electrical equipment to be worked on is energised in order for the work to be carried out properly; or   
(c) it is necessary for the purposes of testing required under regulation 155; or   
(d) there is no reasonable alternative means of carrying out the work. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) The electrical work that may be carried out under subregulation (1)(a), (b) and (d) may include testing of the energised electrical equipment.

matches.target_title: 240 Maintenance, inspection and testing of amusement devices and passenger ropeways
matches.target_text:
(1) The person with management or control of an amusement device or passenger ropeway at a workplace must ensure that the maintenance, inspection and, if necessary, testing of the device or ropeway is carried out:

(a) by a competent person; and (b) in accordance with:

(i) the recommendations of the designer or manufacturer or designer and manufacturer; or

(ii) if a maintenance manual for the device or ropeway has been prepared by a competent person, the requirements of the maintenance manual.

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

(2) A person is not a competent person to carry out a detailed inspection of an amusement device or passenger ropeway that includes an electrical installation unless the person is qualified, or is assisted by a person who is qualified, to inspect electrical installations.

compliance.reasoning: The provision requires that maintenance/inspection/testing of amusement devices or passenger ropeways be performed by a competent person and, where a detailed inspection includes an electrical installation, the inspector must be qualified (or assisted by someone qualified) to inspect electrical installations. The policy addresses electrical workers performing electrical work on non-fixed electrical equipment where a local electrical licence is not required, using a CSIRO practical competency assessment (valid within 5 years). It explicitly states the assessment does not permit work requiring an electrical licence. The policy does not ensure that inspections of amusement devices/ropeways are conducted in accordance with manufacturer/designer recommendations or a maintenance manual, nor does it require that persons conducting detailed inspections involving electrical installations are qualified to inspect electrical installations (or assisted by a qualified person). Therefore, the policy does not fully meet the provision’s competency/qualification requirements for this specific context.
compliance.remediation: Update the policy (or add a specific procedure for amusement devices/passenger ropeways) to: (1) require maintenance/inspection/testing to follow designer/manufacturer recommendations or an approved maintenance manual; (2) define 'competent person' for this work; and (3) mandate that any detailed inspection involving an electrical installation is performed by a person qualified to inspect electrical installations, or under the assistance/supervision of such a qualified person, rather than relying solely on the CSIRO competency assessment.

matches.target_title: 147 Risk management
matches.target_text:
A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must manage risks to health and safety associated with electrical risks at the workplace, in accordance with Part 3.1. 

Example 

Electrical risks associated with the design, construction, installation, protection, maintenance and testing of electrical equipment and electrical installations at a workplace. 

Note 

WHS Act—section 19 (see regulation 9).

compliance.reasoning: The provision imposes a general duty on the PCBU to manage health and safety risks associated with electrical risks at the workplace. The policy establishes a control measure (practical electrical competency assessment within the past 5 years for workers performing certain electrical work where a licence is not required) aimed at ensuring workers have the skills and safe work practices to manage electrical risks. This is functionally related to, and supports, the risk-management obligation. While the provision references managing risks in accordance with Part 3.1 (a broader risk management framework), the policy’s competency assessment requirement is consistent with implementing controls to manage electrical risks and does not conflict with the legal duty.

source_text:
The verification of electrical competency must establish whether the electrical worker:

1. Has the licences, qualifications, training and experience necessary to safely carry out the required electrical work; and   
2. Can demonstrate an understanding of the specific risks and control measures needed to carry out specific electrical work tasks.

What to consider when completing a verification form?

Prior to carrying out electrical work in CSIRO, an electrical worker must complete and submit a verification of electrical competency form to their line manager for consideration and approval.

When completing the form consider the following:

a. How do you plan on carrying out the electrical work – i.e. is it de-energised or energised? If energised, do you have an appropriate electrical licence? An application to undertake energised electrical work need to be completed to obtain permission to work energised.   
b. Does your training/qualifications/licences/experience address the competencies required for the electrical tasks you intend to carry out? – copies of qualifications/training/licences need to be attached to the verification form.   
c. Have you completed Activity Risk Assessments and SWIs, if applicable, for the electrical work you intend to carry out – copies of the risk assessment and SWIs need to be attached to the verification form.

What to consider when reviewing and approving a verification form?

The Line Manager along with the Electrical Safety Officer (ESO) should review the details in Part 2 & 3 of a submitted Verification of electrical competency.

When completing Part 4 & 5, discuss the electrical tasks to be performed and consider the following:

a. Does the licence/qualification/training/experience address the work described in Part 2? i.e. Do they require an electrical licence, additional training, equipment/tools, supervision?   
b. Any of the electrical work tasks need to be performed energised – an application to undertake energised electrical work needs to be completed and submitted by the electrical worker/competent person to obtain permission to work energised.   
c. Was there a previous electrical approval – is this a renewal or has there been a change in the electrical tasks?   
d. Has the worker provided copies of licences, certificates, qualification – copies must be provided. If the worker is unable to provide copies, approval cannot be given, and electrical work must be ceased until all supporting documentation provided.   
e. Are the licences, certificates, qualifications still in date and valid e.g. some licences have a review/renewal date. If out of date, the worker should be notified, and it noted on the verification form.   
f. Have all known risks and hazards been assessed for the work to be undertaken, has it been documented and have the control measures been implemented - copies of Activity Risk Assessments and Safe Work Instructions must be provided for the electrical tasks. If the worker is unable to provide copies, approval cannot be given, and electrical work must cease until the completed risk assessments and SWI, if applicable, have been provided.   
g. Are there further requirements for the worker to be approved - where it has been identified that additional training, supervision, procedures, etc are required, it is to be noted on the workers verification form and actioned appropriately.

Setting conditions and limitations to electrical work

Upon reviewing and approving verification form, a Line Manager may want to set conditions or limitation to what electrical work an electrical worker can carry out based on the risk and competency of the work.

Examples of conditions and limitations include:

 Electrical work is restricted to testing only, (no working on energised circuits).   
 Working on energised circuits strictly in accordance with the conditions of an electrical licence.  Working strictly under supervision by an electrically competent and qualified electrical worker. Isolate, tag, de- energise using appropriate PPE and work on de-energised equipment or installation.   
Restricted to electrical measurements on live external terminals, with appropriate PPE. Replacement of components in accordance with engineering specifications, like-for-like on deenergised circuits.

matches.target_title: 161 How the work is to be carried out
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical work on energised electrical equipment is carried out: 

(a) by a competent person who has tools, testing equipment and personal protective equipment that: 

(i) are suitable for the work; and (ii) have been properly tested; and (iii) are maintained in good working order; and 

(b) in accordance with a safe work method statement prepared for the work; and   
(c) subject to subregulation (5), with a safety observer present who has the competence and qualifications specified in subregulation (4). 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) The person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure, so far as is reasonably practicable, that the person who carries out the electrical work uses the tools, testing equipment and personal protective equipment properly. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(3) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(b), the safe work method statement must: 

(a) identify the electrical work; and   
(b) specify hazards associated with that electrical work and risks associated with those hazards; and   
(c) describe the measures to be implemented to control the risks; and   
(d) describe how the risk control measures are to be implemented, monitored and reviewed. 

(4) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(c): 

(a) the safety observer must be competent: 

(b) the safety observer must have been assessed in the previous 12 months as competent to rescue and resuscitate a person. 

(5) A safety observer is not required if: 

(a) the work consists only of testing; and   
(b) the person conducting the business or undertaking has conducted a risk assessment under regulation 158(1)(a) that shows that there is no serious risk associated with the proposed work.

compliance.reasoning: The POLICY is functionally related to the PROVISION because it establishes an internal verification/approval process for electrical competency and risk controls, including for energised electrical work. However, it does not fully meet the specific regulatory requirements for energised electrical work. The PROVISION requires that energised electrical work is carried out (a) by a competent person with suitable, properly tested and maintained tools/testing equipment/PPE; (b) in accordance with a safe work method statement (SWMS) that includes specific content (identify work, hazards/risks, controls, and how controls are implemented/monitored/reviewed); and (c) with a qualified safety observer who has been assessed within the previous 12 months as competent to rescue and resuscitate, unless the testing-only + risk assessment no serious risk exception applies. The POLICY addresses competency verification (licences/qualifications/training/experience) and requires risk assessments and SWIs, and mentions restrictions such as testing-only and PPE, but it does not explicitly require: (1) a SWMS for energised work (and the mandated SWMS content); (2) a safety observer for energised work and the observer’s specific competence/12-month rescue and resuscitation assessment; (3) assurance that tools/testing equipment/PPE are properly tested and maintained; or (4) the specific testing-only exception conditions (including a risk assessment demonstrating no serious risk) as the basis for omitting a safety observer.
compliance.remediation: Update the POLICY/procedure for energised electrical work to explicitly require: (1) a SWMS for energised electrical work that includes all elements in subregulation (3) (identify work; hazards/risks; controls; implementation/monitoring/review); (2) a safety observer for energised work, including defined qualifications/competence and documented evidence of rescue and resuscitation competency assessment within the last 12 months, and define when the observer is not required consistent with subregulation (5) (testing-only plus risk assessment showing no serious risk); (3) verification that tools, testing equipment and PPE are suitable, properly tested, and maintained in good working order, and that workers use them properly (include checks/records); and (4) incorporate these checks into the verification/approval workflow (forms, attachments, and approval criteria) and ensure cessation of energised work if these prerequisites are not met.

matches.target_title: 158 Preliminary steps
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure the following before electrical work on energised electrical equipment commences: 

(a) a risk assessment is conducted in relation to the proposed electrical work;   
(b) the area where the electrical work is to be carried out is clear of obstructions so as to allow for easy access and exit;   
(c) the point at which the electrical equipment can be disconnected or isolated from its electricity supply is: (i) clearly marked or labelled; and (ii) clear of obstructions so as to allow for easy access and exit by the worker who is to carry out the electrical work or any other competent person; and (iii) capable of being operated quickly;   
(d) the person authorises the electrical work after consulting with the person with management or control of the workplace. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(a), the risk assessment must be: 

(a) conducted by a competent person; and (b) recorded. 

(3) Subregulation (1)(c) does not apply to electrical work on electrical equipment if: 

(a) the work is to be carried out on the supply side of the main switch on the main switchboard for the equipment; and   
(b) the point at which the equipment can be disconnected from its electricity supply is not reasonably accessible from the work location.

compliance.reasoning: The POLICY is functionally related because it governs verification/approval of electrical competency and includes requirements around risk assessments, SWIs, and permission for energised work—topics directly connected to the PROVISION’s prerequisites for commencing energised electrical work. However, the POLICY does not fully ensure all mandatory preconditions in the PROVISION are met before energised work commences. Specifically: (1) While the POLICY requires Activity Risk Assessments/SWIs to be completed and provided, it does not explicitly require that the risk assessment for energised work be conducted by a competent person and recorded (PROVISION 2(a)-(b)); it implies documentation but not the competent-person requirement. (2) The POLICY does not address the PROVISION’s site/area readiness requirements: clearing obstructions for access/egress at the work area (1)(b), and ensuring the isolation/disconnection point is clearly marked/labelled, unobstructed, and operable quickly (1)(c)(i)-(iii). (3) The POLICY references an application/permission process for energised work and line manager/ESO review, but it does not explicitly require authorisation after consulting with the person with management or control of the workplace as stated in (1)(d). Therefore, the POLICY is only partially aligned and is not sufficient on its face to ensure compliance with all elements of the PROVISION.
compliance.remediation: Amend the POLICY/procedures for energised electrical work to explicitly require, before commencement: (1) a risk assessment conducted by a competent person and recorded (and retained with the verification/application); (2) verification that the work area is clear of obstructions to allow easy access and exit; (3) verification that the isolation/disconnection point is clearly labelled/marked, unobstructed, and capable of quick operation (and include the exception conditions where applicable); and (4) explicit authorisation of the energised work after consultation with the person with management or control of the workplace, with that consultation/authorisation documented.

matches.target_title: 155 Duty to determine whether equipment is energised
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that, before electrical work is carried out on electrical equipment, the equipment is tested by a competent person to determine whether or not it is energised. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

Note 

Regulation 157 allows electrical testing to be carried out on electrical equipment for the purposes of this regulation. Regulation 161 sets out how the testing is to be carried out. 

(2) The person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that: 

(a) each exposed part is treated as energised until it is isolated and determined not to be energised; and   
(b) each high-voltage exposed part is earthed after being deenergised. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning: The provision imposes operational safety obligations immediately before electrical work: (1) electrical equipment must be tested by a competent person to determine whether it is energised; and (2) exposed parts must be treated as energised until isolated and proven de-energised, and high-voltage exposed parts must be earthed after de-energisation. The policy focuses on verifying worker competency (licences/training/experience), risk assessments/SWIs, and approvals/conditions for energised vs de-energised work. While it mentions isolating/tagging/de-energising and working de-energised, it does not explicitly require pre-work testing of equipment by a competent person to confirm energisation status, does not state the rule to treat exposed parts as energised until isolation and confirmation, and does not address the specific requirement to earth high-voltage exposed parts after de-energisation. Therefore, the policy does not fully cover the provision’s mandatory procedural controls.
compliance.remediation: Update the policy and associated SWI/verification requirements to explicitly mandate: (1) before any electrical work, the equipment must be tested by a competent person to determine whether it is energised (with reference to the applicable testing procedure); (2) all exposed parts are treated as energised until isolated and confirmed de-energised; and (3) high-voltage exposed parts are earthed after being de-energised. Require these steps to be documented in Activity Risk Assessments/SWIs and verified during line manager/ESO approval.

matches.target_title: 150 Inspection and testing of electrical equipment
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must ensure that electrical equipment is regularly inspected and tested by a competent person if the electrical equipment is: 

(a) supplied with electricity through an electrical socket outlet; and   
(b) used in an environment in which the normal use of electrical equipment exposes the equipment to operating conditions that are likely to result in damage to the equipment or a reduction in its expected life span, including conditions that involve exposure to moisture, heat, vibration, mechanical damage, corrosive chemicals or dust. 

Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty. 

(2) In the case of electrical equipment that is new and unused at the workplace, the person conducting the business or undertaking: 

(a) is not required to comply with subregulation (1); and (b) must ensure that the equipment is inspected for obvious damage before being used. 

Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty. 

Note 

However, electrical equipment that is unsafe must not be used (see regulation 149). 

(3) The person must ensure that a record of any testing carried out under subregulation (1) is kept until the electrical equipment is: 

(a) next tested; or (b) permanently removed from the workplace or disposed of. 


Maximum penalty: tier I monetary penalty. 

(4) The record of testing: 

(a) must specify the following: (i) the name of the person who carried out the testing; (ii) the date of the testing; (iii) the outcome of the testing; (iv) the date on which the next testing must be carried out; and   
(b) may be in the form of a tag attached to the electrical equipment tested.

matches.target_title: 146 Meaning of electrical work

compliance.reasoning:
The POLICY establishes an internal verification/approval process for electrical competency (licences, qualifications, risk understanding, risk assessments/SWIs, and conditions/limitations including energised work permissions). The PROVISION defines what constitutes “electrical work” and enumerates exclusions (tasks that are not electrical work, and tasks allowed under supervision or without exposure to electrical risk). A compliance check is necessary because the POLICY governs who may perform electrical work and under what conditions, and therefore should align with the scope of “electrical work” and its exclusions.

Non-compliance arises because the POLICY treats “electrical work” broadly and requires verification/approval prior to carrying out electrical work in CSIRO, but it does not incorporate or reference the PROVISION’s exclusions. As written, the POLICY could be applied to activities that the PROVISION explicitly says are not “electrical work” (e.g., plugging in equipment, non-electrical component work without electrical risk, replacing a fuse/light bulb where safely performed by a non-expert, certain manufacturing/assembly tasks, mounting equipment not related to connection). This creates a definitional mismatch and may impose controls inconsistent with the legal/regulated definition being relied upon.

Additionally, the POLICY discusses supervision and restrictions for energised work, but it does not explicitly address the PROVISION’s supervised-assistance and supervised-duct/conduit work carve-outs (e.g., assistance by a supervised person with no contact with energised equipment; supervised work on ducts/conduits/troughs under specified conditions). Without mapping these scenarios, the POLICY does not clearly ensure that tasks falling outside “electrical work” are treated appropriately, nor that supervised non-licensed activities are governed consistently with the PROVISION’s conditions.
compliance.remediation: Amend the POLICY to align scope with the PROVISION by: (1) adding a definition of “electrical work” consistent with the PROVISION (including the listed inclusions); (2) explicitly listing or referencing the exclusions and clarifying that the verification/approval requirement applies only to activities that meet the definition of “electrical work”; (3) adding guidance for excluded activities (e.g., plug-in connection, non-electrical component work without electrical risk, minor replacements) and when risk assessment/SWIs are still required; (4) explicitly addressing supervised activities permitted under the PROVISION (assisting a licensed/registered worker, supervised conduit/duct work, eligibility/training work) and documenting supervision requirements and prohibitions (e.g., no physical contact with energised equipment).

matches.target_title: 147 Risk management
matches.target_text:
A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must manage risks to health and safety associated with electrical risks at the workplace, in accordance with Part 3.1. 

Example 

Electrical risks associated with the design, construction, installation, protection, maintenance and testing of electrical equipment and electrical installations at a workplace. 

Note 

WHS Act—section 19 (see regulation 9).

compliance.reasoning: The PROVISION requires the PCBU to manage risks to health and safety associated with electrical risks at the workplace (in accordance with Part 3.1). The POLICY establishes a structured process to manage electrical risks by verifying electrical worker competency (licences/qualifications/training/experience and understanding of task-specific risks and controls), requiring documented risk assessments and Safe Work Instructions, requiring additional authorization for energised work, and empowering managers/ESO to set conditions/limitations, require supervision/training, and stop work if documentation is missing or invalid. These measures are consistent with a risk management approach for electrical risks (e.g., maintenance/testing work) and therefore satisfy the provision at a procedural level.

matches.target_title: 157 Electrical work on energised electrical equipment—when permitted
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical work on energised electrical equipment is not carried out unless: 

(a) it is necessary in the interests of health and safety that the electrical work is carried out on the equipment while the equipment is energised; or Example 

It may be necessary that life-saving equipment remain energised and operating while electrical work is carried out on the equipment.   
(b) it is necessary that the electrical equipment to be worked on is energised in order for the work to be carried out properly; or   
(c) it is necessary for the purposes of testing required under regulation 155; or   
(d) there is no reasonable alternative means of carrying out the work. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) The electrical work that may be carried out under subregulation (1)(a), (b) and (d) may include testing of the energised electrical equipment.

compliance.reasoning: The provision prohibits energised electrical work unless one of the specified necessity conditions applies (health and safety necessity, work cannot be done properly de-energised, testing required, or no reasonable alternative). The policy requires a verification process and an internal permission/application for energised work, but it does not explicitly incorporate or require assessment/documentation that energised work meets one of the legal preconditions in (1)(a)-(d), nor does it state the default prohibition on energised work unless those conditions are satisfied. As written, the policy could allow approval of energised work based on licensing/competency and internal permission alone, without confirming the regulatory necessity criteria.
compliance.remediation: Amend the verification/approval and energised-work permission process to: (1) state that energised electrical work is prohibited unless one of (1)(a)-(d) applies; (2) require the worker/manager/ESO to document which specific condition applies and why de-energised work is not reasonably practicable; (3) require evidence/justification in the risk assessment/SWI and retain records; and (4) ensure approvals cannot be granted unless the necessity criterion is met (testing-only allowances should be tied to the regulation 155 testing purpose where applicable).

matches.target_title: 252 Who can be the design verifier
matches.target_text:
(1) A person is eligible to be a design verifier for the design of an item of plant if the person is a competent person.

(2) Despite subregulation (1), a person is not eligible to be a design verifier for the design of an item of plant if the person was involved in the production of the design.

matches.target_title: 240 Maintenance, inspection and testing of amusement devices and passenger ropeways
matches.target_text:
(1) The person with management or control of an amusement device or passenger ropeway at a workplace must ensure that the maintenance, inspection and, if necessary, testing of the device or ropeway is carried out:

(a) by a competent person; and (b) in accordance with:

(i) the recommendations of the designer or manufacturer or designer and manufacturer; or

(ii) if a maintenance manual for the device or ropeway has been prepared by a competent person, the requirements of the maintenance manual.

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

(2) A person is not a competent person to carry out a detailed inspection of an amusement device or passenger ropeway that includes an electrical installation unless the person is qualified, or is assisted by a person who is qualified, to inspect electrical installations.

compliance.reasoning: The provision requires that maintenance/inspection/testing of an amusement device or passenger ropeway be carried out by a competent person and in accordance with the designer/manufacturer recommendations or a competent-person-prepared maintenance manual. It also requires that a person conducting a detailed inspection that includes an electrical installation must be qualified to inspect electrical installations (or be assisted by someone who is). The policy establishes an internal process to verify electrical competency (licences/qualifications/training/experience, risk understanding, manager/ESO review, documentation, and conditions/limitations), which is relevant to ensuring work is done by competent persons. However, the policy does not specifically address (1) amusement devices or passenger ropeways, (2) the obligation to follow designer/manufacturer recommendations or a maintenance manual, or (3) the specific requirement that detailed inspections involving electrical installations be performed by a person qualified to inspect electrical installations (or assisted by such a person). Therefore, it only partially aligns and does not fully satisfy the provision’s requirements.
compliance.remediation: Amend the policy/procedure (or add a specific procedure for amusement devices/passenger ropeways) to: (1) explicitly require maintenance/inspection/testing to be performed in accordance with designer/manufacturer recommendations or an approved maintenance manual; (2) define and require appointment/verification of a 'competent person' for these devices; (3) require that any detailed inspection including an electrical installation is performed by a person qualified to inspect electrical installations, or under documented assistance/supervision by such a qualified person; and (4) include recordkeeping to evidence compliance (maintenance manuals, manufacturer instructions, inspection/test records, and qualification/assistance records).

matches.target_title: 95 Reassessment of competency of licence holder
matches.target_text:
The regulator may direct a licence holder to obtain a reassessment of the competency of the licence holder to carry out the high risk work covered by the licence if the regulator reasonably believes that the licence holder may not be competent to carry out that work. 

Examples 

1 The training or competency assessment of the licence holder did not meet the standard required to hold the licence.   
2 The regulator receives information that the licence holder has carried out high risk work incompetently. 

Subdivision 3 Amendment of licence document

source_text:
Section 154 of the WHS Regulations states that electrical work is not to be carried out on energised electrical equipment. However, exemptions do apply in certain circumstances provided in Section 157 of the regulations. Refer to Section 7 of this guideline for more information about energised electrical work requirements.

Wherever possible electrical work must be carried out de-energised, this includes testing and fault finding. All possible de-energised working methods must be identified and tried before working energised should be considered.

‘Test for Dead’ before you touch

Before electrical work is carried out, the equipment needs to be tested by a competent person to determine whether or not it is energised. You need to ensure:

 Each exposed part of the equipment is treated as energised until it is isolated and determined not to be energised; and   
 Each high voltage exposed part is earthed after being de-energised.

Electrical equipment that is de-energised must not be inadvertently energised while work is being carried out.

Work on Electrical Installations

Electrical work on an installation involving any part thereof, in service following construction, alteration or repair, it shall be verified, as far as practicable, that the installation is safe to energise and will operate in accordance with the requirements of AS 3000. Precautions shall be taken to ensure the safety of persons and to avoid damage to property and the electrical installation equipment during inspecting and testing.

Where electrical installation is an alteration or repair to an existing electrical installation, it shall be verified that the alteration or repair complies with this AS 3000 and does not impair the safety of the existing electrical installation.

matches.target_title: 155 Duty to determine whether equipment is energised
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that, before electrical work is carried out on electrical equipment, the equipment is tested by a competent person to determine whether or not it is energised. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

Note 

Regulation 157 allows electrical testing to be carried out on electrical equipment for the purposes of this regulation. Regulation 161 sets out how the testing is to be carried out. 

(2) The person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that: 

(a) each exposed part is treated as energised until it is isolated and determined not to be energised; and   
(b) each high-voltage exposed part is earthed after being deenergised. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning: The POLICY text is functionally related to the PROVISION because it sets operational requirements for testing electrical equipment before work and for treating exposed parts as energised until proven otherwise, which directly correspond to the duties imposed on the PCBU in the PROVISION. The POLICY states that before electrical work is carried out, equipment must be tested by a competent person to determine whether it is energised, and it requires that each exposed part be treated as energised until isolated and determined not energised, and that each high-voltage exposed part be earthed after being de-energised. These match PROVISION (1) and (2)(a)-(b).

matches.target_title: 157 Electrical work on energised electrical equipment—when permitted
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical work on energised electrical equipment is not carried out unless:
