(a) it is necessary in the interests of health and safety that the electrical work is carried out on the equipment while the equipment is energised; or Example 

It may be necessary that life-saving equipment remain energised and operating while electrical work is carried out on the equipment.   
(b) it is necessary that the electrical equipment to be worked on is energised in order for the work to be carried out properly; or   
(c) it is necessary for the purposes of testing required under regulation 155; or   
(d) there is no reasonable alternative means of carrying out the work. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) The electrical work that may be carried out under subregulation (1)(a), (b) and (d) may include testing of the energised electrical equipment.

compliance.reasoning: The POLICY is functionally related to the PROVISION because it sets internal requirements for when electrical work may be performed on energised equipment and emphasizes de-energised work as the default, aligning with the regulatory prohibition and conditional exemptions. The POLICY states energised electrical work is generally not permitted (referencing WHS Reg 154) and that all possible de-energised methods (including for testing and fault finding) must be identified and tried before considering energised work. This is consistent with the PROVISION’s requirement that energised work must not occur unless one of the necessity conditions applies (health and safety necessity, necessity for proper work, testing, or no reasonable alternative). The POLICY also includes controls consistent with safe practice (e.g., 'Test for Dead', treat exposed parts as energised until proven otherwise, earth high voltage parts after de-energising, prevent inadvertent re-energisation). While the POLICY does not restate each exemption criterion verbatim, it references the applicable exemption section and establishes a stricter default (de-energised wherever possible), which does not conflict with the PROVISION.

matches.target_title: 158 Preliminary steps
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure the following before electrical work on energised electrical equipment commences: 

(a) a risk assessment is conducted in relation to the proposed electrical work;   
(b) the area where the electrical work is to be carried out is clear of obstructions so as to allow for easy access and exit;   
(c) the point at which the electrical equipment can be disconnected or isolated from its electricity supply is: (i) clearly marked or labelled; and (ii) clear of obstructions so as to allow for easy access and exit by the worker who is to carry out the electrical work or any other competent person; and (iii) capable of being operated quickly;   
(d) the person authorises the electrical work after consulting with the person with management or control of the workplace. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(a), the risk assessment must be: 

(a) conducted by a competent person; and (b) recorded. 

(3) Subregulation (1)(c) does not apply to electrical work on electrical equipment if: 

(a) the work is to be carried out on the supply side of the main switch on the main switchboard for the equipment; and   
(b) the point at which the equipment can be disconnected from its electricity supply is not reasonably accessible from the work location.

compliance.reasoning: The POLICY addresses working de-energised wherever possible, “test for dead,” treating exposed parts as energised until proven otherwise, earthing high-voltage parts after de-energising, preventing inadvertent re-energisation, and verifying installations are safe to energise per AS 3000. The PROVISION, however, sets specific mandatory prerequisites before energised electrical work commences: (1) a risk assessment conducted, (2) the work area clear of obstructions for access/egress, (3) the isolation/disconnection point clearly marked/labelled, unobstructed, and operable quickly, and (4) formal authorisation after consultation with the person with management/control of the workplace; additionally, the risk assessment must be conducted by a competent person and recorded. The POLICY does not explicitly require a documented risk assessment for energised work, does not require the isolation point to be marked/labelled and operable quickly, does not require the work area and isolation point to be clear of obstructions for access/exit, and does not require authorisation after consultation with workplace management/control. Therefore it does not fully meet the PROVISION’s prerequisites for energised electrical work.
compliance.remediation: Update the POLICY (or add an energised work procedure/permit) to explicitly require, before any energised electrical work starts: (1) a risk assessment conducted by a competent person and recorded; (2) the work area is cleared of obstructions to allow easy access and exit; (3) the isolation/disconnection point is clearly marked/labelled, unobstructed for access/exit, and capable of being operated quickly (and address the stated exception where applicable); and (4) formal authorisation of the energised work after consultation with the person with management or control of the workplace. Ensure these steps are implemented via checklists/permits and retained as records.

matches.target_title: 161 How the work is to be carried out
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical work on energised electrical equipment is carried out: 

(a) by a competent person who has tools, testing equipment and personal protective equipment that: 

(i) are suitable for the work; and (ii) have been properly tested; and (iii) are maintained in good working order; and 

(b) in accordance with a safe work method statement prepared for the work; and   
(c) subject to subregulation (5), with a safety observer present who has the competence and qualifications specified in subregulation (4). 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) The person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure, so far as is reasonably practicable, that the person who carries out the electrical work uses the tools, testing equipment and personal protective equipment properly. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(3) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(b), the safe work method statement must: 

(a) identify the electrical work; and   
(b) specify hazards associated with that electrical work and risks associated with those hazards; and   
(c) describe the measures to be implemented to control the risks; and   
(d) describe how the risk control measures are to be implemented, monitored and reviewed. 

(4) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(c): 

(a) the safety observer must be competent: 

(b) the safety observer must have been assessed in the previous 12 months as competent to rescue and resuscitate a person. 

(5) A safety observer is not required if: 

(a) the work consists only of testing; and   
(b) the person conducting the business or undertaking has conducted a risk assessment under regulation 158(1)(a) that shows that there is no serious risk associated with the proposed work.

compliance.reasoning: The POLICY is functionally related to the PROVISION because it addresses requirements for electrical work on energised equipment (WHS Regs s154/157 context) and sets expectations around de-energised work, testing ('test for dead'), and verification of installations. However, it does not include several mandatory controls required by the PROVISION for energised electrical work: (1) it does not require that energised work be carried out in accordance with a safe work method statement (SWMS) that meets the specified content requirements (identify work, hazards/risks, controls, implementation/monitoring/review); (2) it does not require a safety observer for energised work, nor specify the observer competency and the requirement for assessment within the previous 12 months for rescue/resuscitation; (3) it does not require ensuring the competent person has suitable, properly tested, and maintained tools/testing equipment/PPE for energised work, nor the duty to ensure (so far as reasonably practicable) that these are used properly; (4) it does not reflect the exception where a safety observer is not required for testing-only work when a risk assessment shows no serious risk. Because these elements are absent or not explicit, the POLICY does not fully satisfy the PROVISION’s obligations for energised electrical work.
compliance.remediation: Update the POLICY to explicitly incorporate the PROVISION’s energised-work controls: require energised electrical work to be performed only by a competent person using suitable tools/testing equipment/PPE that are properly tested and maintained; require the PCBU to ensure (so far as reasonably practicable) proper use of such equipment; mandate an SWMS for energised work and include the required SWMS contents (work identification, hazards/risks, controls, implementation/monitoring/review); require a safety observer for energised work and specify observer competency plus rescue/resuscitation competency assessment within the last 12 months; include the testing-only exception where a safety observer is not required if a regulation 158 risk assessment shows no serious risk.

matches.target_title: 146 Meaning of electrical work

compliance.reasoning: The texts are functionally related because the POLICY sets rules for how “electrical work” must be performed (primarily de-energised, test-before-touch, verification/testing of installations), while the PROVISION defines what constitutes “electrical work” and what is excluded. A compliance check is necessary to ensure the POLICY’s scope and requirements align with the legal definition and exclusions. The POLICY is not fully compliant because it treats testing and fault finding as necessarily de-energised electrical work (“Wherever possible electrical work must be carried out de-energised, this includes testing and fault finding”), but the PROVISION’s definition explicitly includes “testing” as electrical work and also creates multiple exclusions (e.g., plug-and-socket connection, non-electrical component work with no electrical risk, replacing fuses/light bulbs if safely performed by non-experts, supervised assistance without contact with energised equipment, etc.). The POLICY does not acknowledge these exclusions or clarify that its requirements apply only when the activity falls within the regulated definition of electrical work. This can over-apply obligations to activities that the PROVISION says are not electrical work, and it also fails to incorporate the PROVISION’s supervision/eligibility carve-outs and boundary conditions for what is in-scope.
compliance.remediation: Amend the POLICY to (1) explicitly adopt the PROVISION’s definition of “electrical work” and list/recognize the key exclusions; (2) clarify scope—state that the de-energised/test-for-dead and installation verification requirements apply when the task is “electrical work” as defined, and specify how excluded tasks are managed (e.g., general safety procedures rather than electrical-work controls); (3) add guidance for supervised assistants and trainees consistent with the PROVISION (no physical contact with energised equipment, direct supervision requirements); and (4) ensure any statements about testing/fault-finding reflect that some testing may require energisation under permitted exemptions, while still requiring de-energised methods where practicable.

matches.target_title: 150 Inspection and testing of electrical equipment
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must ensure that electrical equipment is regularly inspected and tested by a competent person if the electrical equipment is: 

(a) supplied with electricity through an electrical socket outlet; and   
(b) used in an environment in which the normal use of electrical equipment exposes the equipment to operating conditions that are likely to result in damage to the equipment or a reduction in its expected life span, including conditions that involve exposure to moisture, heat, vibration, mechanical damage, corrosive chemicals or dust. 

Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty. 

(2) In the case of electrical equipment that is new and unused at the workplace, the person conducting the business or undertaking: 

(a) is not required to comply with subregulation (1); and (b) must ensure that the equipment is inspected for obvious damage before being used. 

Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty. 

Note 

However, electrical equipment that is unsafe must not be used (see regulation 149). 

(3) The person must ensure that a record of any testing carried out under subregulation (1) is kept until the electrical equipment is: 

(a) next tested; or (b) permanently removed from the workplace or disposed of. 


Maximum penalty: tier I monetary penalty. 

(4) The record of testing: 

(a) must specify the following: (i) the name of the person who carried out the testing; (ii) the date of the testing; (iii) the outcome of the testing; (iv) the date on which the next testing must be carried out; and   
(b) may be in the form of a tag attached to the electrical equipment tested.

compliance.reasoning: The POLICY addresses safe methods for performing electrical work (de-energised work, test-for-dead, preventing inadvertent re-energisation, and verification of installations to AS 3000). The PROVISION imposes a separate but related obligation on the PCBU to ensure certain socket-outlet supplied electrical equipment used in harsh/damaging environments is regularly inspected and tested by a competent person, with specific recordkeeping requirements (who, when, outcome, next test date; tag permitted) and a pre-use visual inspection requirement for new/unused equipment. The POLICY does not establish a program for regular inspection/testing of such equipment, does not define applicability to socket-outlet equipment in specified environments, and does not include the mandated testing records content/retention. Therefore it does not demonstrate compliance with the PROVISION.
compliance.remediation: Add a documented inspection and test (test-and-tag) program requiring: (1) identification of in-scope equipment (socket-outlet supplied equipment used in environments with moisture/heat/vibration/mechanical damage/corrosives/dust); (2) regular inspection and testing intervals and responsibility assignment; (3) testing performed by a competent person; (4) pre-use inspection for obvious damage for new/unused equipment; (5) recordkeeping until next test or disposal, including tester name, test date, outcome, and next test due date (tag or equivalent record); and (6) a control that unsafe equipment is removed from service.

matches.target_title: 154 Electrical work on energised electrical equipment—prohibited
matches.target_text:
Subject to this Division, a person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical work is not carried out on electrical equipment while the equipment is energised. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning: The PROVISION imposes an obligation that electrical work must not be carried out on energised electrical equipment. The POLICY directly addresses this requirement by stating electrical work is not to be carried out on energised equipment, requiring de-energised methods wherever possible (including testing and fault finding), and mandating verification steps such as 'test for dead' and preventing inadvertent re-energisation. While the POLICY mentions exemptions under Section 157, it frames them as limited circumstances and does not contradict the general prohibition in the PROVISION. Therefore, the POLICY is aligned with and supports compliance with the PROVISION.

matches.target_title: 160 Contact with equipment being worked on
matches.target_text:
A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that, while electrical work is being carried out on energised electrical equipment, all persons are prevented from creating an electrical risk by inadvertently making contact with an exposed energised component of the equipment. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning: The provision requires the PCBU to ensure that, when electrical work is performed on energised electrical equipment, all persons are prevented from creating an electrical risk by inadvertently contacting exposed energised components. The policy strongly emphasises avoiding energised work (de-energise wherever possible, 'test for dead', treat exposed parts as energised until proven otherwise, prevent inadvertent re-energisation) and includes general inspection/testing precautions. However, it does not set out specific controls for situations where energised work is actually being carried out (e.g., barriers/insulation, exclusion zones, access control, supervision, signage, use of insulating mats/covers, PPE requirements, or procedures to prevent inadvertent contact with exposed energised components). Therefore, it does not fully address the provision’s obligation for energised work scenarios.
compliance.remediation: Add an explicit section for energised electrical work that requires controls to prevent inadvertent contact with exposed energised components, such as: establishing exclusion zones and access control; using insulating barriers/covers and shrouding; applying lockout/tagout for adjacent circuits where possible; mandatory PPE and insulated tools; signage and spotter/supervision requirements; and a documented energised work permit/JSA process specifying how persons will be prevented from contact during the task.

matches.target_title: 149 Unsafe electrical equipment
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must ensure that any unsafe electrical equipment at the workplace: 

(a) is disconnected (or isolated) from its electricity supply; and (b) once disconnected (or isolated): 

(i) is not reconnected until it is repaired or tested and found to be safe; or (ii) is replaced or permanently removed from use. 

Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty. 

(2) For the purposes of this regulation, electrical equipment or a component of electrical equipment is unsafe if there are reasonable grounds for believing it to be unsafe.

compliance.reasoning: The provision requires the PCBU to ensure unsafe electrical equipment is disconnected/isolated and, once isolated, is not reconnected until repaired or tested and found safe, or is replaced/permanently removed. The policy focuses on prohibiting energized work, requiring de-energised work where possible, “test for dead,” treating exposed parts as energised until isolated, earthing high-voltage parts after de-energising, preventing inadvertent re-energisation during work, and verifying installations are safe to energise after construction/alteration/repair. However, it does not explicitly address the required controls for 'unsafe electrical equipment' (i.e., mandatory disconnection/isolation upon being unsafe, prohibition on reconnection until repaired/tested safe, or replacement/permanent removal). The policy’s testing and isolation concepts partially align but do not clearly fulfill the specific obligation to manage unsafe equipment status and reconnection controls.
compliance.remediation: Add an explicit section on unsafe electrical equipment requiring: (1) immediate disconnection/isolation when there are reasonable grounds to believe equipment is unsafe; (2) tagging/lockout and controls to prevent reconnection; (3) prohibition on reconnection until repaired or tested and confirmed safe by a competent person; and (4) replacement or permanent removal from service where repair/testing is not undertaken. Define 'unsafe' consistent with the regulation (reasonable grounds) and assign responsibility to the PCBU for ensuring these steps occur.

matches.target_title: 156 De-energised equipment must not be inadvertently re-energised
matches.target_text:
A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical equipment that has been de-energised to allow electrical work to be carried out on it is not inadvertently re-energised while the work is being carried out. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning: The provision imposes an obligation to ensure de-energised electrical equipment is not inadvertently re-energised while work is being carried out. The policy explicitly states: "Electrical equipment that is de-energised must not be inadvertently energised while work is being carried out," which directly addresses and satisfies the provision’s requirement.

source_text:
All electrical tools and equipment should be suitable for the work and be maintained in good working order, including regular maintenance, inspection and testing. Inadequate maintenance may lead to serious electrical risks, for example an insulating medium might conceal a mechanical defect that could cause an open circuit in a testing device. Where any doubt exists about adequacy of the insulation of tools and equipment they should not be used and removed from service.

Insulating barriers and insulating mats

Insulating covers and mats used for electrical purposes should comply with AS/NZS2978: Insulating mats for electrical purposes. Insulated barriers should be of suitable material to effectively separate electrical workers from adjacent energised electrical equipment or energised parts at a higher voltage e.g. protection from low voltage energised parts when working with extra-low voltage. These items should be visually inspected before and after each use. If the items have a defect that adversely affect the performance, then it must be removed from service.

matches.target_title: 150 Inspection and testing of electrical equipment
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must ensure that electrical equipment is regularly inspected and tested by a competent person if the electrical equipment is: 

(a) supplied with electricity through an electrical socket outlet; and   
(b) used in an environment in which the normal use of electrical equipment exposes the equipment to operating conditions that are likely to result in damage to the equipment or a reduction in its expected life span, including conditions that involve exposure to moisture, heat, vibration, mechanical damage, corrosive chemicals or dust. 

Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty. 

(2) In the case of electrical equipment that is new and unused at the workplace, the person conducting the business or undertaking: 

(a) is not required to comply with subregulation (1); and (b) must ensure that the equipment is inspected for obvious damage before being used. 

Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty. 

Note 

However, electrical equipment that is unsafe must not be used (see regulation 149). 

(3) The person must ensure that a record of any testing carried out under subregulation (1) is kept until the electrical equipment is: 

(a) next tested; or (b) permanently removed from the workplace or disposed of. 


Maximum penalty: tier I monetary penalty. 

(4) The record of testing: 

(a) must specify the following: (i) the name of the person who carried out the testing; (ii) the date of the testing; (iii) the outcome of the testing; (iv) the date on which the next testing must be carried out; and   
(b) may be in the form of a tag attached to the electrical equipment tested.

compliance.reasoning: The POLICY addresses maintenance, inspection and testing of electrical tools/equipment and removal from service when insulation adequacy is in doubt, which is functionally related to the PROVISION requiring regular inspection and testing by a competent person for certain socket-outlet supplied equipment used in damaging environments, plus recordkeeping of testing. However, the POLICY does not (1) explicitly limit/trigger the requirement to the specified high-risk environments and socket-outlet supplied equipment, (2) require inspection/testing to be performed by a competent person, (3) include the specific requirement for new/unused equipment to be inspected for obvious damage before first use, or (4) require keeping testing records with the specified minimum contents and retention period. Therefore it does not fully satisfy the PROVISION.
compliance.remediation: Update the policy to: (1) require regular inspection and testing by a competent person for electrical equipment supplied via socket outlets when used in environments likely to cause damage (moisture, heat, vibration, mechanical damage, corrosive chemicals, dust); (2) require new/unused equipment to be inspected for obvious damage before first use; (3) mandate keeping testing records until next test or removal/disposal, including tester name, test date, outcome, and next test due date (e.g., via tags); and (4) clarify applicability/scope to ensure these triggers are covered.

matches.target_title: 155 Duty to determine whether equipment is energised
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that, before electrical work is carried out on electrical equipment, the equipment is tested by a competent person to determine whether or not it is energised. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

Note 

Regulation 157 allows electrical testing to be carried out on electrical equipment for the purposes of this regulation. Regulation 161 sets out how the testing is to be carried out. 

(2) The person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that: 

(a) each exposed part is treated as energised until it is isolated and determined not to be energised; and   
(b) each high-voltage exposed part is earthed after being deenergised. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning: The provision requires that before electrical work is carried out on electrical equipment, the equipment must be tested by a competent person to determine whether it is energised, and that exposed parts are treated as energised until isolated and proven de-energised, with high-voltage exposed parts earthed after de-energising. The policy focuses on suitability and maintenance of electrical tools/equipment and inspection/removal from service of insulating mats/barriers, but it does not require pre-work energisation testing by a competent person, does not state the 'treat as energised until isolated and determined not energised' rule, and does not address earthing of high-voltage exposed parts after de-energising. Therefore it does not meet the provision’s specific obligations.
compliance.remediation: Update the policy to explicitly require: (1) before electrical work on electrical equipment, testing by a competent person to confirm whether the equipment is energised (with reference to applicable testing procedures); (2) treating all exposed parts as energised until isolated and proven de-energised; and (3) earthing each high-voltage exposed part after it is de-energised. Include responsibilities, competency requirements, and procedural steps consistent with the relevant regulations.

matches.target_title: 161 How the work is to be carried out
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical work on energised electrical equipment is carried out: 

(a) by a competent person who has tools, testing equipment and personal protective equipment that: 

(i) are suitable for the work; and (ii) have been properly tested; and (iii) are maintained in good working order; and 

(b) in accordance with a safe work method statement prepared for the work; and   
(c) subject to subregulation (5), with a safety observer present who has the competence and qualifications specified in subregulation (4). 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) The person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure, so far as is reasonably practicable, that the person who carries out the electrical work uses the tools, testing equipment and personal protective equipment properly. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(3) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(b), the safe work method statement must: 

(a) identify the electrical work; and   
(b) specify hazards associated with that electrical work and risks associated with those hazards; and   
(c) describe the measures to be implemented to control the risks; and   
(d) describe how the risk control measures are to be implemented, monitored and reviewed. 

(4) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(c): 

(a) the safety observer must be competent: 

(b) the safety observer must have been assessed in the previous 12 months as competent to rescue and resuscitate a person. 

(5) A safety observer is not required if: 

(a) the work consists only of testing; and   
(b) the person conducting the business or undertaking has conducted a risk assessment under regulation 158(1)(a) that shows that there is no serious risk associated with the proposed work.

compliance.reasoning: The POLICY is functionally related to the PROVISION because it addresses the requirement that tools, testing equipment and PPE used for energised electrical work be suitable, properly tested, and maintained in good working order. It also includes inspection and removal-from-service controls for insulating mats/barriers, which align with suitability/maintenance expectations. However, the PROVISION imposes additional mandatory elements not covered by the POLICY: (1) energised electrical work must be carried out in accordance with a safe work method statement (SWMS) with specified contents; (2) a competent safety observer must be present (unless the testing-only/risk-assessed exception applies) and must have been assessed within the previous 12 months as competent to rescue and resuscitate; and (3) the PCBU must ensure, so far as reasonably practicable, that workers use tools/testing equipment/PPE properly. Because these obligations are absent from the POLICY text provided, the POLICY does not fully satisfy the PROVISION.
compliance.remediation: Update the policy/procedure for energised electrical work to: (1) require a SWMS for energised electrical work and specify minimum SWMS content (work identification, hazards/risks, control measures, and implementation/monitoring/review); (2) require a safety observer meeting competency and current (within 12 months) rescue/resuscitation assessment requirements, and document the limited exception for testing-only work supported by a risk assessment; (3) include an explicit requirement that the PCBU ensures workers use tools, testing equipment and PPE properly (e.g., training, supervision, verification).

matches.target_title: 158 Preliminary steps
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure the following before electrical work on energised electrical equipment commences: 

(a) a risk assessment is conducted in relation to the proposed electrical work;   
(b) the area where the electrical work is to be carried out is clear of obstructions so as to allow for easy access and exit;   
(c) the point at which the electrical equipment can be disconnected or isolated from its electricity supply is: (i) clearly marked or labelled; and (ii) clear of obstructions so as to allow for easy access and exit by the worker who is to carry out the electrical work or any other competent person; and (iii) capable of being operated quickly;   
(d) the person authorises the electrical work after consulting with the person with management or control of the workplace. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(a), the risk assessment must be: 

(a) conducted by a competent person; and (b) recorded. 

(3) Subregulation (1)(c) does not apply to electrical work on electrical equipment if: 

(a) the work is to be carried out on the supply side of the main switch on the main switchboard for the equipment; and   
(b) the point at which the equipment can be disconnected from its electricity supply is not reasonably accessible from the work location.

matches.target_title: 149 Unsafe electrical equipment
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must ensure that any unsafe electrical equipment at the workplace: 

(a) is disconnected (or isolated) from its electricity supply; and (b) once disconnected (or isolated): 

(i) is not reconnected until it is repaired or tested and found to be safe; or (ii) is replaced or permanently removed from use. 

Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty. 

(2) For the purposes of this regulation, electrical equipment or a component of electrical equipment is unsafe if there are reasonable grounds for believing it to be unsafe.

compliance.reasoning: The provision requires that unsafe electrical equipment be disconnected/isolated and not returned to service until repaired or tested safe, or else replaced/removed. The policy states that tools/equipment must be maintained, inspected and tested; that where there is any doubt about adequacy of insulation they must not be used and must be removed from service; and that defective insulating mats/barriers must be removed from service. These controls functionally meet the provision’s intent to prevent use of unsafe equipment and ensure it is removed from service until safe.

matches.target_title: 147 Risk management
matches.target_text:
A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must manage risks to health and safety associated with electrical risks at the workplace, in accordance with Part 3.1. 

Example 

Electrical risks associated with the design, construction, installation, protection, maintenance and testing of electrical equipment and electrical installations at a workplace. 

Note 

WHS Act—section 19 (see regulation 9).

compliance.reasoning: The provision requires the PCBU to manage risks to health and safety associated with electrical risks at the workplace (including risks arising from maintenance and testing of electrical equipment) in accordance with the general risk management requirements. The policy establishes controls that directly manage electrical risks by requiring electrical tools/equipment to be suitable for the work, maintained in good working order with regular maintenance/inspection/testing, removed from service where insulation adequacy is in doubt, and requiring insulating mats/barriers to meet a specified standard (AS/NZS 2978), be suitable for separation from energised parts, be visually inspected before/after use, and removed from service if defective. These measures are consistent with managing electrical risks and align with the provision’s intent.

matches.target_title: 151 Untested electrical equipment not to be used
matches.target_text:
A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure, so far as is reasonably practicable, that electrical equipment is not used if the equipment: 

(a) is required to be tested under regulation 150; and (b) has not been tested. 

Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning: The provision requires that electrical equipment that is required to be tested under regulation 150 must not be used if it has not been tested. The policy discusses maintaining tools and equipment in good working order and includes inspection and testing as part of maintenance, and states that where there is doubt about insulation adequacy the equipment should not be used and removed from service. However, it does not explicitly require that equipment subject to mandatory testing under regulation 150 must not be used unless it has been tested (i.e., it lacks a clear prohibition tied to the regulatory testing requirement and does not reference the required testing status as a condition of use). Therefore, the policy does not fully satisfy the specific obligation in the provision.
compliance.remediation: Amend the policy to explicitly state that any electrical equipment required to be tested under regulation 150 must not be used unless it has been tested in accordance with that regulation (including defined test intervals, record-keeping, tagging/verification of test status, and a clear process to remove untested equipment from service).

matches.target_title: 160 Contact with equipment being worked on
matches.target_text:
A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that, while electrical work is being carried out on energised electrical equipment, all persons are prevented from creating an electrical risk by inadvertently making contact with an exposed energised component of the equipment. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning: The PROVISION requires the PCBU to ensure that during energised electrical work, all persons are prevented from creating an electrical risk by inadvertently contacting exposed energised components. The POLICY addresses suitability and maintenance of electrical tools/equipment and the use/inspection/removal-from-service of insulating barriers and mats. While these controls can contribute to preventing inadvertent contact, the POLICY does not explicitly require preventing all persons from inadvertent contact with exposed energised components during energised work, nor does it set out mandatory controls (e.g., guarding, exclusion zones, supervision, access control, or procedures) to ensure this outcome. Therefore, the POLICY only partially addresses the obligation and does not demonstrate full compliance with the PROVISION.
compliance.remediation: Amend the policy to explicitly require that, whenever energised electrical work is performed, the PCBU must implement controls to prevent any person from inadvertent contact with exposed energised components (e.g., insulating barriers/blankets rated for the voltage, guarding, exclusion zones and access control, signage, supervision/spotters where needed, and documented procedures/permits). Define responsibilities, minimum control measures, and verification (pre-job checks and ongoing monitoring) to ensure the prevention outcome is achieved.

matches.target_title: 240 Maintenance, inspection and testing of amusement devices and passenger ropeways
matches.target_text:
(1) The person with management or control of an amusement device or passenger ropeway at a workplace must ensure that the maintenance, inspection and, if necessary, testing of the device or ropeway is carried out:

(a) by a competent person; and (b) in accordance with:

(i) the recommendations of the designer or manufacturer or designer and manufacturer; or

(ii) if a maintenance manual for the device or ropeway has been prepared by a competent person, the requirements of the maintenance manual.

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

(2) A person is not a competent person to carry out a detailed inspection of an amusement device or passenger ropeway that includes an electrical installation unless the person is qualified, or is assisted by a person who is qualified, to inspect electrical installations.

compliance.reasoning: The provision requires the person with management/control of an amusement device or passenger ropeway to ensure maintenance, inspection and (if necessary) testing is carried out by a competent person and in accordance with manufacturer/designer recommendations or a competent-person-prepared maintenance manual. It also requires that any detailed inspection involving an electrical installation be performed by a qualified person (or assisted by one). The policy addresses electrical tools/equipment and insulating mats/barriers, requiring suitability, regular maintenance/inspection/testing, and removal from service when insulation is doubtful or defects exist. However, it does not (1) explicitly cover amusement devices/passenger ropeways, (2) require maintenance/inspection/testing to be performed by a competent person, (3) require alignment with manufacturer/designer recommendations or a formal maintenance manual, or (4) require electrical-installation inspections to be performed by (or assisted by) a qualified electrical inspector. Therefore, the policy does not fully meet the provision’s specific competency/qualification and documentation/alignment requirements.
compliance.remediation: Update the policy/procedure for amusement devices and passenger ropeways to: (1) mandate maintenance/inspection/testing be performed by a competent person; (2) require work be conducted in accordance with manufacturer/designer recommendations or an approved maintenance manual prepared by a competent person; (3) specify that detailed inspections involving electrical installations must be performed by a qualified person (or under assistance/supervision by a qualified person); and (4) implement recordkeeping to evidence competency/qualifications and adherence to the applicable recommendations/manual.

matches.target_title: 144 Meaning of electrical equipment
matches.target_text:
(1) In this Part, electrical equipment means any apparatus, appliance, cable, conductor, fitting, insulator, material, meter or wire that: 

(a) is used for controlling, generating, supplying, transforming or transmitting electricity at a voltage greater than extra-low voltage; or   
(b) is operated by electricity at a voltage greater than extra-low voltage; or   
(c) is part of an electrical installation located in an area in which the atmosphere presents a risk to health and safety from fire or explosion; or   
(d) is, or is part of, an active impressed current cathodic protection system within the meaning of AS 2832.1:2015 (Cathodic protection of metals—Pipes and cables). 

(2) In this Part, electrical equipment does not include any apparatus, appliance, cable, conductor, fitting, insulator, material, meter or wire that is part of a vehicle that is a motor car or motorcycle if: 

(a) the equipment is part of a unit of the vehicle that provides propulsion for the vehicle; or   
(b) the electricity source for the equipment is a unit of the vehicle that provides propulsion for the vehicle. 

Note 

See the jurisdictional note in the Appendix.

source_text:
If an electrical worker needs to carry out electrical work on a site other than their home site, they should notify the applicable Site Electrical Safety Officer (Site ESO) for the site they plan to work on of their intention to conduct electrical work.

Upon request, electrical workers should provide the ESO with a copy of their completed Verification of electrical competency, Activity risk assessment and SWI for review.

Example – Notifying a Site ESO when working on a different site

An Electrical worker from Bribie Island needs to go to St Lucia to carry out electrical testing of instrumentation. If there is a Site ESO at St Lucia, the worker should the Site ESO of their plans to conduct electrical work.

matches.target_title: 161 How the work is to be carried out
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical work on energised electrical equipment is carried out: 

(a) by a competent person who has tools, testing equipment and personal protective equipment that: 

(i) are suitable for the work; and (ii) have been properly tested; and (iii) are maintained in good working order; and 

(b) in accordance with a safe work method statement prepared for the work; and   
(c) subject to subregulation (5), with a safety observer present who has the competence and qualifications specified in subregulation (4). 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) The person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure, so far as is reasonably practicable, that the person who carries out the electrical work uses the tools, testing equipment and personal protective equipment properly. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(3) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(b), the safe work method statement must: 

(a) identify the electrical work; and   
(b) specify hazards associated with that electrical work and risks associated with those hazards; and   
(c) describe the measures to be implemented to control the risks; and   
(d) describe how the risk control measures are to be implemented, monitored and reviewed. 

(4) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(c): 

(a) the safety observer must be competent: 

(b) the safety observer must have been assessed in the previous 12 months as competent to rescue and resuscitate a person. 

(5) A safety observer is not required if: 

(a) the work consists only of testing; and   
(b) the person conducting the business or undertaking has conducted a risk assessment under regulation 158(1)(a) that shows that there is no serious risk associated with the proposed work.

(c) the point at which the electrical equipment can be disconnected or isolated from its electricity supply is: (i) clearly marked or labelled; and (ii) clear of obstructions so as to allow for easy access and exit by the worker who is to carry out the electrical work or any other competent person; and (iii) capable of being operated quickly;   
(d) the person authorises the electrical work after consulting with the person with management or control of the workplace. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(a), the risk assessment must be: 

(a) conducted by a competent person; and (b) recorded. 

(3) Subregulation (1)(c) does not apply to electrical work on electrical equipment if:

(b) the point at which the equipment can be disconnected from its electricity supply is not reasonably accessible from the work location.

matches.target_title: 155 Duty to determine whether equipment is energised
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that, before electrical work is carried out on electrical equipment, the equipment is tested by a competent person to determine whether or not it is energised. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

Note 

Regulation 157 allows electrical testing to be carried out on electrical equipment for the purposes of this regulation. Regulation 161 sets out how the testing is to be carried out. 

(2) The person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that: 

(a) each exposed part is treated as energised until it is isolated and determined not to be energised; and   
(b) each high-voltage exposed part is earthed after being deenergised.

matches.target_title: 146 Meaning of electrical work

compliance.reasoning: The provision defines what constitutes “electrical work” (including testing, installing, repairing, maintaining, etc.) and what is excluded. The policy sets an internal procedural control requiring electrical workers to notify the applicable Site Electrical Safety Officer when performing electrical work at a site other than their home site, and to provide competency verification and risk documentation upon request. This policy does not contradict the provision’s definitions or exclusions; it applies when the activity falls within the provision’s definition of electrical work (e.g., electrical testing of instrumentation) and adds an administrative notification requirement. Therefore, it is consistent with and supportive of managing activities that meet the defined scope of electrical work.

matches.target_title: 147 Risk management
matches.target_text:
A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must manage risks to health and safety associated with electrical risks at the workplace, in accordance with Part 3.1. 

Example 

Electrical risks associated with the design, construction, installation, protection, maintenance and testing of electrical equipment and electrical installations at a workplace. 

Note 

WHS Act—section 19 (see regulation 9).

compliance.reasoning: The provision imposes a general duty on the PCBU to manage risks to health and safety associated with electrical risks at the workplace. The policy requires electrical workers to notify the applicable Site Electrical Safety Officer when performing electrical work at a non-home site and to provide competency verification and risk assessment/SWI documentation upon request. These controls support risk management for electrical work (planning, oversight, verification of competency, and review of risk controls) and are consistent with the obligation to manage electrical risks.

matches.target_title: 150 Inspection and testing of electrical equipment
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must ensure that electrical equipment is regularly inspected and tested by a competent person if the electrical equipment is: 

(a) supplied with electricity through an electrical socket outlet; and   
(b) used in an environment in which the normal use of electrical equipment exposes the equipment to operating conditions that are likely to result in damage to the equipment or a reduction in its expected life span, including conditions that involve exposure to moisture, heat, vibration, mechanical damage, corrosive chemicals or dust. 

Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty. 

(2) In the case of electrical equipment that is new and unused at the workplace, the person conducting the business or undertaking: 

(a) is not required to comply with subregulation (1); and (b) must ensure that the equipment is inspected for obvious damage before being used. 

Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty. 

Note 

However, electrical equipment that is unsafe must not be used (see regulation 149). 

(3) The person must ensure that a record of any testing carried out under subregulation (1) is kept until the electrical equipment is: 

(a) next tested; or (b) permanently removed from the workplace or disposed of. 


Maximum penalty: tier I monetary penalty. 

(4) The record of testing: 

(a) must specify the following: (i) the name of the person who carried out the testing; (ii) the date of the testing; (iii) the outcome of the testing; (iv) the date on which the next testing must be carried out; and   
(b) may be in the form of a tag attached to the electrical equipment tested.

matches.target_title: 157 Electrical work on energised electrical equipment—when permitted
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical work on energised electrical equipment is not carried out unless: 

(a) it is necessary in the interests of health and safety that the electrical work is carried out on the equipment while the equipment is energised; or Example 

It may be necessary that life-saving equipment remain energised and operating while electrical work is carried out on the equipment.   
(b) it is necessary that the electrical equipment to be worked on is energised in order for the work to be carried out properly; or   
(c) it is necessary for the purposes of testing required under regulation 155; or   
(d) there is no reasonable alternative means of carrying out the work. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) The electrical work that may be carried out under subregulation (1)(a), (b) and (d) may include testing of the energised electrical equipment.

matches.target_title: 160 Contact with equipment being worked on
matches.target_text:
A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that, while electrical work is being carried out on energised electrical equipment, all persons are prevented from creating an electrical risk by inadvertently making contact with an exposed energised component of the equipment. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

matches.target_title: 166 Duty of person conducting a business or undertaking
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must ensure, so far as is reasonably practicable, that no person, plant or thing at the workplace comes within an unsafe distance of an overhead or underground electric line. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) If it is not reasonably practicable to ensure the safe distance of a person, plant or thing from an overhead or underground electric line, the person conducting the business or undertaking at the workplace must ensure that:
(a) a risk assessment is conducted in relation to the proposed work; and 

(b) control measures implemented are consistent with: 

(i) the risk assessment; and 

(ii) if an electricity supply authority is responsible for the electric line, any requirements of the authority. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

Notes 

1 [Relevant electricity safety legislation] will also apply to the person conducting the business or undertaking.

matches.target_title: 163 Duty of person conducting business or undertaking
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking that includes the carrying out of construction work must comply with AS/NZS 3012:2019 (Electrical installations—Construction and demolition sites). Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.   
(2) For the purposes of subregulation (1), AS/NZS 3012:2019 (Electrical installations— Construction and demolition sites) applies as if any term that is defined in that Standard and that is also defined in the Act or these Regulations has the same meaning as it has in the Act or these Regulations.   
(3) If any requirement in AS/NZS 3012:2019 (Electrical installations— Construction and demolition sites) deals with the same matter as a requirement under this Part, it is sufficient that the person conducting the business or undertaking complies with the requirement in AS/NZS 3012:2019 as modified by subregulation (2).

source_text: To ensure that electrical work on de-energised equipment cannot be inadvertently re-energised, workers are required to isolate the equipment at the electrical supply source. The switch must be locked out and tagged to prevent re-energisation. Workers must test the electrical equipment to confirm that it has been de-energised before commencing work. The worker should use personal locks and danger tags at the isolation point to prevent the risk of inadvertent re-energisation.
