(c) the point at which the electrical equipment can be disconnected or isolated from its electricity supply is: (i) clearly marked or labelled; and (ii) clear of obstructions so as to allow for easy access and exit by the worker who is to carry out the electrical work or any other competent person; and (iii) capable of being operated quickly;   
(d) the person authorises the electrical work after consulting with the person with management or control of the workplace. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(a), the risk assessment must be: 

(a) conducted by a competent person; and (b) recorded. 

(3) Subregulation (1)(c) does not apply to electrical work on electrical equipment if: 

(a) the work is to be carried out on the supply side of the main switch on the main switchboard for the equipment; and   
(b) the point at which the equipment can be disconnected from its electricity supply is not reasonably accessible from the work location.

matches.target_title: 155 Duty to determine whether equipment is energised
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that, before electrical work is carried out on electrical equipment, the equipment is tested by a competent person to determine whether or not it is energised. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

Note 

Regulation 157 allows electrical testing to be carried out on electrical equipment for the purposes of this regulation. Regulation 161 sets out how the testing is to be carried out. 

(2) The person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that: 

(a) each exposed part is treated as energised until it is isolated and determined not to be energised; and   
(b) each high-voltage exposed part is earthed after being deenergised. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning: The policy is functionally related to the provision because it addresses controls required before electrical work (isolation/lockout/tagout and testing to confirm de-energised). It complies with Provision (1) in substance by requiring testing to confirm equipment is de-energised before commencing work, but it does not explicitly require that the testing be performed by a competent person. It also does not address Provision (2)(a) that exposed parts must be treated as energised until isolated and determined not energised, nor Provision (2)(b) that each high-voltage exposed part is earthed after being de-energised. Therefore, the policy does not fully meet the provision’s requirements.
compliance.remediation: Update the policy to: (1) explicitly require testing to be performed by a competent person (and reference applicable testing procedures/standards if relevant); (2) state that all exposed parts must be treated as energised until isolated and proven de-energised; and (3) require that high-voltage exposed parts are earthed after de-energisation (including when/where earthing is required and who is responsible).

matches.target_title: 156 De-energised equipment must not be inadvertently re-energised
matches.target_text:
A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical equipment that has been de-energised to allow electrical work to be carried out on it is not inadvertently re-energised while the work is being carried out. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning: The provision requires ensuring de-energised electrical equipment is not inadvertently re-energised during electrical work. The policy directly addresses this obligation by requiring isolation at the supply source, lockout and tagging, use of personal locks and danger tags at the isolation point, and testing to confirm de-energisation before commencing work—controls that are intended to prevent inadvertent re-energisation while work is carried out.

matches.target_title: 149 Unsafe electrical equipment
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must ensure that any unsafe electrical equipment at the workplace: 

(a) is disconnected (or isolated) from its electricity supply; and (b) once disconnected (or isolated): 

(i) is not reconnected until it is repaired or tested and found to be safe; or (ii) is replaced or permanently removed from use. 

Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty. 

(2) For the purposes of this regulation, electrical equipment or a component of electrical equipment is unsafe if there are reasonable grounds for believing it to be unsafe.

compliance.reasoning: The provision requires the PCBU to ensure that unsafe electrical equipment is isolated/disconnected and, once isolated, is not reconnected until it is repaired or tested and found safe, or is replaced/permanently removed from use. The policy addresses isolation/lockout/tagout and testing to confirm de-energisation before commencing work, which aligns with the disconnection/isolation aspect. However, it does not address the required controls after isolation: prohibiting reconnection until repair/testing confirms safety, or replacement/permanent removal. It also does not define or trigger actions based on equipment being 'unsafe' (reasonable grounds). Therefore, the policy only partially covers the provision and is not fully compliant.
compliance.remediation: Amend the policy to: (1) explicitly require that any electrical equipment reasonably believed to be unsafe is disconnected/isolated from supply; (2) prohibit reconnection until the equipment is repaired or tested and verified safe by a competent person; and (3) require replacement or permanent removal from use where repair/testing is not performed or the equipment cannot be made safe. Include criteria/process for identifying 'unsafe' equipment and responsibilities (e.g., PCBU/supervisor) for ensuring it remains disconnected.

matches.target_title: 160 Contact with equipment being worked on
matches.target_text:
A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that, while electrical work is being carried out on energised electrical equipment, all persons are prevented from creating an electrical risk by inadvertently making contact with an exposed energised component of the equipment. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

matches.target_title: 150 Inspection and testing of electrical equipment
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must ensure that electrical equipment is regularly inspected and tested by a competent person if the electrical equipment is: 

(a) supplied with electricity through an electrical socket outlet; and   
(b) used in an environment in which the normal use of electrical equipment exposes the equipment to operating conditions that are likely to result in damage to the equipment or a reduction in its expected life span, including conditions that involve exposure to moisture, heat, vibration, mechanical damage, corrosive chemicals or dust. 

Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty. 

(2) In the case of electrical equipment that is new and unused at the workplace, the person conducting the business or undertaking: 

(a) is not required to comply with subregulation (1); and (b) must ensure that the equipment is inspected for obvious damage before being used. 

Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty. 

Note 

However, electrical equipment that is unsafe must not be used (see regulation 149). 

(3) The person must ensure that a record of any testing carried out under subregulation (1) is kept until the electrical equipment is: 

(a) next tested; or (b) permanently removed from the workplace or disposed of. 


Maximum penalty: tier I monetary penalty. 

(4) The record of testing: 

(a) must specify the following: (i) the name of the person who carried out the testing; (ii) the date of the testing; (iii) the outcome of the testing; (iv) the date on which the next testing must be carried out; and   
(b) may be in the form of a tag attached to the electrical equipment tested.

matches.target_title: 161 How the work is to be carried out
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical work on energised electrical equipment is carried out: 

(a) by a competent person who has tools, testing equipment and personal protective equipment that: 

(i) are suitable for the work; and (ii) have been properly tested; and (iii) are maintained in good working order; and 

(b) in accordance with a safe work method statement prepared for the work; and   
(c) subject to subregulation (5), with a safety observer present who has the competence and qualifications specified in subregulation (4). 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) The person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure, so far as is reasonably practicable, that the person who carries out the electrical work uses the tools, testing equipment and personal protective equipment properly. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(3) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(b), the safe work method statement must: 

(a) identify the electrical work; and   
(b) specify hazards associated with that electrical work and risks associated with those hazards; and   
(c) describe the measures to be implemented to control the risks; and   
(d) describe how the risk control measures are to be implemented, monitored and reviewed. 

(4) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(c): 

(a) the safety observer must be competent: 

(b) the safety observer must have been assessed in the previous 12 months as competent to rescue and resuscitate a person. 

(5) A safety observer is not required if: 

(a) the work consists only of testing; and   
(b) the person conducting the business or undertaking has conducted a risk assessment under regulation 158(1)(a) that shows that there is no serious risk associated with the proposed work.

matches.target_title: 157 Electrical work on energised electrical equipment—when permitted
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical work on energised electrical equipment is not carried out unless: 

(a) it is necessary in the interests of health and safety that the electrical work is carried out on the equipment while the equipment is energised; or Example 

It may be necessary that life-saving equipment remain energised and operating while electrical work is carried out on the equipment.   
(b) it is necessary that the electrical equipment to be worked on is energised in order for the work to be carried out properly; or   
(c) it is necessary for the purposes of testing required under regulation 155; or   
(d) there is no reasonable alternative means of carrying out the work. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) The electrical work that may be carried out under subregulation (1)(a), (b) and (d) may include testing of the energised electrical equipment.

compliance.reasoning: The provision prohibits carrying out electrical work on energised equipment except in limited circumstances (necessity for safety, necessity for proper work, required testing, or no reasonable alternative). The policy addresses working on de-energised equipment (isolation, lockout/tagout, and test for dead) and does not state that energised electrical work is prohibited by default, nor does it define/limit the exceptions under which energised work may occur. It also does not require a decision process to confirm that one of the permitted exceptions applies before any energised work is undertaken. Therefore, the policy does not demonstrate controls to ensure compliance with the prohibition/exception framework in the provision.
compliance.remediation: Amend the policy to explicitly prohibit electrical work on energised equipment unless one of the specified exceptions applies, and require documented justification/authorization (e.g., risk assessment/permit) confirming: (1) which exception applies (a–d), (2) why de-energising is not reasonably practicable, and (3) what controls will be used when energised work/testing is permitted.

matches.target_title: 154 Electrical work on energised electrical equipment—prohibited
matches.target_text:
Subject to this Division, a person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical work is not carried out on electrical equipment while the equipment is energised. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning: The provision requires the PCBU to ensure electrical work is not carried out while equipment is energised. The policy establishes controls to achieve de-energised work (isolation at supply source, lockout/tagout, and testing to confirm de-energisation before commencing work), which functionally ensures work is performed only when equipment is not energised.

matches.target_title: 211 Emergency stops
matches.target_text:
(1) If plant at a workplace is designed to be operated or attended by more than 1 person and more than 1 emergency stop control is fitted, the person with management or control of plant at the workplace must ensure that the multiple emergency stop controls are of the "stop and lock-off" type so that the plant cannot be restarted after an emergency stop control has been used unless that emergency stop control is reset.

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

(2) If the design of plant at a workplace includes an emergency stop control, the person with management or control of the plant at the workplace must ensure that:

(a) the stop control is prominent, clearly and durably marked and immediately accessible to each operator of the plant; and   
(b) any handle, bar or push button associated with the stop control is coloured red; and   
(c) the stop control cannot be adversely affected by electrical or electronic circuit malfunction.

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

source_text: Isolation points must be fitted with control mechanisms that prevent the electrical equipment from being inadvertently re-energised. The control mechanism should require a deliberate action to engage or dis-engage the device. It must withstand conditions that could lead to the isolation failing. Examples of this include switches with a built-in lock, and lock out for switches, circuit breakers, fuses and safety lockout jaws ‘hasps’.

matches.target_title: 210 Operational controls
matches.target_text:
(1) The person with management or control of plant at a workplace must ensure that any operator's controls are:

(a) identified on the plant so as to indicate their nature and function and direction of operation; and   
(b) located so as to be readily and conveniently operated by each person using the plant; and   
(c) located or guarded to prevent unintentional activation; and 

(d) able to be locked into the "off" position to enable the disconnection of all motive power.

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

(2) If the need for plant to be operated during maintenance or cleaning cannot be eliminated, the person with management or control of the plant at a workplace must ensure that the operator's controls:

(a) permit operation of the plant while a person is undertaking the maintenance or cleaning of the plant; and 

(b) while the plant is being maintained or cleaned, either: 

(i) cannot be operated by any person other than the person who is carrying out the maintenance or cleaning of the plant; or 

(ii) if subparagraph (i) cannot be complied with because the plant must be operated by a person other than the person who is carrying out the maintenance or cleaning of the plant, cannot be operated except by a person authorised by the person with management or control of the plant for that purpose; and 

(c) will allow operation of the plant in such a way that any risk associated with the activities in relation to any person who is carrying out the maintenance or cleaning: 

(i) is eliminated so far as is reasonably practicable; or (ii) if it is not reasonably practicable to eliminate the risk, is minimised so far as is reasonably practicable.

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning: The POLICY requires isolation points to have control mechanisms that prevent inadvertent re-energisation, require deliberate action to engage/disengage, and be robust (e.g., lockable switches/lockout devices). The PROVISION requires operator controls to be guarded/located to prevent unintentional activation and to be able to be locked into the "off" position to enable disconnection of motive power. These requirements are functionally aligned: both aim to prevent unintended activation/energisation and require lockable controls for safe isolation. While the POLICY adds detail about deliberate action and withstanding adverse conditions, the PROVISION’s requirements for preventing unintentional activation and lockable-off controls substantively support the POLICY intent, and the POLICY’s examples (locks/hasps/lockout) meet the PROVISION’s lock-off expectation.

matches.target_title: 158 Preliminary steps
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure the following before electrical work on energised electrical equipment commences: 

(a) a risk assessment is conducted in relation to the proposed electrical work;   
(b) the area where the electrical work is to be carried out is clear of obstructions so as to allow for easy access and exit;   
(c) the point at which the electrical equipment can be disconnected or isolated from its electricity supply is: (i) clearly marked or labelled; and (ii) clear of obstructions so as to allow for easy access and exit by the worker who is to carry out the electrical work or any other competent person; and (iii) capable of being operated quickly;   
(d) the person authorises the electrical work after consulting with the person with management or control of the workplace. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(a), the risk assessment must be: 

(a) conducted by a competent person; and (b) recorded. 

(3) Subregulation (1)(c) does not apply to electrical work on electrical equipment if: 

(a) the work is to be carried out on the supply side of the main switch on the main switchboard for the equipment; and   
(b) the point at which the equipment can be disconnected from its electricity supply is not reasonably accessible from the work location.

compliance.reasoning: The POLICY addresses requirements for isolation points to have control mechanisms that prevent inadvertent re-energisation (i.e., deliberate action/lockout-type controls and robustness against failure). The PROVISION requires, before energised electrical work commences, that the isolation/disconnection point is clearly marked/labelled, unobstructed for access/egress, and capable of being operated quickly, plus risk assessment and authorisation steps. While both concern isolation points for electrical work, the POLICY does not cover the PROVISION’s specific pre-work obligations (risk assessment by a competent person and recorded; marking/labelling; keeping the isolation point clear of obstructions; ensuring it can be operated quickly; and authorisation after consultation). Therefore the POLICY is not sufficient to demonstrate compliance with the PROVISION.
compliance.remediation: Update the policy/procedure to explicitly require, before commencing energised electrical work: (1) a recorded risk assessment conducted by a competent person; (2) verification that the isolation/disconnection point is clearly marked/labelled; (3) confirmation the work area and the isolation point are free of obstructions to allow easy access and exit; (4) confirmation the isolation point is capable of being operated quickly; and (5) formal authorisation after consultation with the person with management/control of the workplace. Also include the stated exception conditions where applicable.

matches.target_title: 211 Emergency stops
matches.target_text:
(1) If plant at a workplace is designed to be operated or attended by more than 1 person and more than 1 emergency stop control is fitted, the person with management or control of plant at the workplace must ensure that the multiple emergency stop controls are of the "stop and lock-off" type so that the plant cannot be restarted after an emergency stop control has been used unless that emergency stop control is reset.

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

(2) If the design of plant at a workplace includes an emergency stop control, the person with management or control of the plant at the workplace must ensure that:

(a) the stop control is prominent, clearly and durably marked and immediately accessible to each operator of the plant; and   
(b) any handle, bar or push button associated with the stop control is coloured red; and   
(c) the stop control cannot be adversely affected by electrical or electronic circuit malfunction.

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning: The POLICY requires isolation points to have control mechanisms that prevent inadvertent re-energisation, require deliberate action to engage/disengage, and withstand conditions that could cause isolation failure (e.g., lockable switches/lockout devices). The PROVISION addresses emergency stop controls (including stop-and-lock-off behavior, prominence/marking, red color, and resilience to electrical/electronic malfunction). While both concern preventing unintended restart/energisation, the PROVISION is limited to emergency stop controls and does not establish requirements for isolation points or lockout/tagout-style isolation mechanisms. Therefore, the POLICY obligations are not fully met by the PROVISION text as provided.
compliance.remediation: Add/ensure requirements and controls for electrical isolation points (not just emergency stops), including lockable isolation devices or lockout provisions that require deliberate action and prevent inadvertent re-energisation, and specify robustness against conditions that could cause isolation failure (e.g., lockable isolators, lockout for breakers/fuses, hasps).

matches.target_title: 191 Emergency stop controls
matches.target_text:
(1) If plant is designed to be operated or attended by more than 1 person and more than 1 emergency stop control is fitted, the designer of the plant must ensure that the design provides for the multiple emergency stop controls to be of the "stop and lock-off" type so that the plant cannot be restarted after an emergency stop control has been used unless that emergency stop control is reset. Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) If the design of the plant includes an emergency stop control for the plant, the designer of the plant must ensure that the design provides: 

(a) for the stop control to be prominent, clearly and durably marked and immediately accessible to each operator of the plant; and   
(b) for any handle, bar or push button associated with the stop control to be coloured red; and   
(c) that the stop control cannot be adversely affected by electrical or electronic circuit malfunction. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning: The POLICY requires isolation points to have control mechanisms that prevent inadvertent re-energisation, require deliberate action to engage/disengage, and withstand conditions that could cause isolation failure (e.g., lockable switches/lockout devices). The PROVISION addresses emergency stop controls (including stop-and-lock-off behavior for multiple E-stops, reset requirement, prominence/marking/accessibility, red actuators, and immunity to electrical/electronic malfunction). While both concern preventing unintended energisation/restart, the PROVISION does not cover isolation points or lockout/tagout-style isolation mechanisms, nor does it require deliberate-action isolation controls or lockable isolation devices. Therefore, the POLICY obligations are not fully met by the PROVISION requirements.
compliance.remediation: Add/ensure design and procedural requirements for isolation points specifically: fit lockable isolation devices or equivalent lockout mechanisms (e.g., built-in lock, lockout for switches/circuit breakers/fuses, hasps), require deliberate action to operate, and specify robustness against foreseeable conditions that could defeat isolation (including electrical/electronic malfunction where relevant). Document these controls separately from emergency stop requirements.

matches.target_title: 155 Duty to determine whether equipment is energised
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that, before electrical work is carried out on electrical equipment, the equipment is tested by a competent person to determine whether or not it is energised. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

Note 

Regulation 157 allows electrical testing to be carried out on electrical equipment for the purposes of this regulation. Regulation 161 sets out how the testing is to be carried out. 

(2) The person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that: 

(a) each exposed part is treated as energised until it is isolated and determined not to be energised; and   
(b) each high-voltage exposed part is earthed after being deenergised. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning: The provision requires that before electrical work is carried out, equipment is tested by a competent person to determine whether it is energised, that exposed parts are treated as energised until isolated and confirmed de-energised, and that high-voltage exposed parts are earthed after de-energisation. The policy text focuses on isolation points having control mechanisms to prevent inadvertent re-energisation (e.g., lockable switches/lockout devices) and does not address mandatory pre-work testing by a competent person, the requirement to treat exposed parts as energised until proven otherwise, or the earthing requirement for high-voltage exposed parts. Therefore, the policy does not fully cover the obligations in the provision.
compliance.remediation: Update the policy to explicitly require: (1) testing of electrical equipment by a competent person before electrical work to confirm whether it is energised (including reference to approved testing methods/procedures); (2) a rule that all exposed parts are treated as energised until isolated and verified de-energised; and (3) earthing of high-voltage exposed parts after de-energisation. Incorporate these steps into the isolation/lockout procedure and training/verification requirements.

matches.target_title: 156 De-energised equipment must not be inadvertently re-energised
matches.target_text:
A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical equipment that has been de-energised to allow electrical work to be carried out on it is not inadvertently re-energised while the work is being carried out. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning: The provision requires ensuring de-energised electrical equipment is not inadvertently re-energised while electrical work is being carried out. The policy directly addresses this obligation by requiring isolation points to be fitted with control mechanisms that prevent inadvertent re-energisation, require deliberate action to engage/disengage, and withstand conditions that could cause isolation failure, with examples such as lockable switches and lockout devices. This is functionally aligned with the provision’s requirement and describes controls that would satisfy it.

matches.target_title: 190 Operational controls
matches.target_text:
(1) A designer of plant must ensure that the design provides for any operator's controls for the plant to be: 

(a) identified on the plant so as to indicate their nature and function and direction of operation; and   
(b) located so as to be readily and conveniently operated by each person using the plant; and   
(c) located or guarded to prevent unintentional activation; and   
(d) able to be locked into the "off" position to enable the disconnection of all motive power. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) If the need for plant to be operated during maintenance or cleaning cannot be eliminated, the designer of the plant must ensure that the design provides for operator's controls that: 

(a) permit operation of the plant while a person is undertaking the maintenance or cleaning of the plant; and   
(b) while the plant is being maintained or cleaned, cannot be operated by any person other than the person who is carrying out the maintenance or cleaning of the plant; and   
(c) will allow operation of the plant in such a way that any risk associated with the activities in relation to any person who is carrying out the maintenance or cleaning: (i) is eliminated so far as is reasonably practicable; or (ii) if it is not reasonably practicable to eliminate the risk, is minimised so far as is reasonably practicable.

compliance.reasoning: The POLICY requires isolation points to have control mechanisms that prevent inadvertent re-energisation, require deliberate action to engage/disengage, and be robust (e.g., lockable switches/lockout devices). The PROVISION requires operator controls to be guarded/located to prevent unintentional activation and to be able to be locked into the "off" position to disconnect motive power. These requirements are functionally aligned: lockable-off controls and prevention of unintentional activation support preventing inadvertent re-energisation and deliberate action. The POLICY’s examples (lockout/hasps) are consistent with the PROVISION’s lock-off requirement. The PROVISION also includes additional design obligations (identification, convenient location, and maintenance-operation exclusivity) that are not contradicted by the POLICY.

matches.target_title: 149 Unsafe electrical equipment
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must ensure that any unsafe electrical equipment at the workplace: 

(a) is disconnected (or isolated) from its electricity supply; and (b) once disconnected (or isolated): 

(i) is not reconnected until it is repaired or tested and found to be safe; or (ii) is replaced or permanently removed from use. 

Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty. 

(2) For the purposes of this regulation, electrical equipment or a component of electrical equipment is unsafe if there are reasonable grounds for believing it to be unsafe.

compliance.reasoning: The provision requires that unsafe electrical equipment be disconnected/isolated and, once isolated, not be reconnected until repaired/tested safe (or replaced/removed). The policy addresses isolation points having control mechanisms to prevent inadvertent re-energisation (e.g., lockable switches/lockout devices), which is functionally related to ensuring isolated equipment is not reconnected. However, the policy does not explicitly require that unsafe equipment be isolated when identified as unsafe, nor does it state that reconnection is prohibited until repair/testing confirms safety, or that equipment must be replaced/removed if not repaired/tested. Therefore it does not fully meet the provision’s requirements.
compliance.remediation: Amend the policy to explicitly require: (1) any electrical equipment reasonably believed to be unsafe must be immediately disconnected/isolated from supply; (2) once isolated, it must not be reconnected until it is repaired or tested and found safe; and (3) if not repaired/tested, it must be replaced or permanently removed from use. Include procedural controls (tag-out/lock-out, authorization to re-energize, recordkeeping) to enforce the prohibition on reconnection.

matches.target_title: 160 Contact with equipment being worked on
matches.target_text:
A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that, while electrical work is being carried out on energised electrical equipment, all persons are prevented from creating an electrical risk by inadvertently making contact with an exposed energised component of the equipment. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning: The provision requires the duty holder to ensure that, during electrical work on energised equipment, persons are prevented from creating electrical risk by inadvertently contacting exposed energised components. The policy text addresses a different control: preventing inadvertent re-energisation via isolation point control mechanisms (lockable switches/lockout devices). While both relate to electrical safety during work, the policy does not specify measures to prevent inadvertent contact with exposed energised components (e.g., barriers, insulation, shrouding, exclusion zones, PPE, supervision). Therefore it does not demonstrate compliance with the provision’s specific requirement.
compliance.remediation: Add explicit requirements and controls for preventing inadvertent contact with exposed energised components during energised electrical work, such as: insulating/shrouding exposed live parts; installing barriers/guards and signage; establishing exclusion zones and access control; using appropriate PPE and insulated tools; implementing safe work method statements/permits for energised work; and ensuring competent supervision and training.

matches.target_title: 222 Industrial robots
matches.target_text:
(1) This regulation applies to a person with management or control of an industrial robot or other remotely or automatically energised plant at a workplace.

(2) The person must not direct or allow a worker to work in the immediate vicinity of the plant if it could start without warning and cause a hazard, unless suitable control measures are in place to control the risks to health and safety. Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.   
(3) If the remote or automatic energising of the plant could lead to risks to health and safety, the person must ensure that access to the area in the immediate vicinity of the plant is controlled at all times: (a) by isolating the area; or (b) by: (i) providing interlocked guards; or (ii) if a risk remains, providing presence-sensing devices; or (iii) if a risk then remains, providing permit to work systems.

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning: The POLICY requires isolation points to have control mechanisms that prevent inadvertent re-energisation (i.e., deliberate-action controls and lockout/secure mechanisms). The PROVISION requires suitable control measures where plant could start without warning and, where remote/automatic energising creates risk, requires controlled access via isolating the area or interlocked guards/presence-sensing/permit-to-work. While both address preventing unexpected energisation and controlling risk, the PROVISION does not explicitly require lockable isolation points or deliberate-action anti-re-energisation mechanisms at isolation points; it focuses on area access control and guarding systems. Therefore, the PROVISION does not fully meet the specific isolation-point control requirements in the POLICY.
compliance.remediation: Add explicit requirements that isolation points for remotely/automatically energised plant are fitted with lockable, deliberate-action control mechanisms (e.g., lockable isolators, lockout devices/hasps for switches, breakers, fuses) that withstand foreseeable conditions and prevent inadvertent re-energisation, and integrate these into the control measures/permit-to-work and access control arrangements.

source_text:
To meet the legislative requirements the following must be considered and addressed in an Activity Risk Assessment prior to carrying out the work:

a. The area where the electrical work is carried out is clear of obstructions so far as to allow for ease of access and exit   
b. The point at which the electrical equipment can be disconnected or isolated from its supply is: Clearly marked and labelled, and Cleared of obstructions so as to allow easy access and exit by the worker who is to carry out the electrical work or any other competent person; and – Capable of being operated quickly c. The person authorises the electrical work after consulting with the person with management or control of the workplace.

matches.target_title: 158 Preliminary steps
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure the following before electrical work on energised electrical equipment commences: 

(a) a risk assessment is conducted in relation to the proposed electrical work;   
(b) the area where the electrical work is to be carried out is clear of obstructions so as to allow for easy access and exit;   
(c) the point at which the electrical equipment can be disconnected or isolated from its electricity supply is: (i) clearly marked or labelled; and (ii) clear of obstructions so as to allow for easy access and exit by the worker who is to carry out the electrical work or any other competent person; and (iii) capable of being operated quickly;   
(d) the person authorises the electrical work after consulting with the person with management or control of the workplace. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(a), the risk assessment must be: 

(a) conducted by a competent person; and (b) recorded. 

(3) Subregulation (1)(c) does not apply to electrical work on electrical equipment if: 

(a) the work is to be carried out on the supply side of the main switch on the main switchboard for the equipment; and   
(b) the point at which the equipment can be disconnected from its electricity supply is not reasonably accessible from the work location.

compliance.reasoning: The POLICY text is clearly intended to address the same preconditions for energised electrical work as the PROVISION (risk assessment, clear work area, isolation point marking/access/quick operation, and authorisation after consultation). However, it does not fully meet the PROVISION because it omits key mandatory elements: (1) it does not explicitly require that the risk assessment be conducted (it only says items must be considered/addressed in an Activity Risk Assessment), (2) it does not require the risk assessment to be conducted by a competent person, and (3) it does not require the risk assessment to be recorded. The POLICY also does not reflect the conditional exception in subregulation (3), though omission of an exception is not necessarily non-compliance; the primary gap is the missing competence and recording requirements for the risk assessment.
compliance.remediation: Amend the POLICY to explicitly require that, before energised electrical work commences: (a) a risk assessment is conducted; (b) the risk assessment is conducted by a competent person; and (c) the risk assessment is recorded. Optionally add guidance reflecting the subregulation (3) exception regarding the supply side of the main switch where the isolation point is not reasonably accessible.

matches.target_title: 161 How the work is to be carried out
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical work on energised electrical equipment is carried out: 

(a) by a competent person who has tools, testing equipment and personal protective equipment that: 

(i) are suitable for the work; and (ii) have been properly tested; and (iii) are maintained in good working order; and 

(b) in accordance with a safe work method statement prepared for the work; and   
(c) subject to subregulation (5), with a safety observer present who has the competence and qualifications specified in subregulation (4). 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) The person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure, so far as is reasonably practicable, that the person who carries out the electrical work uses the tools, testing equipment and personal protective equipment properly. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(3) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(b), the safe work method statement must: 

(a) identify the electrical work; and   
(b) specify hazards associated with that electrical work and risks associated with those hazards; and   
(c) describe the measures to be implemented to control the risks; and   
(d) describe how the risk control measures are to be implemented, monitored and reviewed. 

(4) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(c): 

(a) the safety observer must be competent: 

(b) the safety observer must have been assessed in the previous 12 months as competent to rescue and resuscitate a person. 

(5) A safety observer is not required if: 

(a) the work consists only of testing; and   
(b) the person conducting the business or undertaking has conducted a risk assessment under regulation 158(1)(a) that shows that there is no serious risk associated with the proposed work.

compliance.reasoning: The POLICY sets requirements to be addressed in an Activity Risk Assessment before electrical work (clear access/egress, clearly marked and accessible isolation/disconnection point capable of quick operation, and authorisation after consultation). The PROVISION imposes additional mandatory controls specifically for electrical work on energised electrical equipment: it must be done by a competent person with suitable/tested/maintained tools and PPE; in accordance with a safe work method statement (SWMS) containing specified elements; and with a competent safety observer (with recent rescue/resuscitation competency) unless a testing-only exception applies supported by a risk assessment. The POLICY does not address competency requirements, tools/testing equipment/PPE suitability and maintenance, SWMS preparation and required contents, safety observer requirements, or ensuring proper use of tools/PPE. Therefore it does not fully meet the obligations in the PROVISION.
compliance.remediation: Update the policy/procedure for energised electrical work to explicitly require: (1) work only by a competent person; (2) use of suitable tools/testing equipment/PPE that are properly tested and maintained; (3) a SWMS for the work that identifies the work, hazards/risks, control measures, and how controls are implemented/monitored/reviewed; (4) a competent safety observer present, assessed within the last 12 months as competent in rescue and resuscitation, unless the work is testing-only and a documented risk assessment demonstrates no serious risk; and (5) processes to ensure workers properly use the tools/testing equipment/PPE.

matches.target_title: 147 Risk management
matches.target_text:
A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must manage risks to health and safety associated with electrical risks at the workplace, in accordance with Part 3.1. 

Example 

Electrical risks associated with the design, construction, installation, protection, maintenance and testing of electrical equipment and electrical installations at a workplace. 

Note 

WHS Act—section 19 (see regulation 9).

compliance.reasoning: The provision imposes a broad duty on a PCBU to manage risks to health and safety associated with electrical risks at the workplace in accordance with Part 3.1 (risk management). The policy text addresses some specific pre-work controls within an Activity Risk Assessment (clear access/egress, accessible/labelled isolation point, and authorization after consultation). However, it does not demonstrate the full Part 3.1 risk management requirements (e.g., systematic hazard identification, risk assessment where required, implementation of controls using the hierarchy of control, and ongoing review/monitoring of control effectiveness) for electrical risks across design, installation, maintenance, testing, etc. Therefore, while aligned in intent, it is incomplete against the broader obligation to manage electrical risks in accordance with Part 3.1.
compliance.remediation: Expand the policy/ARA requirements to explicitly align with Part 3.1 by requiring: (1) identification of electrical hazards/risks for the task and workplace; (2) assessment of risks (where required) and selection of controls using the hierarchy of control (e.g., de-energisation/lockout-tagout, RCDs, insulation, exclusion zones, competent persons, PPE); (3) implementation details (procedures, training/competency, supervision, permits/authorisations); and (4) review and monitoring of controls (including after incidents/changes) and recordkeeping. Ensure the scope covers electrical risks associated with design, installation, maintenance and testing, not only access/isolation/authorisation.

source_title: 7.2 Application to undertake energised electrical work
source_text:
To carry out energised electrical work, electrical workers must complete an Application to undertake energised electrical work (CSIRO Electrical Safety Procedure Appendix 4).

A completed application must be endorsed by the electrical workers line manager and then submitted to the applicable Group Leader and BU Director for consideration and approval.

The purpose of the application form is to provide senior management with visibility of what if any energised electrical work is being conducted within a business unit and to ensure that the work:

 will be conducted by a competent person,   
 is justified and meets the energised electrical work exemptions under s157 of the WHS Regulations, and   
 will be conducted in a safe manner.

What to consider when completing an application?

When completing the application consider the following:

a. How do you plan on carrying out the electrical work – i.e. is it de-energised or energised? If energised, do you have an appropriate electrical licence? An application to undertake energised electrical work need to be completed to obtain permission to work energised.   
b. Does your training/qualifications/licences/experience address the competencies required for the electrical tasks you intend to carry out? – copies of qualifications/training/licences need to be attached to the verification form.   
c. Have you completed Activity Risk Assessments and SWIs, for the electrical work you intend to carry out – copies of the risk assessment/s need to be attached to the application.   
d. Does the risk assessment address all the considerations documented in Part 3 of the application form?   
e. Do you have a current and approved Verification of electrical competency form? Does it cover the electrical work listed in the application? Do you require an appropriate electrical licence to conduct the work – copy of the approved verification form needs to be attached to the application.   
f. Are there any unexpected events such as equipment failures, that may require you to perform non-routine energised electrical work? – make sure your application covers all routine and potential non-routine work to avoid having to apply for approval at short notice.

(d) the person authorises the electrical work after consulting with the person with management or control of the workplace. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(a), the risk assessment must be: 

(a) conducted by a competent person; and (b) recorded. 

(3) Subregulation (1)(c) does not apply to electrical work on electrical equipment if: 

(a) the work is to be carried out on the supply side of the main switch on the main switchboard for the equipment; and   
(b) the point at which the equipment can be disconnected from its electricity supply is not reasonably accessible from the work location.

source_text:
To ensure that energised electrical work is carried out safely and risk minimised to yourself and others, the work must be performed:

a. By a competent person who has the tools, testing equipment and PPE that are suitable for the work, have been properly tested and are maintained in good working order;   
b. In accordance with a Safe Work Instruction prepared for the work; and   
c. Subject to the exception explained below – with a safety observer present who is competent: To implement control measures in an emergency, and – To rescue and resuscitate the worker who is carrying out the work if necessary, and Has been assessed in the previous 12 months as competent to rescue and resuscitate a person.

A safety observer is not required if the work consists only of testing and the risk assessment shows there is no serious risk associated with the proposed work.

matches.target_title: 161 How the work is to be carried out
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical work on energised electrical equipment is carried out: 

(a) by a competent person who has tools, testing equipment and personal protective equipment that: 

(i) are suitable for the work; and (ii) have been properly tested; and (iii) are maintained in good working order; and 

(b) in accordance with a safe work method statement prepared for the work; and   
(c) subject to subregulation (5), with a safety observer present who has the competence and qualifications specified in subregulation (4). 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) The person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure, so far as is reasonably practicable, that the person who carries out the electrical work uses the tools, testing equipment and personal protective equipment properly. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(3) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(b), the safe work method statement must: 

(a) identify the electrical work; and   
(b) specify hazards associated with that electrical work and risks associated with those hazards; and   
(c) describe the measures to be implemented to control the risks; and   
(d) describe how the risk control measures are to be implemented, monitored and reviewed. 

(4) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(c): 

(a) the safety observer must be competent: 

(b) the safety observer must have been assessed in the previous 12 months as competent to rescue and resuscitate a person. 

(5) A safety observer is not required if: 

(a) the work consists only of testing; and   
(b) the person conducting the business or undertaking has conducted a risk assessment under regulation 158(1)(a) that shows that there is no serious risk associated with the proposed work.

compliance.reasoning: The POLICY is clearly intended to implement the same obligations as the PROVISION for energised electrical work: competent person with suitable/tested/maintained tools/PPE; work performed under a documented safe work document; safety observer requirements with a testing-only/no-serious-risk exception. However, the POLICY does not fully meet the PROVISION because: (1) it requires a “Safe Work Instruction” rather than a “safe work method statement” and does not include the mandatory SWMS content elements (identify work; specify hazards/risks; describe control measures; describe implementation/monitoring/review). (2) It omits the duty to ensure, so far as reasonably practicable, that the worker uses tools/testing equipment/PPE properly. (3) For the safety observer, the POLICY states the observer is competent to implement emergency controls and rescue/resuscitate and has been assessed within 12 months, but it does not explicitly require the observer to have the “competence and qualifications specified” (i.e., it does not explicitly cover any qualification requirement beyond competence/assessment). (4) The exception for no safety observer references a “risk assessment” but does not specify it must be conducted by the PCBU under the relevant regulation; this is a minor alignment gap.
compliance.remediation: Update the POLICY to: (1) require a Safe Work Method Statement (or ensure the “Safe Work Instruction” is explicitly defined to meet SWMS requirements) and include the required SWMS elements: identification of the work, hazards/risks, control measures, and how controls are implemented/monitored/reviewed; (2) add an explicit requirement that, so far as reasonably practicable, the PCBU ensures the worker uses tools/testing equipment/PPE properly; (3) explicitly state the safety observer must meet the competence and any required qualifications (and retain the 12-month rescue/resuscitation assessment requirement); (4) align the testing-only exception wording to require the PCBU-conducted risk assessment demonstrating no serious risk.

matches.target_title: 158 Preliminary steps
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure the following before electrical work on energised electrical equipment commences: 

(a) a risk assessment is conducted in relation to the proposed electrical work;   
(b) the area where the electrical work is to be carried out is clear of obstructions so as to allow for easy access and exit;   
(c) the point at which the electrical equipment can be disconnected or isolated from its electricity supply is: (i) clearly marked or labelled; and (ii) clear of obstructions so as to allow for easy access and exit by the worker who is to carry out the electrical work or any other competent person; and (iii) capable of being operated quickly;   
(d) the person authorises the electrical work after consulting with the person with management or control of the workplace. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(a), the risk assessment must be: 

(a) conducted by a competent person; and (b) recorded. 

(3) Subregulation (1)(c) does not apply to electrical work on electrical equipment if: 

(a) the work is to be carried out on the supply side of the main switch on the main switchboard for the equipment; and   
(b) the point at which the equipment can be disconnected from its electricity supply is not reasonably accessible from the work location.

compliance.reasoning: The POLICY governs how energised electrical work must be performed (competent person, SWI, safety observer with an exception for testing). The PROVISION imposes additional pre-commencement duties on the PCBU for energised electrical work: (1)(a) conduct a risk assessment; (2) ensure it is conducted by a competent person and recorded; (1)(b) keep the work area clear for access/exit; (1)(c) ensure the isolation/disconnection point is labelled, unobstructed, and quickly operable (with a limited exception in (3)); and (1)(d) authorise the work after consulting with the person with management/control of the workplace. The POLICY only partially aligns by referencing a risk assessment for the safety observer exception, but it does not require that a risk assessment be conducted before work commences, does not require it to be recorded, and does not address the access/egress and isolation-point labelling/clearance/quick operation requirements, nor the authorisation-after-consultation requirement. Therefore, the POLICY does not fully satisfy the PROVISION.
compliance.remediation: Update the POLICY (or add a linked procedure) to require, before energised electrical work starts: (1) a documented risk assessment conducted by a competent person; (2) verification that the work area is clear of obstructions for safe access/exit; (3) verification that the isolation/disconnection point is clearly labelled/marked, unobstructed, and can be operated quickly (and document when the supply-side exception applies); and (4) formal authorisation of the work after consultation with the person with management/control of the workplace, with records retained.

matches.target_title: 155 Duty to determine whether equipment is energised
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that, before electrical work is carried out on electrical equipment, the equipment is tested by a competent person to determine whether or not it is energised. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

Note 

Regulation 157 allows electrical testing to be carried out on electrical equipment for the purposes of this regulation. Regulation 161 sets out how the testing is to be carried out. 

(2) The person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that: 

(a) each exposed part is treated as energised until it is isolated and determined not to be energised; and   
(b) each high-voltage exposed part is earthed after being deenergised. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning: The texts are functionally related because both govern safety controls around electrical work on equipment that may be energised. The provision requires that before electrical work is carried out, the equipment is tested by a competent person to determine whether it is energised, and that exposed parts are treated as energised until isolated and proven de-energised, with additional earthing requirements for high-voltage exposed parts after de-energising. The policy focuses on conditions for performing energised electrical work (competent person, SWI, safety observer exception for testing) but does not explicitly require pre-work testing to determine energisation status, does not state that exposed parts must be treated as energised until isolated and confirmed de-energised, and does not address earthing of high-voltage exposed parts after de-energising. Therefore, the policy does not fully meet the provision’s specific obligations.
compliance.remediation: Update the policy/SWI requirements to explicitly: (1) require that before any electrical work on electrical equipment, a competent person tests the equipment to determine whether it is energised; (2) require all exposed parts to be treated as energised until isolated and confirmed not energised; and (3) require each high-voltage exposed part to be earthed after being de-energised. Reference or align with the applicable testing method requirements (e.g., procedures consistent with the relevant testing regulations/standards).

matches.target_title: 157 Electrical work on energised electrical equipment—when permitted
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical work on energised electrical equipment is not carried out unless: 

(a) it is necessary in the interests of health and safety that the electrical work is carried out on the equipment while the equipment is energised; or Example 

It may be necessary that life-saving equipment remain energised and operating while electrical work is carried out on the equipment.   
(b) it is necessary that the electrical equipment to be worked on is energised in order for the work to be carried out properly; or   
(c) it is necessary for the purposes of testing required under regulation 155; or   
(d) there is no reasonable alternative means of carrying out the work. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) The electrical work that may be carried out under subregulation (1)(a), (b) and (d) may include testing of the energised electrical equipment.

compliance.reasoning: The provision prohibits energised electrical work unless one of the necessity conditions in (1)(a)-(d) is met (health/safety necessity, necessary to do the work properly, necessary for testing under reg 155, or no reasonable alternative). The policy focuses on how to perform energised work safely (competent person, SWI, safety observer with a testing-only exception) but does not state that energised work is only permitted when one of the provision’s necessity criteria is satisfied, nor does it require documenting/justifying that criterion before proceeding. Therefore, the policy does not fully implement the threshold prohibition/permission conditions required by the provision.
compliance.remediation: Amend the policy to explicitly prohibit energised electrical work unless one of the conditions in (1)(a)-(d) applies, and require a documented pre-work assessment/authorization that records which condition applies (including consideration of de-energising and why no reasonable alternative exists). Ensure the testing exception aligns with (1)(c) (testing required under reg 155) or otherwise requires justification under (1)(a), (b), or (d).

matches.target_title: 150 Inspection and testing of electrical equipment
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must ensure that electrical equipment is regularly inspected and tested by a competent person if the electrical equipment is: 

(a) supplied with electricity through an electrical socket outlet; and   
(b) used in an environment in which the normal use of electrical equipment exposes the equipment to operating conditions that are likely to result in damage to the equipment or a reduction in its expected life span, including conditions that involve exposure to moisture, heat, vibration, mechanical damage, corrosive chemicals or dust. 

Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty. 

(2) In the case of electrical equipment that is new and unused at the workplace, the person conducting the business or undertaking: 

(a) is not required to comply with subregulation (1); and (b) must ensure that the equipment is inspected for obvious damage before being used. 

Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty. 

Note 

However, electrical equipment that is unsafe must not be used (see regulation 149). 

(3) The person must ensure that a record of any testing carried out under subregulation (1) is kept until the electrical equipment is: 

(a) next tested; or (b) permanently removed from the workplace or disposed of. 


Maximum penalty: tier I monetary penalty. 

(4) The record of testing: 

(a) must specify the following: (i) the name of the person who carried out the testing; (ii) the date of the testing; (iii) the outcome of the testing; (iv) the date on which the next testing must be carried out; and   
(b) may be in the form of a tag attached to the electrical equipment tested.

matches.target_title: 240 Maintenance, inspection and testing of amusement devices and passenger ropeways
matches.target_text:
(1) The person with management or control of an amusement device or passenger ropeway at a workplace must ensure that the maintenance, inspection and, if necessary, testing of the device or ropeway is carried out:

(a) by a competent person; and (b) in accordance with:

(i) the recommendations of the designer or manufacturer or designer and manufacturer; or

(ii) if a maintenance manual for the device or ropeway has been prepared by a competent person, the requirements of the maintenance manual.

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

(2) A person is not a competent person to carry out a detailed inspection of an amusement device or passenger ropeway that includes an electrical installation unless the person is qualified, or is assisted by a person who is qualified, to inspect electrical installations.

compliance.reasoning: The provision requires that maintenance/inspection/testing of an amusement device or passenger ropeway be carried out by a competent person and in accordance with the designer/manufacturer recommendations or a competent-person-prepared maintenance manual. It also requires that any detailed inspection that includes an electrical installation be performed by a qualified electrical-installation inspector (or assisted by one). The policy text addresses safe performance of energised electrical work (competency, SWI, safety observer/rescue capability) but does not require adherence to manufacturer/designer recommendations or a maintenance manual for the device/ropeway, and it does not require electrical-installation inspection qualifications/assistance for detailed inspections. Therefore, the policy does not fully satisfy the provision’s obligations.
compliance.remediation: Update the policy/procedure for amusement devices/passenger ropeways to: (1) mandate maintenance/inspection/testing be performed in accordance with designer/manufacturer recommendations or an approved maintenance manual prepared by a competent person; and (2) require that any detailed inspection involving an electrical installation be conducted by a qualified electrical-installation inspector or with documented assistance from such a qualified person. Ensure role/qualification criteria and records are defined.

matches.target_title: 160 Contact with equipment being worked on
matches.target_text:
A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that, while electrical work is being carried out on energised electrical equipment, all persons are prevented from creating an electrical risk by inadvertently making contact with an exposed energised component of the equipment. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning: The provision requires the PCBU to ensure that, during energised electrical work, all persons are prevented from creating an electrical risk by inadvertently contacting an exposed energised component. The policy focuses on who may perform energised work (competent person with suitable tools/PPE), requiring a Safe Work Instruction, and generally requiring a competent safety observer (with an exception for certain testing). However, it does not explicitly require controls to prevent all persons (including bystanders/other workers) from inadvertent contact with exposed energised components (e.g., barriers, exclusion zones, insulation/covering, access control, signage). The presence of a safety observer and PPE may reduce risk but does not, on its face, ensure prevention of inadvertent contact by all persons as required by the provision.
compliance.remediation: Amend the policy/SWI requirements to explicitly mandate controls that prevent inadvertent contact with exposed energised components by any person, such as: establishing and enforcing exclusion zones; physical barriers/guards and insulating covers; lockable access control to the work area; signage and spotter duties focused on keeping others out; and verification steps (pre-job and during work) that exposed energised parts are guarded and the area is controlled.

matches.target_title: 147 Risk management
matches.target_text:
A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must manage risks to health and safety associated with electrical risks at the workplace, in accordance with Part 3.1. 

Example 

Electrical risks associated with the design, construction, installation, protection, maintenance and testing of electrical equipment and electrical installations at a workplace. 

Note 

WHS Act—section 19 (see regulation 9).

compliance.reasoning: The provision requires the PCBU to manage risks to health and safety associated with electrical risks at the workplace in accordance with Part 3.1 (general risk management). The policy sets specific controls for energised electrical work (competent person with suitable/maintained tools and PPE, use of a Safe Work Instruction, and use of a competent safety observer with rescue/resuscitation capability unless testing-only with no serious risk per risk assessment). These measures constitute a structured approach to identifying and controlling electrical risks and align with the obligation to manage electrical risks under the WHS framework. The policy is more specific than the provision and does not conflict with it.

matches.target_title: 166 Duty of person conducting a business or undertaking
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must ensure, so far as is reasonably practicable, that no person, plant or thing at the workplace comes within an unsafe distance of an overhead or underground electric line. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) If it is not reasonably practicable to ensure the safe distance of a person, plant or thing from an overhead or underground electric line, the person conducting the business or undertaking at the workplace must ensure that:
(a) a risk assessment is conducted in relation to the proposed work; and 

(b) control measures implemented are consistent with: 

(i) the risk assessment; and 

(ii) if an electricity supply authority is responsible for the electric line, any requirements of the authority. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

Notes 

1 [Relevant electricity safety legislation] will also apply to the person conducting the business or undertaking.

matches.target_title: 213 Maintenance and inspection of plant
matches.target_text:
(1) The person with management or control of plant at a workplace must ensure that the maintenance, inspection and, if necessary, testing of the plant is carried out by a competent person. Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty.

(2) The maintenance, inspection and testing must be carried out:

(a) in accordance with the manufacturer's recommendations, if any; or   
(b) if there are no manufacturer's recommendations, in accordance with the recommendations of a competent person; or   
(c) in relation to inspection, if it is not reasonably practicable to comply with paragraph (a) or (b), annually.

matches.target_title: 299 Safe work method statement required for high risk construction work
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking that includes the carrying out of high risk construction work must, before high risk construction work commences, ensure that a safe work method statement for the proposed work: 

(a) is prepared; or (b) has already been prepared by another person. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) A safe work method statement must: 

(a) identify the work that is high risk construction work; and   
(b) specify hazards relating to the high risk construction work and risks to health and safety associated with those hazards; and   
(c) describe the measures to be implemented to control the risks; and   
(d) describe how the control measures are to be implemented, monitored and reviewed. 

(3) A safe work method statement must: 

(a) be prepared taking into account all relevant matters, including: 

(i) circumstances at the workplace that may affect the way in which the high risk construction work is carried out; and   
(ii) if the high risk construction work is carried out in connection with a construction project—the WHS management plan that has been prepared for the workplace; and 

(b) be set out and expressed in a way that is readily accessible and understandable to persons who use it.

compliance.reasoning: The POLICY is functionally related to the PROVISION because it establishes an internal equivalent (SWI) to the WHS requirement for a SWMS for high-risk work, and sets content/review expectations for energised electrical work. However, it does not fully meet the PROVISION’s requirements for a SWMS for high risk construction work: (1) it does not explicitly require the SWI/SWMS to be in place before the high-risk work commences; (2) it does not explicitly require the statement to describe the risk control measures to be implemented, nor how those controls will be implemented, monitored and reviewed (beyond a general requirement that SWIs be regularly reviewed/revised); (3) it does not address preparation requirements considering workplace circumstances and (where applicable) alignment with a WHS management plan; and (4) it does not require the document to be readily accessible and understandable to persons who use it. Additionally, the POLICY scope is 'High-Risk Work' including energised electrical work, while the PROVISION is specifically triggered by 'high risk construction work'; the POLICY does not explicitly confirm coverage when the energised electrical work occurs as high risk construction work.
compliance.remediation: Amend the POLICY/SWI requirements to explicitly: (1) require an SWI/SWMS to be prepared/available before high-risk (construction) work commences; (2) include mandatory sections describing risk control measures and how they will be implemented, monitored, and reviewed; (3) require preparation to consider workplace-specific circumstances and, where applicable, the WHS management plan for the construction project; (4) require the SWI/SWMS to be set out in a form that is readily accessible and understandable to workers using it; and (5) clarify applicability when energised electrical work constitutes high risk construction work, ensuring SWMS-equivalent compliance in those scenarios.

matches.target_title: 161 How the work is to be carried out
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical work on energised electrical equipment is carried out: 

(a) by a competent person who has tools, testing equipment and personal protective equipment that: 

(i) are suitable for the work; and (ii) have been properly tested; and (iii) are maintained in good working order; and 

(b) in accordance with a safe work method statement prepared for the work; and   
(c) subject to subregulation (5), with a safety observer present who has the competence and qualifications specified in subregulation (4). 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) The person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure, so far as is reasonably practicable, that the person who carries out the electrical work uses the tools, testing equipment and personal protective equipment properly. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(3) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(b), the safe work method statement must: 

(a) identify the electrical work; and   
(b) specify hazards associated with that electrical work and risks associated with those hazards; and   
(c) describe the measures to be implemented to control the risks; and   
(d) describe how the risk control measures are to be implemented, monitored and reviewed. 

(4) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(c): 

(a) the safety observer must be competent: 

(b) the safety observer must have been assessed in the previous 12 months as competent to rescue and resuscitate a person. 

(5) A safety observer is not required if: 

(a) the work consists only of testing; and   
(b) the person conducting the business or undertaking has conducted a risk assessment under regulation 158(1)(a) that shows that there is no serious risk associated with the proposed work.

compliance.reasoning: The POLICY is intended to implement the PROVISION requirement that energised electrical work be carried out in accordance with a safe work method statement (SWMS), using CSIRO’s Safe Work Instruction (SWI) as the equivalent. The POLICY includes several SWMS-aligned content requirements (describe the work; specify hazards; describe health and safety requirements; include emergency steps; include authorisation/approval arrangements) and requires review/revision. However, the PROVISION imposes additional mandatory elements and conditions that are not fully addressed in the POLICY: (1) it requires energised electrical work be carried out by a competent person with suitable, tested, and maintained tools/testing equipment/PPE, and that their proper use is ensured so far as reasonably practicable—these requirements are not stated; (2) it requires a safety observer meeting specific competence and recent rescue/resuscitation assessment requirements, with a limited exception—this is not mentioned; (3) it requires the SWMS to describe risk control measures and how they will be implemented, monitored, and reviewed—while the POLICY requires SWIs be reviewed and revised, it does not explicitly require the SWI to document the control measures nor the implementation/monitoring/review method within the SWI. Therefore the POLICY is only partially aligned and does not fully satisfy the PROVISION.
compliance.remediation: Update the SWI/SWI procedure for energised electrical work to explicitly require: (1) confirmation that the work is performed by a competent person and that tools, testing equipment and PPE are suitable, properly tested, maintained, and used properly; (2) a safety observer for energised work, including required competence and evidence of rescue/resuscitation competency assessment within the last 12 months, plus the testing-only/risk-assessment exception criteria; (3) SWI content to include the specific risk control measures and a documented method for how controls will be implemented, monitored, and reviewed (not just that the SWI itself is periodically reviewed).

matches.target_title: 158 Preliminary steps
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure the following before electrical work on energised electrical equipment commences: 

(a) a risk assessment is conducted in relation to the proposed electrical work;   
(b) the area where the electrical work is to be carried out is clear of obstructions so as to allow for easy access and exit;   
(c) the point at which the electrical equipment can be disconnected or isolated from its electricity supply is: (i) clearly marked or labelled; and (ii) clear of obstructions so as to allow for easy access and exit by the worker who is to carry out the electrical work or any other competent person; and (iii) capable of being operated quickly;   
(d) the person authorises the electrical work after consulting with the person with management or control of the workplace. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(a), the risk assessment must be: 

(a) conducted by a competent person; and (b) recorded. 

(3) Subregulation (1)(c) does not apply to electrical work on electrical equipment if: 

(a) the work is to be carried out on the supply side of the main switch on the main switchboard for the equipment; and   
(b) the point at which the equipment can be disconnected from its electricity supply is not reasonably accessible from the work location.

compliance.reasoning: The POLICY is functionally related to the PROVISION because it sets internal requirements (SWIs for energised electrical work) intended to support compliance with WHS obligations for energised electrical work, including risk assessment and authorisation. However, the POLICY does not fully cover several explicit pre-commencement requirements in the PROVISION. Specifically: (1) The PROVISION requires the work area to be clear of obstructions for easy access/exit (1)(b); the POLICY does not state this. (2) The PROVISION requires the isolation/disconnection point to be clearly marked/labelled, unobstructed for access/exit, and capable of being operated quickly (1)(c)(i)-(iii); the POLICY does not include these requirements. (3) The PROVISION requires authorisation of the electrical work after consulting with the person with management/control of the workplace (1)(d); the POLICY requires SWIs to describe approval/authorisation arrangements and who is approved, but does not explicitly require consultation with the person with management/control of the workplace prior to authorisation. (4) The PROVISION requires the risk assessment to be conducted by a competent person and recorded (2)(a)-(b); the POLICY references hazards identified in the risk assessment and CSIRO Risk Management Procedure, but does not explicitly require competence of the assessor or that the risk assessment be recorded. Therefore, the POLICY is not sufficient on its face to demonstrate compliance with all elements of the PROVISION.
