compliance.remediation: Update the SWI requirements (and/or explicitly cross-reference mandatory steps in the CSIRO Risk Management Procedure) to include: (1) a pre-start check that the work area is clear of obstructions and provides easy access/egress; (2) requirements that the isolation/disconnection point is clearly marked/labelled, unobstructed, and operable quickly; (3) an explicit requirement that authorisation occurs only after consultation with the person with management/control of the workplace; (4) an explicit requirement that the energised electrical work risk assessment is conducted by a competent person and is recorded (with record retention/location specified). Ensure SWI templates include fields/checklists for these items and require verification before work commences.

matches.target_title: 147 Risk management
matches.target_text:
A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must manage risks to health and safety associated with electrical risks at the workplace, in accordance with Part 3.1. 

Example 

Electrical risks associated with the design, construction, installation, protection, maintenance and testing of electrical equipment and electrical installations at a workplace. 

Note 

WHS Act—section 19 (see regulation 9).

compliance.reasoning: The provision requires the PCBU to manage risks to health and safety associated with electrical risks at the workplace in accordance with Part 3.1 (general risk management duties). The policy focuses on one control mechanism for a subset of electrical risk activities—energised electrical work—by requiring a Safe Work Instruction (SWI) with specified content and review requirements. While this supports risk management, it does not explicitly require managing electrical risks across the broader lifecycle examples in the provision (design, construction, installation, protection, maintenance, testing) nor does it explicitly align the risk management process to Part 3.1 (e.g., identify hazards, assess risks if required, implement controls using the hierarchy, and review controls) for all electrical risks. Therefore, the policy only partially addresses the provision and is not fully compliant as a complete implementation of the duty.
compliance.remediation: Expand the policy to explicitly require electrical risk management in accordance with WHS Regulations Part 3.1 for all electrical risks at the workplace (not only energised electrical work/high-risk work). Include clear requirements to: identify electrical hazards; assess risks where required; implement and maintain controls using the hierarchy of control; and review control measures. Ensure coverage of electrical risks across design, construction, installation, protection, maintenance and testing activities, and define responsibilities/accountabilities for the PCBU and workers.

matches.target_title: 162 Record keeping
matches.target_text:
(1) This regulation applies if a person conducting a business or undertaking prepares: 

(a) a risk assessment under regulation 158; or (b) a safe work method statement under regulation 161. 

(2) Subject to subregulation (3), the person must keep: 

(a) a copy of the risk assessment until at least 28 days after the work to which it relates is completed; and 

(b) a copy of the safe work method statement until the work to which it relates is completed. 

Maximum penalty: tier I monetary penalty. 

(3) If a notifiable incident occurs in connection with the work to which the assessment or statement relates, the person must keep the assessment or statement (as applicable) for at least 2 years after the incident occurs. Maximum penalty: tier I monetary penalty.   
(4) The person must ensure that, for the period for which the assessment or statement must be kept under this regulation, a copy is readily accessible to any worker engaged by the person to carry out electrical work to which the assessment or statement relates. Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty.   
(5) The person must ensure that, for the period for which the assessment or statement must be kept under this regulation, a copy is available for inspection under the Act. Maximum penalty: tier I monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning: The POLICY requires SWIs (CSIRO equivalent of SWMS) for energised electrical work and specifies SWI content and review requirements. The PROVISION imposes recordkeeping and accessibility obligations for electrical-work risk assessments and SWMS: retain copies for specified periods, keep for 2 years after a notifiable incident, ensure copies are readily accessible to workers during the retention period, and available for inspection. The POLICY does not address keeping/retention timeframes, post-incident retention, worker accessibility, or inspection availability for SWIs/SWMS or risk assessments. Therefore, while the POLICY is related (it governs SWI/SWMS use), it does not meet the PROVISION’s recordkeeping/accessibility requirements.
compliance.remediation: Update the POLICY (or add a linked procedure) to require: (1) retention of electrical-work risk assessments for at least 28 days after completion; (2) retention of SWMS/SWI until the work is completed; (3) retention of the relevant assessment/statement for at least 2 years after any notifiable incident connected to the work; (4) ensuring copies are readily accessible to workers performing the electrical work for the entire retention period; and (5) ensuring copies are available for inspection under the Act for the entire retention period. Define where records are stored, who is responsible, and how accessibility/inspection will be provided.

matches.target_title: 300 Compliance with safe work method statement
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking that includes the carrying out of high risk construction work must put in place arrangements for ensuring that high risk construction work is carried out in accordance with the safe work method statement for the work. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) If high risk construction work is not carried out in accordance with the safe work method statement for the work, the person must ensure that the work: 

(a) is stopped immediately or as soon as it is safe to do so; and (b) resumed only in accordance with the statement. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning: The provision requires a PCBU to have arrangements ensuring high risk construction work is carried out in accordance with the applicable SWMS, and to ensure work is stopped immediately (or as soon as safe) if it is not being carried out in accordance with the SWMS, and only resumed in accordance with it. The policy establishes that energised electrical work (treated as high-risk work) requires a CSIRO Safe Work Instruction (SWI) equivalent to a SWMS and specifies required SWI content and review requirements. However, it does not explicitly require (1) arrangements to ensure the work is carried out in accordance with the SWI/SWMS, nor (2) an explicit stop-work and resume-only-when-compliant requirement when work deviates from the SWI/SWMS. Therefore, it only partially addresses the provision and is not fully compliant.
compliance.remediation: Amend the policy/SWI requirements to explicitly: (1) require documented arrangements (e.g., supervision, verification, pre-start checks, audits) to ensure energised electrical/high-risk construction work is performed in accordance with the SWI/SWMS; and (2) mandate that if work is not being performed in accordance with the SWI/SWMS it must be stopped immediately or as soon as it is safe, and only resumed once compliance with the SWI/SWMS is restored. Ensure these requirements are communicated, implemented, and periodically verified.

matches.target_title: 157 Electrical work on energised electrical equipment—when permitted
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical work on energised electrical equipment is not carried out unless: 

(a) it is necessary in the interests of health and safety that the electrical work is carried out on the equipment while the equipment is energised; or Example 

It may be necessary that life-saving equipment remain energised and operating while electrical work is carried out on the equipment.   
(b) it is necessary that the electrical equipment to be worked on is energised in order for the work to be carried out properly; or   
(c) it is necessary for the purposes of testing required under regulation 155; or   
(d) there is no reasonable alternative means of carrying out the work. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) The electrical work that may be carried out under subregulation (1)(a), (b) and (d) may include testing of the energised electrical equipment.

compliance.reasoning: The texts are functionally related because the POLICY sets internal requirements (SWI/SWMS) for conducting energised electrical work, which is a control mechanism relevant to the PROVISION’s restriction that energised electrical work must not be carried out unless specific necessity conditions are met. However, the POLICY does not state or require the key legal threshold from the PROVISION: that energised electrical work is prohibited unless one of the listed necessity criteria applies (health and safety necessity, required for proper work, testing under reg 155, or no reasonable alternative). The POLICY focuses on documenting how to do energised work safely (via SWI content and review) but does not require a decision/justification process to confirm that energised work is permitted only when de-energising is not reasonably practicable or when one of the other regulatory conditions applies. Therefore, it does not fully implement the PROVISION’s primary obligation to avoid energised work unless justified.
compliance.remediation: Amend the POLICY/SWI requirements to explicitly prohibit energised electrical work unless one of the regulatory necessity conditions is met, and require the SWI (or associated risk assessment/permit) to document: (1) which condition under (1)(a)-(d) applies, (2) why de-energising is not reasonably practicable/what alternatives were considered, and (3) any testing justification where applicable (including reference to reg 155 where relevant). Include an approval/authorisation step that verifies this justification before work proceeds.

matches.target_title: 155 Duty to determine whether equipment is energised
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that, before electrical work is carried out on electrical equipment, the equipment is tested by a competent person to determine whether or not it is energised. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

Note 

Regulation 157 allows electrical testing to be carried out on electrical equipment for the purposes of this regulation. Regulation 161 sets out how the testing is to be carried out. 

(2) The person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that: 

(a) each exposed part is treated as energised until it is isolated and determined not to be energised; and   
(b) each high-voltage exposed part is earthed after being deenergised. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning: The provision imposes specific pre-work and work-practice obligations for electrical work: (1) before electrical work is carried out, the equipment must be tested by a competent person to determine whether it is energised; and (2) exposed parts must be treated as energised until isolated and confirmed de-energised, and high-voltage exposed parts must be earthed after de-energisation. The policy text focuses on requiring a Safe Work Instruction (SWI) for energised electrical work and lists SWI content requirements (authorisation, task description, hazards, safety requirements, emergency steps, review). However, it does not explicitly require pre-work testing by a competent person to confirm energisation status, does not state that exposed parts must be treated as energised until isolation and confirmation, and does not require earthing of high-voltage exposed parts after de-energisation. Therefore, while the policy is functionally related (it governs safe methods for electrical work), it does not demonstrate coverage of the specific obligations in the provision.
compliance.remediation: Update the SWI requirements (and/or the energised electrical work SWI template) to explicitly include: (1) mandatory pre-work testing of electrical equipment by a competent person to determine energisation status (with reference to applicable testing method requirements); (2) a rule that all exposed parts are treated as energised until isolated and verified de-energised; and (3) a requirement that each high-voltage exposed part is earthed after being de-energised. Ensure these steps are embedded as mandatory procedural controls and are included in SWI review criteria.

matches.target_title: 146 Meaning of electrical work

compliance.reasoning: The texts are functionally related because the POLICY governs how energised electrical work (a subset of “electrical work”) must be controlled via an SWI/SWMS, while the PROVISION defines what constitutes “electrical work” and what is excluded. A compliance check is necessary to ensure the POLICY’s scope and requirements align with the regulatory definition. The POLICY is not compliant with the PROVISION because it does not define “electrical work” or acknowledge the exclusions listed in the PROVISION (e.g., plug-and-socket connection, non-electrical component work without electrical risk, replacing fuses/light bulbs, certain supervised assistance, etc.). By treating “energised electrical work” as “High-Risk Work” requiring an SWI without clarifying boundaries, the POLICY risks over-scoping or misclassifying tasks that the PROVISION explicitly excludes from “electrical work,” which can lead to inconsistent application and incorrect compliance determinations.
compliance.remediation: Update the POLICY to incorporate (or reference) the regulatory definition of “electrical work” and explicitly list or reference the exclusions. Clarify that the SWI requirement applies to energised electrical work that falls within the defined scope of “electrical work,” and provide guidance/examples to distinguish excluded activities (e.g., plug-in equipment, non-electrical component work without exposure to electrical risk, simple component replacement) from covered electrical work.

matches.target_title: 150 Inspection and testing of electrical equipment
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must ensure that electrical equipment is regularly inspected and tested by a competent person if the electrical equipment is: 

(a) supplied with electricity through an electrical socket outlet; and   
(b) used in an environment in which the normal use of electrical equipment exposes the equipment to operating conditions that are likely to result in damage to the equipment or a reduction in its expected life span, including conditions that involve exposure to moisture, heat, vibration, mechanical damage, corrosive chemicals or dust. 

Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty. 

(2) In the case of electrical equipment that is new and unused at the workplace, the person conducting the business or undertaking: 

(a) is not required to comply with subregulation (1); and (b) must ensure that the equipment is inspected for obvious damage before being used. 

Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty. 

Note 

However, electrical equipment that is unsafe must not be used (see regulation 149). 

(3) The person must ensure that a record of any testing carried out under subregulation (1) is kept until the electrical equipment is: 

(a) next tested; or (b) permanently removed from the workplace or disposed of. 


Maximum penalty: tier I monetary penalty. 

(4) The record of testing: 

(a) must specify the following: (i) the name of the person who carried out the testing; (ii) the date of the testing; (iii) the outcome of the testing; (iv) the date on which the next testing must be carried out; and   
(b) may be in the form of a tag attached to the electrical equipment tested.

matches.target_title: 161 How the work is to be carried out
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical work on energised electrical equipment is carried out: 

(a) by a competent person who has tools, testing equipment and personal protective equipment that: 

(i) are suitable for the work; and (ii) have been properly tested; and (iii) are maintained in good working order; and 

(b) in accordance with a safe work method statement prepared for the work; and   
(c) subject to subregulation (5), with a safety observer present who has the competence and qualifications specified in subregulation (4). 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) The person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure, so far as is reasonably practicable, that the person who carries out the electrical work uses the tools, testing equipment and personal protective equipment properly. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(3) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(b), the safe work method statement must: 

(a) identify the electrical work; and   
(b) specify hazards associated with that electrical work and risks associated with those hazards; and   
(c) describe the measures to be implemented to control the risks; and   
(d) describe how the risk control measures are to be implemented, monitored and reviewed. 

(4) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(c): 

(a) the safety observer must be competent: 

(b) the safety observer must have been assessed in the previous 12 months as competent to rescue and resuscitate a person. 

(5) A safety observer is not required if: 

(a) the work consists only of testing; and   
(b) the person conducting the business or undertaking has conducted a risk assessment under regulation 158(1)(a) that shows that there is no serious risk associated with the proposed work.

compliance.reasoning: The POLICY is clearly intended to implement the PROVISION’s requirements for energised electrical work, particularly the requirement for a safety observer and the exemption for testing based on risk assessment. The POLICY is partially aligned: it requires a competent safety observer for energised work, allows an exemption for testing where a risk assessment shows hazards/risks are eliminated or effectively managed, and requires annual (12-month) assessment for rescue/resuscitation competence. However, it does not fully cover the PROVISION because it omits additional mandatory elements in subregulation (1): (a) ensuring the energised work is carried out by a competent person with suitable, properly tested, and maintained tools/testing equipment/PPE; (b) ensuring the work is carried out in accordance with a safe work method statement (SWMS) prepared for the work; and (2) ensuring, so far as reasonably practicable, that the worker uses the tools/testing equipment/PPE properly. Additionally, the POLICY’s exemption threshold differs from the PROVISION: the PROVISION requires a risk assessment showing “no serious risk,” whereas the POLICY allows exemption when hazards/risks are “eliminated or effectively managed,” which could permit exemption even where a serious risk remains but is managed. Therefore, the POLICY does not fully satisfy the PROVISION’s requirements.
compliance.remediation: Update the POLICY to: (1) require energised electrical work be performed by a competent person using tools/testing equipment/PPE that are suitable, properly tested, and maintained; (2) require a SWMS for energised electrical work that includes identification of work, hazards/risks, controls, and implementation/monitoring/review; (3) require (so far as reasonably practicable) proper use of tools/testing equipment/PPE; and (4) align the safety observer exemption wording to require a risk assessment demonstrating there is “no serious risk” for testing-only work (and reference the applicable risk assessment requirement), rather than the broader “eliminated or effectively managed” standard.

matches.target_title: 158 Preliminary steps
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure the following before electrical work on energised electrical equipment commences: 

(a) a risk assessment is conducted in relation to the proposed electrical work;   
(b) the area where the electrical work is to be carried out is clear of obstructions so as to allow for easy access and exit;   
(c) the point at which the electrical equipment can be disconnected or isolated from its electricity supply is: (i) clearly marked or labelled; and (ii) clear of obstructions so as to allow for easy access and exit by the worker who is to carry out the electrical work or any other competent person; and (iii) capable of being operated quickly;   
(d) the person authorises the electrical work after consulting with the person with management or control of the workplace. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(a), the risk assessment must be: 

(a) conducted by a competent person; and (b) recorded. 

(3) Subregulation (1)(c) does not apply to electrical work on electrical equipment if: 

(a) the work is to be carried out on the supply side of the main switch on the main switchboard for the equipment; and   
(b) the point at which the equipment can be disconnected from its electricity supply is not reasonably accessible from the work location.

compliance.reasoning: The texts are functionally related because both govern prerequisites/controls for energised electrical work and rely on risk assessment to determine required safety measures. However, the POLICY focuses on requiring a competent safety observer (with specific competencies and annual CPR assessment) unless a risk assessment shows an observer is not required. The PROVISION imposes additional mandatory preconditions before energised electrical work commences: (1)(a) a risk assessment must be conducted; (2) it must be conducted by a competent person and recorded; (1)(b) the work area must be clear of obstructions for access/exit; (1)(c) the isolation/disconnection point must be clearly marked, unobstructed, and operable quickly (subject to the exception in (3)); and (1)(d) the work must be authorised after consultation with the person with management/control of the workplace. These requirements are not addressed in the POLICY text, and the POLICY’s risk assessment exception does not state that the risk assessment is recorded or conducted by a competent person. Therefore, the POLICY does not fully satisfy the PROVISION’s obligations.
compliance.remediation: Update the policy/procedure for energised electrical work to explicitly require: (1) a pre-work risk assessment conducted by a competent person and recorded; (2) verification that the work area is clear of obstructions to allow easy access/exit; (3) verification that the isolation/disconnection point is clearly marked/labelled, unobstructed, and capable of quick operation (and document when the supply-side/main switch exception applies); and (4) formal authorisation of the energised work after consultation with the person with management/control of the workplace. Retain the safety observer requirements as an additional control where applicable.

matches.target_title: 155 Duty to determine whether equipment is energised
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that, before electrical work is carried out on electrical equipment, the equipment is tested by a competent person to determine whether or not it is energised. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

Note 

Regulation 157 allows electrical testing to be carried out on electrical equipment for the purposes of this regulation. Regulation 161 sets out how the testing is to be carried out. 

(2) The person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that: 

(a) each exposed part is treated as energised until it is isolated and determined not to be energised; and   
(b) each high-voltage exposed part is earthed after being deenergised. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

matches.target_title: 150 Inspection and testing of electrical equipment
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must ensure that electrical equipment is regularly inspected and tested by a competent person if the electrical equipment is: 

(a) supplied with electricity through an electrical socket outlet; and   
(b) used in an environment in which the normal use of electrical equipment exposes the equipment to operating conditions that are likely to result in damage to the equipment or a reduction in its expected life span, including conditions that involve exposure to moisture, heat, vibration, mechanical damage, corrosive chemicals or dust. 

Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty. 

(2) In the case of electrical equipment that is new and unused at the workplace, the person conducting the business or undertaking: 

(a) is not required to comply with subregulation (1); and (b) must ensure that the equipment is inspected for obvious damage before being used. 

Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty. 

Note 

However, electrical equipment that is unsafe must not be used (see regulation 149). 

(3) The person must ensure that a record of any testing carried out under subregulation (1) is kept until the electrical equipment is: 

(a) next tested; or (b) permanently removed from the workplace or disposed of. 


Maximum penalty: tier I monetary penalty. 

(4) The record of testing: 

(a) must specify the following: (i) the name of the person who carried out the testing; (ii) the date of the testing; (iii) the outcome of the testing; (iv) the date on which the next testing must be carried out; and   
(b) may be in the form of a tag attached to the electrical equipment tested.

matches.target_title: 157 Electrical work on energised electrical equipment—when permitted
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical work on energised electrical equipment is not carried out unless: 

(a) it is necessary in the interests of health and safety that the electrical work is carried out on the equipment while the equipment is energised; or Example 

It may be necessary that life-saving equipment remain energised and operating while electrical work is carried out on the equipment.   
(b) it is necessary that the electrical equipment to be worked on is energised in order for the work to be carried out properly; or   
(c) it is necessary for the purposes of testing required under regulation 155; or   
(d) there is no reasonable alternative means of carrying out the work. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) The electrical work that may be carried out under subregulation (1)(a), (b) and (d) may include testing of the energised electrical equipment.

compliance.reasoning: The provision restricts when energised electrical work may be carried out (only if one of the necessity/no-alternative conditions in (1)(a)-(d) is met). The policy addresses a different control (use of a competent safety observer during energised work, with a limited exception for testing based on risk assessment). The policy does not include or require the prerequisite determination that energised work is only performed when permitted under (1)(a)-(d), nor does it prohibit energised work unless those conditions are satisfied. Therefore, while related to the same activity (energised electrical work), the policy does not fully implement the provision’s core obligation and is not compliant as a standalone control against this provision.
compliance.remediation: Amend the policy to explicitly prohibit energised electrical work unless one of the conditions in (1)(a)-(d) is satisfied, and require documented justification (and approval/verification) identifying which condition applies before work proceeds. Ensure the procedure covers testing as allowed under (1)(c) and clarifies that observer requirements are additional controls, not a substitute for meeting (1)(a)-(d).

matches.target_title: 160 Contact with equipment being worked on
matches.target_text:
A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that, while electrical work is being carried out on energised electrical equipment, all persons are prevented from creating an electrical risk by inadvertently making contact with an exposed energised component of the equipment. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning: The provision requires the PCBU to ensure that, during energised electrical work, all persons are prevented from creating an electrical risk by inadvertently contacting an exposed energised component. The policy focuses on requiring a competent safety observer (with rescue/CPR competence) during energised work, with limited exceptions. While a safety observer may help manage emergency response and oversight, the policy does not explicitly require controls to prevent inadvertent contact with exposed energised components (e.g., insulation, barriers, shrouding, exclusion zones, access control, lockable covers, signage, or supervision of all persons in the area). It also does not clearly address preventing 'all persons' (not just the electrical worker) from creating the risk. Therefore, the policy does not fully satisfy the specific preventive obligation in the provision.
compliance.remediation: Amend the policy to explicitly require measures that prevent inadvertent contact with exposed energised components during energised work, including: establishing and enforcing exclusion zones and access control for all persons; installing/using insulating barriers, shrouds, covers, or screens over exposed energised parts where practicable; using warning signage and physical barricading; ensuring tools/PPE and work methods reduce inadvertent contact risk; and assigning responsibility (PCBU/site supervisor) to verify these controls are in place and maintained throughout the work.

matches.target_title: 240 Maintenance, inspection and testing of amusement devices and passenger ropeways
matches.target_text:
(1) The person with management or control of an amusement device or passenger ropeway at a workplace must ensure that the maintenance, inspection and, if necessary, testing of the device or ropeway is carried out:

(a) by a competent person; and (b) in accordance with:

(i) the recommendations of the designer or manufacturer or designer and manufacturer; or

(ii) if a maintenance manual for the device or ropeway has been prepared by a competent person, the requirements of the maintenance manual.

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

(2) A person is not a competent person to carry out a detailed inspection of an amusement device or passenger ropeway that includes an electrical installation unless the person is qualified, or is assisted by a person who is qualified, to inspect electrical installations.

matches.target_title: 147 Risk management
matches.target_text:
A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must manage risks to health and safety associated with electrical risks at the workplace, in accordance with Part 3.1. 

Example 

Electrical risks associated with the design, construction, installation, protection, maintenance and testing of electrical equipment and electrical installations at a workplace. 

Note 

WHS Act—section 19 (see regulation 9).

compliance.reasoning: The provision imposes a general duty on the PCBU to manage risks to health and safety associated with electrical risks at the workplace. The policy sets specific controls for managing electrical risk during energised electrical work (use of a competent safety observer, competency/CPR requirements, and operational constraints), which is a direct risk-management measure aligned with the provision’s requirement to manage electrical risks. The policy therefore supports compliance with the provision and does not conflict with it. However, the provision is broader than the policy (covering design, construction, installation, protection, maintenance and testing generally), so this policy alone would not be sufficient to demonstrate full compliance with all electrical risk management obligations, but it is compliant as far as it goes.

matches.target_title: 166 Duty of person conducting a business or undertaking
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must ensure, so far as is reasonably practicable, that no person, plant or thing at the workplace comes within an unsafe distance of an overhead or underground electric line. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) If it is not reasonably practicable to ensure the safe distance of a person, plant or thing from an overhead or underground electric line, the person conducting the business or undertaking at the workplace must ensure that:
(a) a risk assessment is conducted in relation to the proposed work; and 

(b) control measures implemented are consistent with: 

(i) the risk assessment; and 

(ii) if an electricity supply authority is responsible for the electric line, any requirements of the authority. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

Notes 

1 [Relevant electricity safety legislation] will also apply to the person conducting the business or undertaking.

matches.target_title: 156 De-energised equipment must not be inadvertently re-energised
matches.target_text:
A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical equipment that has been de-energised to allow electrical work to be carried out on it is not inadvertently re-energised while the work is being carried out. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

source_text:
Section 6.3 of the Code of Practice 2018 – Managing electrical risks in the workplace provides additional guidance on requirements and recommendations to comply with the legislation regarding carrying out energised electrical work, as well as:

 Hazards indirectly caused by electricity, such as conductive materials;  Work positioning that minimises the risk of inadvertent contact with exposed energised parts; and  Safety barriers and signs that must be erected when work is carried out.

matches.target_title: 147 Risk management
matches.target_text:
A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must manage risks to health and safety associated with electrical risks at the workplace, in accordance with Part 3.1. 

Example 

Electrical risks associated with the design, construction, installation, protection, maintenance and testing of electrical equipment and electrical installations at a workplace. 

Note 

WHS Act—section 19 (see regulation 9).

compliance.reasoning: The provision imposes a broad duty on the PCBU to manage electrical health and safety risks at the workplace in accordance with Part 3.1 (risk management). The policy text references Section 6.3 of a Code of Practice and lists specific guidance items (indirect electrical hazards, work positioning, barriers/signs) focused on energised electrical work controls. However, it does not state that the PCBU must manage electrical risks generally, does not reference compliance with Part 3.1 risk management requirements, and does not cover the full lifecycle risks noted in the provision (design, construction, installation, protection, maintenance, testing). Therefore, the policy is only a partial control-focused guidance statement and does not fully meet the provision’s overarching risk management obligation.
compliance.remediation: Update the policy to explicitly require the PCBU to manage electrical risks to health and safety in accordance with Part 3.1, including hazard identification, risk assessment (where required), implementation of controls using the hierarchy of controls, and ongoing review. Expand scope beyond energised electrical work to cover electrical risks associated with design, construction, installation, protection, maintenance and testing of electrical equipment/installations, and link the Code of Practice guidance as supporting measures within the broader risk management framework.

matches.target_title: 158 Preliminary steps
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure the following before electrical work on energised electrical equipment commences: 

(a) a risk assessment is conducted in relation to the proposed electrical work;   
(b) the area where the electrical work is to be carried out is clear of obstructions so as to allow for easy access and exit;   
(c) the point at which the electrical equipment can be disconnected or isolated from its electricity supply is: (i) clearly marked or labelled; and (ii) clear of obstructions so as to allow for easy access and exit by the worker who is to carry out the electrical work or any other competent person; and (iii) capable of being operated quickly;   
(d) the person authorises the electrical work after consulting with the person with management or control of the workplace. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(a), the risk assessment must be: 

(a) conducted by a competent person; and (b) recorded. 

(3) Subregulation (1)(c) does not apply to electrical work on electrical equipment if: 

(a) the work is to be carried out on the supply side of the main switch on the main switchboard for the equipment; and   
(b) the point at which the equipment can be disconnected from its electricity supply is not reasonably accessible from the work location.

compliance.reasoning: The POLICY text is guidance about managing electrical risks when carrying out energised electrical work (including hazards indirectly caused by electricity, safe work positioning to avoid inadvertent contact, and use of safety barriers/signage). The PROVISION imposes specific pre-commencement duties for energised electrical work: conducting and recording a competent-person risk assessment, ensuring the work area and isolation/disconnection point are unobstructed and clearly marked, ensuring the isolation point can be operated quickly, and authorising the work after consultation with workplace management/control. The POLICY does not address these mandatory preconditions (risk assessment requirements, recording, access/egress and obstruction clearance, isolation point marking/operability, or authorisation/consultation). Therefore, it does not demonstrate compliance with the PROVISION.
compliance.remediation: Update the policy/procedure for energised electrical work to explicitly require, before work starts: (1) a risk assessment conducted by a competent person and recorded; (2) the work area is cleared of obstructions to allow easy access/exit; (3) the isolation/disconnection point is clearly marked/labelled, unobstructed for access/exit, and operable quickly (or document applicability of the supply-side exception); and (4) formal authorisation of the work after consultation with the person with management/control of the workplace, with records retained.

matches.target_title: 160 Contact with equipment being worked on
matches.target_text:
A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that, while electrical work is being carried out on energised electrical equipment, all persons are prevented from creating an electrical risk by inadvertently making contact with an exposed energised component of the equipment. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning: The POLICY text is guidance (Code of Practice section 6.3) describing recommended controls for managing risks during energised electrical work, including preventing inadvertent contact with exposed energised parts via work positioning and safety barriers/signs. The PROVISION imposes a mandatory duty on a PCBU to ensure persons are prevented from creating electrical risk by inadvertently contacting exposed energised components while energised work is carried out. The POLICY is therefore functionally related as it addresses measures intended to meet the PROVISION’s obligation. However, the POLICY text does not state that the PCBU 'must ensure' prevention, nor does it specify implemented controls or outcomes demonstrating that persons are actually prevented from inadvertent contact. As written, it is guidance-only and insufficient to confirm compliance with the mandatory requirement.
compliance.remediation: Add an explicit requirement and implementation detail that, during energised electrical work, the PCBU ensures persons are prevented from inadvertent contact with exposed energised components (e.g., establish exclusion zones, physical barriers/insulation, signage, access control/spotters, and verified safe work positioning), and document how these controls are applied and monitored.

matches.target_title: 155 Duty to determine whether equipment is energised
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that, before electrical work is carried out on electrical equipment, the equipment is tested by a competent person to determine whether or not it is energised. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

Note 

Regulation 157 allows electrical testing to be carried out on electrical equipment for the purposes of this regulation. Regulation 161 sets out how the testing is to be carried out. 

(2) The person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that: 

(a) each exposed part is treated as energised until it is isolated and determined not to be energised; and   
(b) each high-voltage exposed part is earthed after being deenergised. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning: The POLICY text provides guidance on managing electrical risks during energised electrical work (e.g., indirect electrical hazards, safe work positioning, and use of safety barriers/signage). The PROVISION imposes specific mandatory pre-work controls: (1) testing electrical equipment by a competent person to determine whether it is energised before electrical work is carried out; and (2) treating exposed parts as energised until isolated and confirmed de-energised, and earthing high-voltage exposed parts after de-energising. The POLICY does not address these required controls (competent-person testing, treat-as-energised-until-proven-dead, isolation confirmation, HV earthing). Therefore, it does not demonstrate compliance with the PROVISION.
compliance.remediation: Update the policy/procedure to explicitly require: (1) pre-work testing of electrical equipment by a competent person to confirm energised/de-energised status (with reference to the applicable testing method requirements); (2) treating all exposed parts as energised until isolated and verified de-energised; and (3) earthing each high-voltage exposed part after de-energising. Include responsibilities, step-by-step process, and documentation/recordkeeping for these actions.

matches.target_title: 157 Electrical work on energised electrical equipment—when permitted
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical work on energised electrical equipment is not carried out unless: 

(a) it is necessary in the interests of health and safety that the electrical work is carried out on the equipment while the equipment is energised; or Example 

It may be necessary that life-saving equipment remain energised and operating while electrical work is carried out on the equipment.   
(b) it is necessary that the electrical equipment to be worked on is energised in order for the work to be carried out properly; or   
(c) it is necessary for the purposes of testing required under regulation 155; or   
(d) there is no reasonable alternative means of carrying out the work. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) The electrical work that may be carried out under subregulation (1)(a), (b) and (d) may include testing of the energised electrical equipment.

compliance.reasoning: The POLICY text provides guidance on managing risks when carrying out energised electrical work (e.g., indirect electrical hazards, safe work positioning, and use of safety barriers/signage). The PROVISION imposes a primary legal obligation that energised electrical work must not be carried out unless one of the listed necessity conditions applies (health and safety necessity, work requires energisation, required testing, or no reasonable alternative). The POLICY does not state or evidence that energised work is prohibited except under those necessity conditions, nor does it require documenting/justifying that one of the exceptions applies before proceeding. Therefore, the POLICY does not fully address the core prohibition/exception test required by the PROVISION, even though it addresses related risk controls.
compliance.remediation: Update the POLICY to explicitly require that energised electrical work is not performed unless one of the permitted conditions in the PROVISION is met, and require documented justification/approval demonstrating which condition applies (including consideration of de-energising and confirming no reasonable alternative). Then link the existing risk-control guidance (positioning, barriers/signage, indirect hazards) as additional controls to be applied when an exception permits energised work.

matches.target_title: 166 Duty of person conducting a business or undertaking
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must ensure, so far as is reasonably practicable, that no person, plant or thing at the workplace comes within an unsafe distance of an overhead or underground electric line. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) If it is not reasonably practicable to ensure the safe distance of a person, plant or thing from an overhead or underground electric line, the person conducting the business or undertaking at the workplace must ensure that:
(a) a risk assessment is conducted in relation to the proposed work; and 

(b) control measures implemented are consistent with: 

(i) the risk assessment; and 

(ii) if an electricity supply authority is responsible for the electric line, any requirements of the authority. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

Notes 

1 [Relevant electricity safety legislation] will also apply to the person conducting the business or undertaking.

compliance.reasoning: The provision imposes a specific duty to keep persons/plant/things a safe distance from overhead/underground electric lines, and where that is not reasonably practicable, to conduct a risk assessment and implement control measures consistent with that assessment and any electricity supply authority requirements. The policy text is broader guidance about managing electrical risks during energised electrical work (e.g., conductive materials, work positioning to avoid contact with exposed energised parts, and use of safety barriers/signs). It does not explicitly address maintaining safe distances from overhead/underground electric lines, does not state the 'so far as reasonably practicable' requirement, and does not include the mandated fallback process (risk assessment + controls aligned to the assessment and supply authority requirements). Therefore, the policy does not sufficiently cover the provision’s obligations.
compliance.remediation: Update the policy to explicitly require: (1) maintaining prescribed/unsafe-distance exclusions from overhead and underground electric lines so far as reasonably practicable; (2) when safe distance cannot be maintained, conducting a documented risk assessment for the proposed work; (3) implementing control measures consistent with the risk assessment; and (4) complying with any applicable electricity supply authority requirements (e.g., isolation, de-energisation, permits, spotters, barriers, signage, exclusion zones). Include procedures, responsibilities, and verification/recordkeeping for these steps.

matches.target_title: 161 How the work is to be carried out
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical work on energised electrical equipment is carried out: 

(a) by a competent person who has tools, testing equipment and personal protective equipment that: 

(i) are suitable for the work; and (ii) have been properly tested; and (iii) are maintained in good working order; and 

(b) in accordance with a safe work method statement prepared for the work; and   
(c) subject to subregulation (5), with a safety observer present who has the competence and qualifications specified in subregulation (4). 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) The person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure, so far as is reasonably practicable, that the person who carries out the electrical work uses the tools, testing equipment and personal protective equipment properly. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(3) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(b), the safe work method statement must: 

(a) identify the electrical work; and   
(b) specify hazards associated with that electrical work and risks associated with those hazards; and   
(c) describe the measures to be implemented to control the risks; and   
(d) describe how the risk control measures are to be implemented, monitored and reviewed. 

(4) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(c): 

(a) the safety observer must be competent: 

(b) the safety observer must have been assessed in the previous 12 months as competent to rescue and resuscitate a person. 

(5) A safety observer is not required if: 

(a) the work consists only of testing; and   
(b) the person conducting the business or undertaking has conducted a risk assessment under regulation 158(1)(a) that shows that there is no serious risk associated with the proposed work.

compliance.reasoning: The POLICY text references Section 6.3 of a Code of Practice as providing guidance on managing risks when carrying out energised electrical work, including indirect electrical hazards (e.g., conductive materials), work positioning to minimise inadvertent contact with exposed energised parts, and erection of safety barriers and signs. The PROVISION text imposes specific mandatory requirements for energised electrical work: it must be performed by a competent person with suitable/tested/maintained tools and PPE; in accordance with a safe work method statement (SWMS) that identifies the work, hazards/risks, and control measures plus implementation/monitoring/review; and with a competent safety observer (with recent rescue/resuscitation competency) unless a testing-only exception applies with a risk assessment showing no serious risk. The POLICY text is high-level guidance and does not demonstrate that these mandatory elements (competent person with tested/maintained equipment, SWMS content, safety observer requirements/exception conditions, and ensuring proper use of equipment) are in place. Therefore, based on the provided POLICY text alone, it does not satisfy the PROVISION’s enforceable requirements.
compliance.remediation: Update the policy/procedure set for energised electrical work to explicitly require: (1) work only by a competent person using tools/testing equipment/PPE that are suitable, properly tested, and maintained; (2) a SWMS for each energised task meeting the required contents (identify work; hazards/risks; controls; implementation/monitoring/review); (3) a competent safety observer present with documented rescue/resuscitation competency assessed within the last 12 months, unless the testing-only exception applies and a documented risk assessment shows no serious risk; and (4) processes to ensure, so far as reasonably practicable, that workers properly use the tools/testing equipment/PPE (e.g., supervision, training, audits).

matches.target_title: 154 Electrical work on energised electrical equipment—prohibited
matches.target_text:
Subject to this Division, a person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical work is not carried out on electrical equipment while the equipment is energised. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning: The provision imposes a primary duty to ensure electrical work is not carried out on energised equipment (i.e., de-energised work is required, subject to limited exceptions). The policy text references guidance on managing risks when carrying out energised electrical work and lists risk controls (conductive materials, work positioning, barriers/signs), but it does not state the overarching prohibition/requirement to avoid energised work or the obligation to ensure work is not performed while energised except as permitted. As written, the policy could be interpreted as permitting energised work as a normal practice provided controls are used, which does not satisfy the provision’s requirement to ensure energised work does not occur (unless an exception applies).
compliance.remediation: Amend the policy to explicitly require that electrical equipment must be de-energised and isolated before electrical work, and that energised electrical work is prohibited except where permitted under the applicable Division (e.g., where de-energising is not reasonably practicable and additional controls/authorisations are implemented). Retain the listed controls as supplementary requirements that apply only when an authorised exception allows energised work, and document the approval process, risk assessment, and verification of isolation.

matches.target_title: 156 De-energised equipment must not be inadvertently re-energised
matches.target_text:
A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical equipment that has been de-energised to allow electrical work to be carried out on it is not inadvertently re-energised while the work is being carried out. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning: The provision imposes a specific duty: when electrical equipment is de-energised for electrical work, the PCBU must ensure it is not inadvertently re-energised during the work (i.e., effective isolation/lockout and control of re-energisation). The policy text references Section 6.3 guidance on managing risks during energised electrical work and mentions indirect hazards, work positioning, and barriers/signs. It does not address preventing inadvertent re-energisation of de-energised equipment (isolation, lockout/tagout, securing switches, testing for dead, control of keys/permits). Therefore, the policy does not demonstrate coverage of the provision’s requirement.
compliance.remediation: Update the policy/procedure to explicitly require controls to prevent inadvertent re-energisation of de-energised equipment during electrical work, such as documented isolation procedures, lockout/tagout, tagging and signage at isolation points, verification/testing for de-energised state, control of access to isolators/keys, and (where applicable) permit-to-work and re-energisation authorization steps.

matches.target_title: 150 Inspection and testing of electrical equipment
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must ensure that electrical equipment is regularly inspected and tested by a competent person if the electrical equipment is: 

(a) supplied with electricity through an electrical socket outlet; and   
(b) used in an environment in which the normal use of electrical equipment exposes the equipment to operating conditions that are likely to result in damage to the equipment or a reduction in its expected life span, including conditions that involve exposure to moisture, heat, vibration, mechanical damage, corrosive chemicals or dust. 

Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty. 

(2) In the case of electrical equipment that is new and unused at the workplace, the person conducting the business or undertaking: 

(a) is not required to comply with subregulation (1); and (b) must ensure that the equipment is inspected for obvious damage before being used. 

Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty. 

Note 

However, electrical equipment that is unsafe must not be used (see regulation 149). 

(3) The person must ensure that a record of any testing carried out under subregulation (1) is kept until the electrical equipment is: 

(a) next tested; or (b) permanently removed from the workplace or disposed of. 


Maximum penalty: tier I monetary penalty. 

(4) The record of testing: 

(a) must specify the following: (i) the name of the person who carried out the testing; (ii) the date of the testing; (iii) the outcome of the testing; (iv) the date on which the next testing must be carried out; and   
(b) may be in the form of a tag attached to the electrical equipment tested.

source_title: 7.7 Energised electrical work – testing and fault-finding

matches.target_title: 155 Duty to determine whether equipment is energised
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that, before electrical work is carried out on electrical equipment, the equipment is tested by a competent person to determine whether or not it is energised. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

Note 

Regulation 157 allows electrical testing to be carried out on electrical equipment for the purposes of this regulation. Regulation 161 sets out how the testing is to be carried out. 

(2) The person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that: 

(a) each exposed part is treated as energised until it is isolated and determined not to be energised; and   
(b) each high-voltage exposed part is earthed after being deenergised. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning: The provision requires the PCBU to ensure that, before electrical work is carried out on electrical equipment, the equipment is tested by a competent person to determine whether it is energised, and that exposed parts are treated as energised until isolated and determined not energised (and HV exposed parts earthed after de-energised). The policy discusses testing and fault-finding practices (including in energised environments), instrument suitability, PPE, barriers, authorised access, and worker competence/training. However, it does not explicitly mandate a pre-work test to determine whether equipment is energised before electrical work begins, nor does it explicitly require treating each exposed part as energised until isolated and proven de-energised. It also does not address the specific requirement to earth each high-voltage exposed part after de-energisation. Therefore, the policy only partially aligns and does not fully satisfy the provision’s explicit obligations.
compliance.remediation: Amend the policy to explicitly require that before any electrical work on electrical equipment begins, a competent person must test the equipment to determine whether it is energised (with reference to the applicable testing method requirements). Add a clear rule that all exposed parts must be treated as energised until isolated and proven de-energised. Add a requirement that each high-voltage exposed part is earthed after being de-energised, including procedural steps and responsibility/accountability (PCBU/authorised persons).

matches.target_title: 161 How the work is to be carried out
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical work on energised electrical equipment is carried out: 

(a) by a competent person who has tools, testing equipment and personal protective equipment that: 

(i) are suitable for the work; and (ii) have been properly tested; and (iii) are maintained in good working order; and 

(b) in accordance with a safe work method statement prepared for the work; and   
(c) subject to subregulation (5), with a safety observer present who has the competence and qualifications specified in subregulation (4). 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) The person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure, so far as is reasonably practicable, that the person who carries out the electrical work uses the tools, testing equipment and personal protective equipment properly. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(3) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(b), the safe work method statement must: 

(a) identify the electrical work; and   
(b) specify hazards associated with that electrical work and risks associated with those hazards; and   
(c) describe the measures to be implemented to control the risks; and   
(d) describe how the risk control measures are to be implemented, monitored and reviewed. 

(4) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(c): 

(a) the safety observer must be competent: 

(b) the safety observer must have been assessed in the previous 12 months as competent to rescue and resuscitate a person. 

(5) A safety observer is not required if: 

(a) the work consists only of testing; and   
(b) the person conducting the business or undertaking has conducted a risk assessment under regulation 158(1)(a) that shows that there is no serious risk associated with the proposed work.

compliance.reasoning: The POLICY is clearly intended to guide/ensure safe energised electrical testing and fault-finding and therefore relates to the PROVISION’s obligations for energised electrical work (competent person with suitable/tested/maintained tools/PPE, SWMS, safety observer requirements, and ensuring proper use). The POLICY substantially covers: (a) competent/trained workers; (b) suitable, tested, maintained tools/test instruments/PPE; (c) use of insulated/rated tools and appropriately rated PPE; (d) safety observer based on risk assessment; and (e) ongoing review and area control. However, it does not explicitly require that energised electrical work be carried out "in accordance with a safe work method statement (SWMS) prepared for the work" nor does it specify the required SWMS contents (identify work, hazards/risks, controls, and implementation/monitoring/review). Additionally, the POLICY’s safety observer approach is not aligned with the PROVISION’s default requirement: the PROVISION requires a safety observer for energised electrical work unless the work is only testing and a risk assessment shows no serious risk; the POLICY instead makes the observer conditional on risk assessment generally and does not include the specific exemption conditions. The POLICY also does not state the requirement that the safety observer be assessed within the previous 12 months as competent to rescue and resuscitate.
compliance.remediation: Update the POLICY to: (1) mandate a SWMS for energised electrical work and require it to include the elements in subregulation (3) (work identification, hazards/risks, control measures, and how controls are implemented/monitored/reviewed); (2) align safety observer requirements with the regulation—require a safety observer for energised electrical work by default, and only allow no observer when the work is only testing and a documented risk assessment shows no serious risk; (3) specify safety observer competency requirements, including documented assessment within the last 12 months for rescue and resuscitation; (4) explicitly require the PCBU to ensure, so far as reasonably practicable, that workers use tools/testing equipment/PPE properly (not just that they are provided/selected).

matches.target_title: 150 Inspection and testing of electrical equipment
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must ensure that electrical equipment is regularly inspected and tested by a competent person if the electrical equipment is: 

(a) supplied with electricity through an electrical socket outlet; and   
(b) used in an environment in which the normal use of electrical equipment exposes the equipment to operating conditions that are likely to result in damage to the equipment or a reduction in its expected life span, including conditions that involve exposure to moisture, heat, vibration, mechanical damage, corrosive chemicals or dust. 

Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty. 

(2) In the case of electrical equipment that is new and unused at the workplace, the person conducting the business or undertaking: 

(a) is not required to comply with subregulation (1); and (b) must ensure that the equipment is inspected for obvious damage before being used. 

Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty. 

Note 

However, electrical equipment that is unsafe must not be used (see regulation 149). 

(3) The person must ensure that a record of any testing carried out under subregulation (1) is kept until the electrical equipment is: 

(a) next tested; or (b) permanently removed from the workplace or disposed of. 


Maximum penalty: tier I monetary penalty. 

(4) The record of testing: 

(a) must specify the following: (i) the name of the person who carried out the testing; (ii) the date of the testing; (iii) the outcome of the testing; (iv) the date on which the next testing must be carried out; and   
(b) may be in the form of a tag attached to the electrical equipment tested.

compliance.reasoning: The provision requires the PCBU to ensure certain electrical equipment (plug-in equipment used in harsh/damaging environments) is regularly inspected and tested by a competent person, and to keep specific records of that testing (tester name, test date, outcome, next test due date) until the next test or removal/disposal. The policy text focuses on safe methods and controls for carrying out testing and fault-finding (especially in energised environments), suitability/calibration of test instruments, PPE, training/competency, and coordination procedures. It does not establish a program for regular inspection/testing of plug-in equipment in specified environments, does not address the exemption/requirement for new and unused equipment to be inspected for obvious damage before use, and does not require keeping the mandated testing records with the specified fields and retention period. Therefore it does not fulfill the core obligations of the provision.
compliance.remediation: Add a section that: (1) defines the scope of equipment covered (electrical equipment supplied via socket outlets used in environments likely to cause damage/reduced lifespan, e.g., moisture/heat/vibration/mechanical damage/chemicals/dust); (2) requires regular inspection and testing by a competent person at defined intervals (or by risk-based schedule) and assigns responsibility to the PCBU; (3) requires pre-use inspection for obvious damage for new/unused equipment; (4) mandates recordkeeping for each test until next test or removal/disposal, including tester name, test date, outcome, and next test due date, and allows tagging as the record; and (5) links to the prohibition on using unsafe equipment.

matches.target_title: 158 Preliminary steps
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure the following before electrical work on energised electrical equipment commences: 

(a) a risk assessment is conducted in relation to the proposed electrical work;   
(b) the area where the electrical work is to be carried out is clear of obstructions so as to allow for easy access and exit;   
(c) the point at which the electrical equipment can be disconnected or isolated from its electricity supply is: (i) clearly marked or labelled; and (ii) clear of obstructions so as to allow for easy access and exit by the worker who is to carry out the electrical work or any other competent person; and (iii) capable of being operated quickly;   
(d) the person authorises the electrical work after consulting with the person with management or control of the workplace. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(a), the risk assessment must be: 

(a) conducted by a competent person; and (b) recorded. 

(3) Subregulation (1)(c) does not apply to electrical work on electrical equipment if: 

(a) the work is to be carried out on the supply side of the main switch on the main switchboard for the equipment; and   
(b) the point at which the equipment can be disconnected from its electricity supply is not reasonably accessible from the work location.

compliance.reasoning: The POLICY is functionally related to the PROVISION because it sets procedural controls for testing/fault-finding in an energised environment (a form of energised electrical work) and assigns duties to ensure safety, tools/PPE, barriers, authorised access, and coordination. However, it does not fully satisfy the specific pre-commencement requirements in the PROVISION: (1)(a) requires a risk assessment before energised work commences, and (2) requires it be conducted by a competent person and recorded—POLICY mentions a risk assessment only in relation to using a safety observer/PPE selection, but does not mandate a pre-work risk assessment nor recording. (1)(b) requires the work area be clear of obstructions for easy access/exit—POLICY does not address access/egress or obstruction clearance. (1)(c) requires the isolation/disconnection point be clearly marked/labelled, unobstructed, and operable quickly—POLICY mentions coordination procedures for switching but does not require marking/labeling, ensuring access, or quick operability of the isolation point. (1)(d) requires authorisation after consulting with the person with management/control of the workplace—POLICY restricts entry to authorised persons but does not require formal authorisation of the work following consultation with workplace management/control. Therefore the POLICY is only partially aligned and is non-compliant against the PROVISION.
compliance.remediation: Update the POLICY to explicitly require, before any energised testing/fault-finding begins: (1) a documented risk assessment conducted by a competent person; (2) verification that the work area and the isolation/disconnection point are free of obstructions and allow easy access/exit; (3) verification that the isolation/disconnection point is clearly marked/labelled and can be operated quickly (or document applicability of the supply-side exception); and (4) formal authorisation of the energised work after consultation with the person with management or control of the workplace, with records retained.

matches.target_title: 157 Electrical work on energised electrical equipment—when permitted
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical work on energised electrical equipment is not carried out unless: 

(a) it is necessary in the interests of health and safety that the electrical work is carried out on the equipment while the equipment is energised; or Example 

It may be necessary that life-saving equipment remain energised and operating while electrical work is carried out on the equipment.   
(b) it is necessary that the electrical equipment to be worked on is energised in order for the work to be carried out properly; or   
(c) it is necessary for the purposes of testing required under regulation 155; or   
(d) there is no reasonable alternative means of carrying out the work. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) The electrical work that may be carried out under subregulation (1)(a), (b) and (d) may include testing of the energised electrical equipment.

compliance.reasoning: The provision prohibits energised electrical work unless one of the necessity conditions in (1)(a)-(d) applies (health/safety necessity, required for proper work, required testing, or no reasonable alternative). The policy focuses on how to conduct testing/fault-finding safely when energised work occurs (barriers, authorised access, suitable instruments/PPE, observer, restoring safe condition, coordination procedures), and it encourages considering de-energised methods first. However, it does not explicitly require a decision/authorisation step that energised work is only permitted when one of the regulatory necessity criteria is met, nor does it require documenting that no reasonable alternative exists. Therefore, the policy does not fully implement the gating prohibition/exception structure required by the provision.
compliance.remediation: Add an explicit rule and workflow stating energised electrical work (including testing) must not be performed unless one of (a)-(d) applies, with mandatory pre-job justification and approval. Require documenting: (1) which criterion applies, (2) why de-energised methods are not reasonably practicable, and (3) the specific testing requirement if relying on testing necessity. Integrate this as a prerequisite in the risk assessment/permit-to-work or switching/coordination procedure before any energised testing/fault-finding begins.

matches.target_title: 160 Contact with equipment being worked on
matches.target_text:
A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that, while electrical work is being carried out on energised electrical equipment, all persons are prevented from creating an electrical risk by inadvertently making contact with an exposed energised component of the equipment. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning: The provision requires the PCBU to ensure that, during energised electrical work, all persons are prevented from creating an electrical risk by inadvertently contacting an exposed energised component. The policy directly addresses this by requiring (before testing/fault-finding in an energised environment) identification of exposed conductive parts that could become energised, installation of temporary or fixed barriers to prevent inadvertent contact, and restricting access so only authorised persons may enter the immediate work area. These controls are functionally aligned with preventing inadvertent contact by any person in the vicinity during energised work.

matches.target_title: 147 Risk management
matches.target_text:
A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must manage risks to health and safety associated with electrical risks at the workplace, in accordance with Part 3.1. 

Example 

Electrical risks associated with the design, construction, installation, protection, maintenance and testing of electrical equipment and electrical installations at a workplace. 

Note 

WHS Act—section 19 (see regulation 9).

compliance.reasoning: The PROVISION requires the PCBU to manage risks to health and safety associated with electrical risks at the workplace in accordance with Part 3.1, including risks arising from testing of electrical equipment/installations. The POLICY specifically addresses risk management for electrical testing and fault-finding, prioritising de-energised methods, prescribing controls for energised testing (barriers, restricted access, suitable/maintained/calibrated instruments, competent workers, PPE selection and use, safety observer based on risk assessment, ongoing hazard review, restoration to safe condition, and coordination/switching procedures). These measures constitute practical risk controls consistent with managing electrical risks as required by the PROVISION.

matches.target_title: 165 Testing of residual current devices
matches.target_text:
(1) A person with management or control of a workplace must take all reasonable steps to ensure that residual current devices used at the workplace are tested regularly by a competent person to ensure that the devices are operating effectively. 

Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty. 

(2) The person must keep a record of all testing of a residual current device (other than any testing conducted daily) until the earlier of the following occurs: 

(a) the device is next tested;   
(b) the device is permanently removed from use. 

Maximum penalty: tier I monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning: The provision imposes a specific obligation on the person with management or control of the workplace to (1) ensure residual current devices (RCDs) are tested regularly by a competent person and (2) keep records of all RCD testing (other than daily testing) until the next test or removal from use. The policy discusses general electrical testing and fault-finding practices, competence, instrument standards, PPE, and safety controls, but it does not specifically require regular testing of RCDs nor does it require maintaining the mandated RCD testing records for the specified retention period. Therefore, the policy does not demonstrate compliance with the provision’s specific RCD testing and recordkeeping requirements.
compliance.remediation: Update the policy (or add a dedicated procedure) to: (1) require regular testing of all workplace RCDs by a competent person (define frequency consistent with applicable standards/legislation), (2) assign responsibility to the person with management/control for ensuring the testing occurs, and (3) require keeping records of all non-daily RCD tests until the next test or the device is permanently removed from use (including what must be recorded and where records are stored).

matches.target_title: 156 De-energised equipment must not be inadvertently re-energised
matches.target_text:
A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical equipment that has been de-energised to allow electrical work to be carried out on it is not inadvertently re-energised while the work is being carried out. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning: The provision requires the PCBU to ensure de-energised electrical equipment is not inadvertently re-energised while work is being carried out (i.e., implement effective isolation/lockout and re-energisation prevention controls). The policy focuses primarily on testing and fault-finding, especially in energised environments, and includes general coordination procedures for switching circuits on/off during testing. However, it does not explicitly require controls to prevent inadvertent re-energisation of de-energised equipment during work (e.g., lockout/tagout, securing isolation points, signage, verification of isolation, control of keys, or procedures to prevent others from re-energising). Therefore, the policy does not clearly fulfill the specific obligation in the provision.
compliance.remediation: Add a specific isolation and re-energisation prevention procedure for de-energised work, including: identify all energy sources; isolate and secure isolation points (lockout); apply tags/signage; control keys/access; verify isolation before starting and after interruptions; restrict access to switchboards/controls; require authorization/permit to re-energise; and define responsibilities and coordination/communication requirements.

matches.target_title: 149 Unsafe electrical equipment
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must ensure that any unsafe electrical equipment at the workplace: 

(a) is disconnected (or isolated) from its electricity supply; and (b) once disconnected (or isolated): 

(i) is not reconnected until it is repaired or tested and found to be safe; or (ii) is replaced or permanently removed from use. 

Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty. 

(2) For the purposes of this regulation, electrical equipment or a component of electrical equipment is unsafe if there are reasonable grounds for believing it to be unsafe.
