compliance.reasoning: The provision requires the PCBU to ensure unsafe electrical equipment is disconnected/isolated and not reconnected until repaired or tested and found safe, or replaced/removed. The policy focuses on safe testing and fault-finding practices (controls for energised testing, instrument standards, PPE, training, restoring circuits safely), but it does not explicitly require that unsafe electrical equipment be disconnected/isolated, nor does it state a prohibition on reconnection until repair/testing confirms safety, or replacement/removal. Because these specific control requirements are not clearly addressed, the policy does not fully satisfy the provision.
compliance.remediation: Add an explicit procedure requiring identification of unsafe electrical equipment, immediate disconnection/isolation from supply, tagging/lockout to prevent reconnection, and a rule that it must not be reconnected until repaired or tested and verified safe by a competent person, or else replaced/permanently removed. Define 'unsafe' (or adopt the regulation’s 'reasonable grounds' threshold) and assign responsibility to the PCBU/supervisors for enforcement and recordkeeping.

source_text:
When purchasing electrical equipment, you must ensure that the equipment meets applicable Australian Standards prior to it being commissioned such as AS/NZS 3820 Essential safety requirements for electrical equipment or equivalent international standards recognised within Australia. Electrical equipment that comply with Australia Standards will have a regulatory compliance mark (a triangle with a circled tick inside) displayed on the equipment.

This is a particular issue for equipment that is purchased internationally, so inspect newly acquired electrical equipment and if there is any doubt engage an electrician to inspect the equipment.

If the equipment is electrically unsafe return it to the supplier or arrange for the equipment to be modified and certified to meet Australian Standards.

matches.target_title: 150 Inspection and testing of electrical equipment
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must ensure that electrical equipment is regularly inspected and tested by a competent person if the electrical equipment is: 

(a) supplied with electricity through an electrical socket outlet; and   
(b) used in an environment in which the normal use of electrical equipment exposes the equipment to operating conditions that are likely to result in damage to the equipment or a reduction in its expected life span, including conditions that involve exposure to moisture, heat, vibration, mechanical damage, corrosive chemicals or dust. 

Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty. 

(2) In the case of electrical equipment that is new and unused at the workplace, the person conducting the business or undertaking: 

(a) is not required to comply with subregulation (1); and (b) must ensure that the equipment is inspected for obvious damage before being used. 

Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty. 

Note 

However, electrical equipment that is unsafe must not be used (see regulation 149). 

(3) The person must ensure that a record of any testing carried out under subregulation (1) is kept until the electrical equipment is: 

(a) next tested; or (b) permanently removed from the workplace or disposed of. 


Maximum penalty: tier I monetary penalty. 

(4) The record of testing: 

(a) must specify the following: (i) the name of the person who carried out the testing; (ii) the date of the testing; (iii) the outcome of the testing; (iv) the date on which the next testing must be carried out; and   
(b) may be in the form of a tag attached to the electrical equipment tested.

compliance.reasoning: The POLICY addresses ensuring purchased electrical equipment meets applicable Australian Standards prior to commissioning, inspecting newly acquired equipment (especially international purchases), engaging an electrician if in doubt, and returning/modifying unsafe equipment. The PROVISION requires (1) regular inspection and testing by a competent person for socket-outlet supplied equipment used in harsh/damaging environments, (2) for new/unused equipment, inspection for obvious damage before use, and (3)-(4) keeping specified testing records until next test/removal. The POLICY partially aligns with subregulation (2)(b) by requiring inspection of newly acquired equipment and escalation to an electrician, but it does not establish a program for 'regularly inspected and tested' in the specified environments, does not require testing by a competent person, and does not require keeping testing records with the mandated details. Therefore it does not fully satisfy the PROVISION.
compliance.remediation: Add requirements to: (1) identify electrical equipment that is socket-outlet supplied and used in environments likely to cause damage (moisture/heat/vibration/mechanical damage/chemicals/dust); (2) implement a schedule for regular inspection and electrical testing (e.g., test-and-tag) by a competent person for that equipment; (3) require pre-use inspection for obvious damage for all new/unused equipment; (4) maintain testing records until the next test or until the equipment is removed/disposed, including tester name, test date, outcome, and next test due date (tagging acceptable).

matches.target_title: 144 Meaning of electrical equipment
matches.target_text:
(1) In this Part, electrical equipment means any apparatus, appliance, cable, conductor, fitting, insulator, material, meter or wire that: 

(a) is used for controlling, generating, supplying, transforming or transmitting electricity at a voltage greater than extra-low voltage; or   
(b) is operated by electricity at a voltage greater than extra-low voltage; or   
(c) is part of an electrical installation located in an area in which the atmosphere presents a risk to health and safety from fire or explosion; or   
(d) is, or is part of, an active impressed current cathodic protection system within the meaning of AS 2832.1:2015 (Cathodic protection of metals—Pipes and cables). 

(2) In this Part, electrical equipment does not include any apparatus, appliance, cable, conductor, fitting, insulator, material, meter or wire that is part of a vehicle that is a motor car or motorcycle if: 

(a) the equipment is part of a unit of the vehicle that provides propulsion for the vehicle; or   
(b) the electricity source for the equipment is a unit of the vehicle that provides propulsion for the vehicle. 

Note 

See the jurisdictional note in the Appendix.

compliance.reasoning: The POLICY sets obligations for purchasing/commissioning electrical equipment to ensure it meets applicable Australian Standards and to inspect/engage an electrician if in doubt. The PROVISION text, however, only defines what counts as “electrical equipment” (and exclusions for certain vehicle equipment) for the purposes of a regulatory Part; it does not impose any requirement about standards compliance, inspection, certification, or commissioning. Because the PROVISION is definitional only, there is no direct obligation in it that can be checked for compliance against the POLICY. The texts are related in scope (they concern electrical equipment), but a compliance determination cannot be made from this PROVISION alone.

matches.target_title: 163 Duty of person conducting business or undertaking
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking that includes the carrying out of construction work must comply with AS/NZS 3012:2019 (Electrical installations—Construction and demolition sites). Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.   
(2) For the purposes of subregulation (1), AS/NZS 3012:2019 (Electrical installations— Construction and demolition sites) applies as if any term that is defined in that Standard and that is also defined in the Act or these Regulations has the same meaning as it has in the Act or these Regulations.   
(3) If any requirement in AS/NZS 3012:2019 (Electrical installations— Construction and demolition sites) deals with the same matter as a requirement under this Part, it is sufficient that the person conducting the business or undertaking complies with the requirement in AS/NZS 3012:2019 as modified by subregulation (2).

compliance.reasoning: The provision imposes a specific legal obligation on a PCBU carrying out construction work to comply with AS/NZS 3012:2019 for electrical installations on construction and demolition sites. The policy addresses procurement/commissioning of electrical equipment to applicable Australian Standards (e.g., AS/NZS 3820) and inspection/rectification of unsafe equipment, but it does not reference AS/NZS 3012:2019, does not set requirements for construction-site electrical installations, and does not establish controls to ensure compliance with AS/NZS 3012 where construction work is performed. Therefore, the policy does not demonstrate compliance with the provision’s specific standard-based requirement.
compliance.remediation: Update the policy (or add a construction electrical installations procedure) to explicitly require compliance with AS/NZS 3012:2019 for any construction/demolition site electrical installations, including roles/responsibilities, verification/inspection and testing regimes, documentation/records, and contractor management to ensure AS/NZS 3012 compliance in addition to equipment procurement standards.

matches.target_title: 15 References to standards
matches.target_text:
(1) In these Regulations, a reference consisting of the words "Australian Standard" or the letters "AS" followed in either case by a number or a number accompanied by a reference to a calendar year is a reference to the standard so numbered published by or on behalf of Standards Australia.

(2) In these Regulations, a reference consisting of the expression "Australian/New Zealand Standard" or "AS/NZS" followed in either case by a number or a number accompanied by a reference to a calendar year is a reference to the standard so numbered published jointly by or on behalf of Standards Australia and the Standards Council of New Zealand.

Notes

1 A person is only required to comply with an Australian Standard or Australian/New Zealand Standard that is applied, adopted or incorporated by, or otherwise referred to in, a regulation to the extent the regulation provides, whether expressly or by necessary implication. For example, in regulation 5, definition boiler, there is an exception in paragraph (e)(iii). The definition does not require a person to comply with AS 2593:2021, but equipment must be certified in compliance with AS 2593:2021 in order to fall within that particular exception. The reference to an Australian Standard in this case is part of a description of equipment excluded from the definition boiler. As a result, the equipment is not covered by particular provisions of these Regulations relating to high risk work.

2 See the jurisdictional note in the Appendix.

compliance.reasoning: The POLICY requires electrical equipment to meet applicable Australian Standards (e.g., AS/NZS 3820) or equivalent international standards recognised in Australia before commissioning, and to inspect/engage an electrician and remediate (return/modify/certify) if unsafe. The PROVISION defines what references to “AS” and “AS/NZS” mean within the Regulations and clarifies that compliance with a Standard is only required where a regulation applies/adopts/incorporates or otherwise refers to it. The POLICY’s use of AS/NZS references is consistent with the PROVISION’s definitional framework and does not contradict the PROVISION’s limitation (it sets an internal procurement/commissioning control that can be stricter than the minimum regulatory trigger).

matches.target_title: 149 Unsafe electrical equipment
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must ensure that any unsafe electrical equipment at the workplace: 

(a) is disconnected (or isolated) from its electricity supply; and (b) once disconnected (or isolated): 

(i) is not reconnected until it is repaired or tested and found to be safe; or (ii) is replaced or permanently removed from use. 

Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty. 

(2) For the purposes of this regulation, electrical equipment or a component of electrical equipment is unsafe if there are reasonable grounds for believing it to be unsafe.

compliance.reasoning: The POLICY addresses ensuring purchased electrical equipment meets Australian Standards before commissioning, inspecting newly acquired equipment (especially international purchases), and returning/modifying/certifying equipment if found electrically unsafe. The PROVISION requires that any unsafe electrical equipment at the workplace is disconnected/isolated from supply and, once disconnected, is not reconnected until repaired or tested and found safe, or is replaced/permanently removed. The POLICY does not explicitly require immediate disconnection/isolation of unsafe equipment, does not prohibit reconnection until repair/testing confirms safety, and does not require replacement or permanent removal as an alternative control. Therefore, it does not fully meet the operational controls mandated by the PROVISION for handling unsafe electrical equipment at the workplace.
compliance.remediation: Update the policy/procedure to explicitly require that any electrical equipment suspected or believed to be unsafe at the workplace is immediately disconnected/isolated from the electricity supply, tagged/secured to prevent use, and not reconnected until it has been repaired or tested and found safe by a competent person; alternatively require replacement or permanent removal from use. Include clear responsibilities, steps for isolation/lockout-tagout (as applicable), and recordkeeping for testing/repair outcomes.

matches.target_title: 158 Preliminary steps
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure the following before electrical work on energised electrical equipment commences: 

(a) a risk assessment is conducted in relation to the proposed electrical work;   
(b) the area where the electrical work is to be carried out is clear of obstructions so as to allow for easy access and exit;   
(c) the point at which the electrical equipment can be disconnected or isolated from its electricity supply is: (i) clearly marked or labelled; and (ii) clear of obstructions so as to allow for easy access and exit by the worker who is to carry out the electrical work or any other competent person; and (iii) capable of being operated quickly;   
(d) the person authorises the electrical work after consulting with the person with management or control of the workplace. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(a), the risk assessment must be: 

(a) conducted by a competent person; and (b) recorded. 

(3) Subregulation (1)(c) does not apply to electrical work on electrical equipment if: 

(a) the work is to be carried out on the supply side of the main switch on the main switchboard for the equipment; and   
(b) the point at which the equipment can be disconnected from its electricity supply is not reasonably accessible from the work location.

matches.target_title: 155 Duty to determine whether equipment is energised
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that, before electrical work is carried out on electrical equipment, the equipment is tested by a competent person to determine whether or not it is energised. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

Note 

Regulation 157 allows electrical testing to be carried out on electrical equipment for the purposes of this regulation. Regulation 161 sets out how the testing is to be carried out. 

(2) The person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that: 

(a) each exposed part is treated as energised until it is isolated and determined not to be energised; and   
(b) each high-voltage exposed part is earthed after being deenergised. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning: The POLICY focuses on ensuring purchased electrical equipment meets Australian Standards (e.g., AS/NZS 3820), inspecting newly acquired equipment (especially imports), and returning/modifying unsafe equipment. The PROVISION requires that before electrical work is carried out on electrical equipment, it must be tested by a competent person to determine whether it is energised, and that exposed parts are treated as energised until isolated and proven de-energised (and high-voltage parts earthed after de-energising). While both concern electrical safety, the POLICY does not address the specific pre-work energisation testing requirement or the required safe work assumptions/earthing steps. Therefore, the POLICY does not fulfill the PROVISION’s obligations.
compliance.remediation: Update the policy/procedure to require that before any electrical work on equipment: (1) a competent person tests the equipment to confirm whether it is energised; (2) all exposed parts are treated as energised until isolated and proven de-energised; and (3) high-voltage exposed parts are earthed after de-energising. Include references to the applicable testing method requirements (e.g., procedures aligned with the relevant regulations/standards) and ensure electricians/competent persons are engaged to perform and document these steps.

matches.target_title: 161 How the work is to be carried out
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical work on energised electrical equipment is carried out: 

(a) by a competent person who has tools, testing equipment and personal protective equipment that: 

(i) are suitable for the work; and (ii) have been properly tested; and (iii) are maintained in good working order; and 

(b) in accordance with a safe work method statement prepared for the work; and   
(c) subject to subregulation (5), with a safety observer present who has the competence and qualifications specified in subregulation (4). 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) The person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure, so far as is reasonably practicable, that the person who carries out the electrical work uses the tools, testing equipment and personal protective equipment properly. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(3) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(b), the safe work method statement must: 

(a) identify the electrical work; and   
(b) specify hazards associated with that electrical work and risks associated with those hazards; and   
(c) describe the measures to be implemented to control the risks; and   
(d) describe how the risk control measures are to be implemented, monitored and reviewed. 

(4) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(c): 

(a) the safety observer must be competent: 

(b) the safety observer must have been assessed in the previous 12 months as competent to rescue and resuscitate a person. 

(5) A safety observer is not required if: 

(a) the work consists only of testing; and   
(b) the person conducting the business or undertaking has conducted a risk assessment under regulation 158(1)(a) that shows that there is no serious risk associated with the proposed work.

matches.target_title: 147 Risk management
matches.target_text:
A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must manage risks to health and safety associated with electrical risks at the workplace, in accordance with Part 3.1. 

Example 

Electrical risks associated with the design, construction, installation, protection, maintenance and testing of electrical equipment and electrical installations at a workplace. 

Note 

WHS Act—section 19 (see regulation 9).

compliance.reasoning: The provision imposes a general duty on a PCBU to manage risks to health and safety associated with electrical risks at the workplace. The policy operationalizes this duty for the procurement/commissioning stage by requiring electrical equipment to meet applicable Australian Standards (or recognised equivalent standards), requiring inspection of newly acquired (especially internationally purchased) equipment, engaging an electrician where doubt exists, and requiring unsafe equipment to be returned or modified and certified. These controls are consistent with managing electrical risks associated with the design/construction/installation and testing of electrical equipment, as contemplated by the provision.

matches.target_title: 164 Use of socket outlets in hostile operating environment
matches.target_text:
(1) This regulation applies in the following circumstances: 

(a) electrical equipment is used in an environment in which the normal use of electrical equipment exposes the equipment to operating conditions that are likely to result in damage to the equipment or a reduction in its expected life span, including conditions that involve exposure to moisture, heat, vibration, mechanical damage, corrosive chemicals or dust; 

(b) electrical equipment is moved between different locations in circumstances where damage to the equipment or to a flexible electricity supply cord is reasonably likely; 

(c) electrical equipment is frequently moved during its normal use; 

(d) electrical equipment forms part of, or is used in connection with, an amusement device. 

(2) In a circumstance set out in subregulation (1), a person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must ensure, so far as is reasonably practicable, that any electrical risk associated with the supply of electricity to the electrical equipment through a socket outlet is minimised by the use of an appropriate residual current device. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(3) Without limiting subregulation (2), the residual current device must have a tripping current that does not exceed 30 milliamps if electricity is supplied to the equipment through a socket outlet not exceeding 20 amps. 

(4) Subregulation (2) does not apply if the supply of electricity to the electrical equipment: 

(a) does not exceed 50 volts alternating current; or 

(b) is direct current; or 

(c) is provided through an isolating transformer that provides at least an equivalent level of protection; or 

(d) is provided from a non-earthed socket outlet supplied by an isolated winding portable generator that provides at least an equivalent level of protection.

compliance.reasoning: The POLICY addresses procurement/commissioning safety by requiring electrical equipment to meet applicable Australian Standards (e.g., AS/NZS 3820), inspection of newly acquired (especially imported) equipment, and returning/modifying unsafe equipment. The PROVISION imposes an operational control requirement in specified higher-risk use circumstances (harsh environments, frequent movement, amusement devices): the PCBU must, so far as reasonably practicable, minimise electrical risk from socket-outlet supply by using an appropriate residual current device (RCD), generally with ≤30 mA trip current for socket outlets ≤20 A, unless an exception applies (≤50 VAC, DC, isolating transformer, or isolated generator). The POLICY does not mention RCD use, the specified triggering circumstances, the 30 mA/20 A requirement, or the listed exceptions. Therefore, while both concern electrical safety, the POLICY does not fulfill the PROVISION’s specific obligation to ensure appropriate RCD protection in the stated circumstances, so it is not compliant with the PROVISION.
compliance.remediation: Update the policy/procedure to require that, where electrical equipment is used in the circumstances listed in the regulation (moisture/heat/vibration/mechanical damage/corrosives/dust exposure; moved between locations with likely damage; frequently moved; amusement devices), the PCBU ensures (so far as reasonably practicable) electrical risk from socket-outlet supply is minimised by an appropriate RCD. Specify that for socket outlets ≤20 A the RCD must trip at ≤30 mA, and document the allowable exceptions (≤50 VAC, DC, isolating transformer providing equivalent protection, or non-earthed isolated-generator supply providing equivalent protection). Include implementation steps (selection, installation, testing/verification, and responsibility assignment) to demonstrate compliance.

source_text:
Modifications made to electrical equipment such as repairing electrical wiring or replacing faulty parts must only be carried out by a competent person.
Upon completion of the work, the equipment must be electrically tested and tagged and certified prior to it being commissioned.

matches.target_title: 155 Duty to determine whether equipment is energised
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that, before electrical work is carried out on electrical equipment, the equipment is tested by a competent person to determine whether or not it is energised. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

Note 

Regulation 157 allows electrical testing to be carried out on electrical equipment for the purposes of this regulation. Regulation 161 sets out how the testing is to be carried out. 

(2) The person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that: 

(a) each exposed part is treated as energised until it is isolated and determined not to be energised; and   
(b) each high-voltage exposed part is earthed after being deenergised. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning: The provision requires that before electrical work is carried out, the equipment is tested by a competent person to determine whether it is energised, and that exposed parts are treated as energised until isolated and proven de-energised (with additional earthing requirements for high-voltage exposed parts). The policy focuses on who may perform modifications (a competent person) and requires electrical testing/tagging/certification only upon completion prior to commissioning. It does not require pre-work testing to confirm de-energisation, does not state the 'treat as energised until proven otherwise' rule, and does not address earthing of high-voltage exposed parts after de-energisation. Therefore it does not meet the provision's pre-work safety testing and handling requirements.
compliance.remediation: Update the policy to require that, before any electrical work begins, a competent person tests the equipment to confirm whether it is energised; require that all exposed parts are treated as energised until isolated and verified de-energised; and include a requirement that any high-voltage exposed parts are earthed after de-energisation. Reference or align procedures with the applicable testing method requirements (e.g., per the relevant regulations/standards) and ensure workers are trained and records kept.

matches.target_title: 150 Inspection and testing of electrical equipment
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must ensure that electrical equipment is regularly inspected and tested by a competent person if the electrical equipment is: 

(a) supplied with electricity through an electrical socket outlet; and   
(b) used in an environment in which the normal use of electrical equipment exposes the equipment to operating conditions that are likely to result in damage to the equipment or a reduction in its expected life span, including conditions that involve exposure to moisture, heat, vibration, mechanical damage, corrosive chemicals or dust. 

Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty. 

(2) In the case of electrical equipment that is new and unused at the workplace, the person conducting the business or undertaking: 

(a) is not required to comply with subregulation (1); and (b) must ensure that the equipment is inspected for obvious damage before being used. 

Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty. 

Note 

However, electrical equipment that is unsafe must not be used (see regulation 149). 

(3) The person must ensure that a record of any testing carried out under subregulation (1) is kept until the electrical equipment is: 

(a) next tested; or (b) permanently removed from the workplace or disposed of. 


Maximum penalty: tier I monetary penalty. 

(4) The record of testing: 

(a) must specify the following: (i) the name of the person who carried out the testing; (ii) the date of the testing; (iii) the outcome of the testing; (iv) the date on which the next testing must be carried out; and   
(b) may be in the form of a tag attached to the electrical equipment tested.

compliance.reasoning: The POLICY addresses electrical work modifications/repairs being done by a competent person and requires post-work electrical testing/tagging/certification before commissioning. The PROVISION requires that certain electrical equipment (plug-in equipment used in harsh/damaging environments) be regularly inspected and tested by a competent person, and that records of testing be kept with specified details (often via a tag). While the POLICY aligns with the 'competent person' and 'tested and tagged' concepts, it does not ensure (1) regular inspection/testing based on the specified risk environment criteria, (2) inspection of new/unused equipment for obvious damage before first use, or (3) retention of testing records until next test/removal and inclusion of required record fields (tester name, test date, outcome, next test due date). Therefore, the POLICY is not fully compliant with the PROVISION.
compliance.remediation: Update the policy to: (1) require regular inspection and testing by a competent person for plug-in electrical equipment used in environments likely to cause damage (moisture/heat/vibration/mechanical damage/corrosive chemicals/dust); (2) require new/unused equipment to be inspected for obvious damage before first use; (3) require keeping testing records until the next test or until the equipment is removed/disposed, and ensure records include tester name, test date, outcome, and next test due date (tag acceptable); and (4) explicitly prohibit use of unsafe electrical equipment.

matches.target_title: 161 How the work is to be carried out
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical work on energised electrical equipment is carried out: 

(a) by a competent person who has tools, testing equipment and personal protective equipment that: 

(i) are suitable for the work; and (ii) have been properly tested; and (iii) are maintained in good working order; and 

(b) in accordance with a safe work method statement prepared for the work; and   
(c) subject to subregulation (5), with a safety observer present who has the competence and qualifications specified in subregulation (4). 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) The person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure, so far as is reasonably practicable, that the person who carries out the electrical work uses the tools, testing equipment and personal protective equipment properly. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(3) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(b), the safe work method statement must: 

(a) identify the electrical work; and   
(b) specify hazards associated with that electrical work and risks associated with those hazards; and   
(c) describe the measures to be implemented to control the risks; and   
(d) describe how the risk control measures are to be implemented, monitored and reviewed. 

(4) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(c): 

(a) the safety observer must be competent: 

(b) the safety observer must have been assessed in the previous 12 months as competent to rescue and resuscitate a person. 

(5) A safety observer is not required if: 

(a) the work consists only of testing; and   
(b) the person conducting the business or undertaking has conducted a risk assessment under regulation 158(1)(a) that shows that there is no serious risk associated with the proposed work.

compliance.reasoning: The policy addresses who may perform modifications/repairs to electrical equipment (competent person) and requires post-work electrical testing/tagging/certification before commissioning. The provision regulates electrical work on energised electrical equipment and requires (1) competent person with suitable/tested/maintained tools/testing equipment/PPE, (2) proper use of that equipment, (3) a safe work method statement (SWMS) with specified contents, and (4) a safety observer meeting competency/rescue/resuscitation requirements unless an exception applies. The policy partially aligns with the 'competent person' requirement but does not address key mandatory elements for energised electrical work: SWMS preparation/content, safety observer requirements/exception conditions, and requirements around tools/testing equipment/PPE suitability, testing, maintenance, and proper use. Therefore it does not fully satisfy the provision.
compliance.remediation: Update the policy/procedure for work on energised electrical equipment to: (1) require a SWMS for energised electrical work that identifies the work, hazards/risks, controls, and how controls are implemented/monitored/reviewed; (2) require a safety observer meeting the specified competency and recent (within 12 months) rescue/resuscitation assessment, unless the work is only testing and a documented risk assessment shows no serious risk; (3) require that the competent person has and uses suitable tools/testing equipment/PPE that are properly tested and maintained in good working order, and that the PCBU ensures proper use so far as reasonably practicable; and (4) clarify scope/definitions to explicitly cover 'energised electrical work' in addition to general modifications/repairs.

matches.target_title: 158 Preliminary steps
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure the following before electrical work on energised electrical equipment commences: 

(a) a risk assessment is conducted in relation to the proposed electrical work;   
(b) the area where the electrical work is to be carried out is clear of obstructions so as to allow for easy access and exit;   
(c) the point at which the electrical equipment can be disconnected or isolated from its electricity supply is: (i) clearly marked or labelled; and (ii) clear of obstructions so as to allow for easy access and exit by the worker who is to carry out the electrical work or any other competent person; and (iii) capable of being operated quickly;   
(d) the person authorises the electrical work after consulting with the person with management or control of the workplace. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(a), the risk assessment must be: 

(a) conducted by a competent person; and (b) recorded. 

(3) Subregulation (1)(c) does not apply to electrical work on electrical equipment if: 

(a) the work is to be carried out on the supply side of the main switch on the main switchboard for the equipment; and   
(b) the point at which the equipment can be disconnected from its electricity supply is not reasonably accessible from the work location.

compliance.reasoning: The POLICY governs modifications/repairs to electrical equipment, requiring (i) work be performed by a competent person and (ii) post-work electrical testing, tagging, and certification before commissioning. The PROVISION sets mandatory preconditions before electrical work on energised electrical equipment commences, including conducting and recording a risk assessment by a competent person, ensuring clear access/egress and isolation points, and authorisation after consultation. While the POLICY aligns partially with the competency concept, it does not address the PROVISION’s required pre-work controls (risk assessment and recording, access/egress and isolation point requirements, and authorisation/consultation). Therefore, the POLICY does not fully satisfy the PROVISION’s obligations for energised electrical work.
compliance.remediation: Update the POLICY (or add a linked procedure) to require, before commencing energised electrical work: (1) a documented risk assessment conducted by a competent person; (2) verification that the work area and isolation/disconnection point are clearly labelled, unobstructed, quickly operable, and allow easy access/exit; and (3) formal authorisation of the work after consultation with the person with management/control of the workplace. Include applicability/exceptions consistent with the provision (e.g., supply side of main switch where isolation point not reasonably accessible).

matches.target_title: 240 Maintenance, inspection and testing of amusement devices and passenger ropeways
matches.target_text:
(1) The person with management or control of an amusement device or passenger ropeway at a workplace must ensure that the maintenance, inspection and, if necessary, testing of the device or ropeway is carried out:

(a) by a competent person; and (b) in accordance with:

(i) the recommendations of the designer or manufacturer or designer and manufacturer; or

(ii) if a maintenance manual for the device or ropeway has been prepared by a competent person, the requirements of the maintenance manual.

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

(2) A person is not a competent person to carry out a detailed inspection of an amusement device or passenger ropeway that includes an electrical installation unless the person is qualified, or is assisted by a person who is qualified, to inspect electrical installations.

compliance.reasoning: The policy addresses electrical modifications to equipment, requiring work by a competent person and post-work electrical testing/tagging/certification before commissioning. The provision requires that maintenance/inspection/testing of an amusement device or passenger ropeway be carried out by a competent person and in accordance with manufacturer/designer recommendations or a competent-person-prepared maintenance manual. It also adds a specific competency requirement for detailed inspections involving electrical installations: the inspector must be qualified to inspect electrical installations or assisted by someone who is. The policy does not require compliance with manufacturer/designer recommendations or a maintenance manual, and it does not address the additional qualification/assistance requirement for detailed inspections of electrical installations. Therefore, the policy is not fully aligned with the provision.
compliance.remediation: Update the policy to (1) require maintenance/inspection/testing to be performed in accordance with manufacturer/designer recommendations or an approved maintenance manual prepared by a competent person, and (2) specify that any detailed inspection involving an electrical installation must be performed by a person qualified to inspect electrical installations or by a competent person assisted by such a qualified person. Clarify applicability to amusement devices/passenger ropeways if within scope.

matches.target_title: 146 Meaning of electrical work

compliance.reasoning: The policy governs who may perform modifications/repairs to electrical equipment and requires post-work electrical testing/tagging/certification before commissioning. The provision defines what constitutes “electrical work” and lists exclusions where tasks are not considered electrical work (e.g., replacing a fuse/light bulb, certain non-electrical component work, manufacturing process repairs). Because the policy applies broadly to “modifications” such as repairing wiring or replacing faulty parts, it would capture some activities that the provision explicitly excludes from “electrical work” and therefore may not require a competent/licensed electrical worker under the provision. The policy does not distinguish between electrical work and excluded tasks, creating a mismatch/overreach relative to the provision’s scope. Additionally, the policy requires testing/tagging/certification for all such modifications, while the provision text provided does not impose that requirement; the policy may be stricter, but it is not aligned to the provision’s defined scope.
compliance.remediation: Amend the policy to align scope with the provision’s definition of “electrical work” and its exclusions. For example: (1) specify that tasks meeting the definition of electrical work must be performed by an appropriately competent/licensed person; (2) explicitly carve out excluded activities (e.g., replacing a fuse/light bulb, non-electrical component work without electrical risk) and state any internal controls for those tasks; (3) clarify when electrical testing/tagging/certification is required (e.g., after electrical work or before commissioning where applicable) and reference the applicable regulatory/testing standard if required.

matches.target_title: 149 Unsafe electrical equipment
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must ensure that any unsafe electrical equipment at the workplace: 

(a) is disconnected (or isolated) from its electricity supply; and (b) once disconnected (or isolated): 

(i) is not reconnected until it is repaired or tested and found to be safe; or (ii) is replaced or permanently removed from use. 

Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty. 

(2) For the purposes of this regulation, electrical equipment or a component of electrical equipment is unsafe if there are reasonable grounds for believing it to be unsafe.

compliance.reasoning: The provision requires that unsafe electrical equipment be disconnected/isolated and not reconnected until it is repaired or tested and found to be safe, or replaced/removed. The policy addresses who may perform modifications/repairs (a competent person) and requires post-work electrical testing/tagging/certification before commissioning. However, it does not explicitly require immediate disconnection/isolation of unsafe equipment, nor does it explicitly prohibit reconnection/use until repaired or tested and found safe (it only speaks to commissioning after modifications). It also does not address replacement or permanent removal as an alternative control. Therefore, the policy only partially aligns and does not fully satisfy the provision’s requirements.
compliance.remediation: Update the policy to explicitly require that any unsafe electrical equipment be immediately disconnected/isolated from the electricity supply, and that it must not be reconnected or used until it has been repaired or tested and found safe by an appropriate person. Include clear options/processes for replacement or permanent removal from use, and define/describe how equipment is identified as 'unsafe' (e.g., reasonable grounds, inspection triggers, reporting).

matches.target_title: 156 De-energised equipment must not be inadvertently re-energised
matches.target_text:
A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical equipment that has been de-energised to allow electrical work to be carried out on it is not inadvertently re-energised while the work is being carried out. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning: The provision requires the PCBU to ensure de-energised electrical equipment is not inadvertently re-energised while electrical work is being carried out (i.e., isolation/lockout and control of re-energisation). The policy addresses who may perform modifications (competent person) and post-work testing/tagging/certification prior to commissioning, but it does not include any requirement or control to prevent inadvertent re-energisation during the work. Therefore, the policy does not satisfy the provision’s obligation.
compliance.remediation: Amend the policy/procedure to require controls preventing inadvertent re-energisation during electrical work, such as isolation at the source, lockout/tagout (LOTO), securing isolation points, warning signage, verification of de-energisation before work, control of keys/access, and a process for re-energisation only after work completion and authorization.

matches.target_title: 213 Maintenance and inspection of plant
matches.target_text:
(1) The person with management or control of plant at a workplace must ensure that the maintenance, inspection and, if necessary, testing of the plant is carried out by a competent person. Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty.

(2) The maintenance, inspection and testing must be carried out:

(a) in accordance with the manufacturer's recommendations, if any; or   
(b) if there are no manufacturer's recommendations, in accordance with the recommendations of a competent person; or   
(c) in relation to inspection, if it is not reasonably practicable to comply with paragraph (a) or (b), annually.

compliance.reasoning: The POLICY addresses maintenance/modification of electrical equipment by requiring work be done by a competent person and requiring post-work electrical testing/tagging/certification before commissioning. The PROVISION requires the person with management or control of plant to ensure maintenance/inspection and, if necessary, testing is carried out by a competent person, and that such activities follow manufacturer recommendations (or competent-person recommendations, or annual inspection if not practicable). While the POLICY aligns with the competent-person requirement and includes a testing requirement after work, it does not state that maintenance/inspection/testing will be conducted in accordance with manufacturer recommendations (or an alternative schedule as specified). It also does not clearly cover ongoing inspection/maintenance requirements beyond post-modification testing. Therefore, the POLICY does not fully satisfy the PROVISION.
compliance.remediation: Amend the POLICY to require that maintenance, inspection and any necessary testing of electrical equipment/plant be performed in accordance with the manufacturer’s recommendations; if none exist, follow recommendations of a competent person; and if not reasonably practicable for inspection, conduct inspections at least annually. Also clarify responsibility of the person with management/control to ensure these activities occur (not only after modifications).

matches.target_title: 157 Electrical work on energised electrical equipment—when permitted
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical work on energised electrical equipment is not carried out unless: 

(a) it is necessary in the interests of health and safety that the electrical work is carried out on the equipment while the equipment is energised; or Example 

It may be necessary that life-saving equipment remain energised and operating while electrical work is carried out on the equipment.   
(b) it is necessary that the electrical equipment to be worked on is energised in order for the work to be carried out properly; or   
(c) it is necessary for the purposes of testing required under regulation 155; or   
(d) there is no reasonable alternative means of carrying out the work. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) The electrical work that may be carried out under subregulation (1)(a), (b) and (d) may include testing of the energised electrical equipment.

compliance.reasoning: The POLICY governs modifications/repairs to electrical equipment (competent person; test/tag/certify before commissioning). The PROVISION specifically regulates electrical work on energised electrical equipment, requiring that such work not be carried out unless one of the listed necessity conditions applies (health/safety necessity, required for proper work, required testing, or no reasonable alternative). The POLICY does not address whether work is performed on energised equipment, does not prohibit energised work by default, and does not require the necessity/justification conditions or controls for energised electrical work. Therefore, the POLICY does not demonstrate compliance with the PROVISION’s restriction on energised electrical work.
compliance.remediation: Amend the policy to explicitly prohibit electrical work on energised equipment except where one of the permitted conditions applies (health/safety necessity, required for proper work, required testing, or no reasonable alternative). Require documented justification for energised work, and include procedural controls (e.g., de-energise/isolations as default, authorisation process for energised work, and testing allowances consistent with the regulation).

source_text:
It is important that electrical equipment is made safe before it is disposed of to prevent any risk of injury to workers or public when handling the equipment.

Prior to disposing of electrical equipment, consider the following:

 How can the equipment be made safe and ready for disposal i.e. can the electrical cord be cut/removed to prevent further use?   
 Does the equipment contain chemicals or radiation sources that need to be managed before it is disposed?   
 Can the equipment be disposed of through an e-waste stream?   
 Is there an alternate energy source within the equipment that retains residual energy, i.e. a capacitor?

matches.target_title: 149 Unsafe electrical equipment
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must ensure that any unsafe electrical equipment at the workplace: 

(a) is disconnected (or isolated) from its electricity supply; and (b) once disconnected (or isolated): 

(i) is not reconnected until it is repaired or tested and found to be safe; or (ii) is replaced or permanently removed from use. 

Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty. 

(2) For the purposes of this regulation, electrical equipment or a component of electrical equipment is unsafe if there are reasonable grounds for believing it to be unsafe.

compliance.reasoning: The provision requires the PCBU to ensure unsafe electrical equipment is disconnected/isolated from the electricity supply and, once disconnected, is not reconnected until repaired/tested safe or is replaced/permanently removed from use. The policy focuses on making equipment safe before disposal (e.g., cutting/removing cords, managing chemicals/radiation, e-waste stream, residual energy) but does not explicitly require disconnection/isolating from supply, controls to prevent reconnection, or a clear requirement to repair/test before reconnection versus permanent removal. Therefore it does not fully satisfy the mandatory controls in the provision.
compliance.remediation: Update the policy to explicitly require that any unsafe electrical equipment is immediately disconnected/isolated from the electricity supply, tagged/secured to prevent reconnection, and either (a) not reconnected until repaired or tested and confirmed safe, or (b) replaced or permanently removed from use (including disposal). Define/align criteria for when equipment is considered 'unsafe' (e.g., reasonable grounds) and include procedural steps and responsibilities.

matches.target_title: 158 Preliminary steps
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure the following before electrical work on energised electrical equipment commences: 

(a) a risk assessment is conducted in relation to the proposed electrical work;   
(b) the area where the electrical work is to be carried out is clear of obstructions so as to allow for easy access and exit;   
(c) the point at which the electrical equipment can be disconnected or isolated from its electricity supply is: (i) clearly marked or labelled; and (ii) clear of obstructions so as to allow for easy access and exit by the worker who is to carry out the electrical work or any other competent person; and (iii) capable of being operated quickly;   
(d) the person authorises the electrical work after consulting with the person with management or control of the workplace. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(a), the risk assessment must be: 

(a) conducted by a competent person; and (b) recorded. 

(3) Subregulation (1)(c) does not apply to electrical work on electrical equipment if: 

(a) the work is to be carried out on the supply side of the main switch on the main switchboard for the equipment; and   
(b) the point at which the equipment can be disconnected from its electricity supply is not reasonably accessible from the work location.

matches.target_title: 155 Duty to determine whether equipment is energised
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that, before electrical work is carried out on electrical equipment, the equipment is tested by a competent person to determine whether or not it is energised. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

Note 

Regulation 157 allows electrical testing to be carried out on electrical equipment for the purposes of this regulation. Regulation 161 sets out how the testing is to be carried out. 

(2) The person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that: 

(a) each exposed part is treated as energised until it is isolated and determined not to be energised; and   
(b) each high-voltage exposed part is earthed after being deenergised. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning: Error after 3 attempts: Connection error.

matches.target_title: 150 Inspection and testing of electrical equipment
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must ensure that electrical equipment is regularly inspected and tested by a competent person if the electrical equipment is: 

(a) supplied with electricity through an electrical socket outlet; and   
(b) used in an environment in which the normal use of electrical equipment exposes the equipment to operating conditions that are likely to result in damage to the equipment or a reduction in its expected life span, including conditions that involve exposure to moisture, heat, vibration, mechanical damage, corrosive chemicals or dust. 

Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty. 

(2) In the case of electrical equipment that is new and unused at the workplace, the person conducting the business or undertaking: 

(a) is not required to comply with subregulation (1); and (b) must ensure that the equipment is inspected for obvious damage before being used. 

Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty. 

Note 

However, electrical equipment that is unsafe must not be used (see regulation 149). 

(3) The person must ensure that a record of any testing carried out under subregulation (1) is kept until the electrical equipment is: 

(a) next tested; or (b) permanently removed from the workplace or disposed of. 


Maximum penalty: tier I monetary penalty. 

(4) The record of testing: 

(a) must specify the following: (i) the name of the person who carried out the testing; (ii) the date of the testing; (iii) the outcome of the testing; (iv) the date on which the next testing must be carried out; and   
(b) may be in the form of a tag attached to the electrical equipment tested.

compliance.reasoning: Error after 3 attempts: Connection error.

matches.target_title: 144 Meaning of electrical equipment
matches.target_text:
(1) In this Part, electrical equipment means any apparatus, appliance, cable, conductor, fitting, insulator, material, meter or wire that: 

(a) is used for controlling, generating, supplying, transforming or transmitting electricity at a voltage greater than extra-low voltage; or   
(b) is operated by electricity at a voltage greater than extra-low voltage; or   
(c) is part of an electrical installation located in an area in which the atmosphere presents a risk to health and safety from fire or explosion; or   
(d) is, or is part of, an active impressed current cathodic protection system within the meaning of AS 2832.1:2015 (Cathodic protection of metals—Pipes and cables). 

(2) In this Part, electrical equipment does not include any apparatus, appliance, cable, conductor, fitting, insulator, material, meter or wire that is part of a vehicle that is a motor car or motorcycle if: 

(a) the equipment is part of a unit of the vehicle that provides propulsion for the vehicle; or   
(b) the electricity source for the equipment is a unit of the vehicle that provides propulsion for the vehicle. 

Note 

See the jurisdictional note in the Appendix.

compliance.reasoning: Error after 3 attempts: Connection error.

matches.target_title: 147 Risk management
matches.target_text:
A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must manage risks to health and safety associated with electrical risks at the workplace, in accordance with Part 3.1. 

Example 

Electrical risks associated with the design, construction, installation, protection, maintenance and testing of electrical equipment and electrical installations at a workplace. 

Note 

WHS Act—section 19 (see regulation 9).

matches.target_title: 156 De-energised equipment must not be inadvertently re-energised
matches.target_text:
A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical equipment that has been de-energised to allow electrical work to be carried out on it is not inadvertently re-energised while the work is being carried out. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

matches.target_title: 161 How the work is to be carried out
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical work on energised electrical equipment is carried out: 

(a) by a competent person who has tools, testing equipment and personal protective equipment that: 

(i) are suitable for the work; and (ii) have been properly tested; and (iii) are maintained in good working order; and 

(b) in accordance with a safe work method statement prepared for the work; and   
(c) subject to subregulation (5), with a safety observer present who has the competence and qualifications specified in subregulation (4). 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) The person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure, so far as is reasonably practicable, that the person who carries out the electrical work uses the tools, testing equipment and personal protective equipment properly. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(3) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(b), the safe work method statement must: 

(a) identify the electrical work; and   
(b) specify hazards associated with that electrical work and risks associated with those hazards; and   
(c) describe the measures to be implemented to control the risks; and   
(d) describe how the risk control measures are to be implemented, monitored and reviewed. 

(4) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(c): 

(a) the safety observer must be competent: 

(b) the safety observer must have been assessed in the previous 12 months as competent to rescue and resuscitate a person. 

(5) A safety observer is not required if: 

(a) the work consists only of testing; and   
(b) the person conducting the business or undertaking has conducted a risk assessment under regulation 158(1)(a) that shows that there is no serious risk associated with the proposed work.

matches.target_title: 157 Electrical work on energised electrical equipment—when permitted
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical work on energised electrical equipment is not carried out unless: 

(a) it is necessary in the interests of health and safety that the electrical work is carried out on the equipment while the equipment is energised; or Example 

It may be necessary that life-saving equipment remain energised and operating while electrical work is carried out on the equipment.   
(b) it is necessary that the electrical equipment to be worked on is energised in order for the work to be carried out properly; or   
(c) it is necessary for the purposes of testing required under regulation 155; or   
(d) there is no reasonable alternative means of carrying out the work. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) The electrical work that may be carried out under subregulation (1)(a), (b) and (d) may include testing of the energised electrical equipment.

compliance.reasoning: Error after 3 attempts: Connection error.

source_text:
A person in control of electrical equipment at the workplace must ensure that the electrical equipment is regularly inspected and tested by a competent person if it is:

 supplied with electricity through an electrical socket outlet; and   
 is used in an environment in which the normal use of electrical equipment exposes it to an environment that are likely to result in damage to that equipment or it reduces its expected life (e.g. conditions that involve exposure to moisture, heat, vibration, mechanical damage, corrosive chemicals or dust.)

Visual inspections of electrical equipment should be carried out each time the equipment is used to help determine whether it is safe. Regular visual inspections can identify any signs of damage, wear and tear or other defects and if present (even if there is doubt), must be isolated or taken out of service until repaired and tested as safe.

Scheduled testing and tagging of electrical equipment can detect electrical faults and deterioration that cannot be detected by visual inspection. The frequency of testing must be conducted in accordance with AS3760 In-service safety inspection and testing of electrical equipment.

New Equipment

Brand-new electrical equipment that has never been put into use does not have to be tested before its first use. However, a ‘new to service’ tag must be attached to the equipment by a person with control or management of that equipment before it goes into service.

It is advisable to conduct a visual inspection of the electrical equipment to ensure that it hasn’t been damaged during transportation, delivery, installation or commissioning.

Once new electrical equipment has been connected for use, it will require testing and tagging at the next scheduled test interval.
