matches.target_title: 213 Maintenance and inspection of plant
matches.target_text:
(1) The person with management or control of plant at a workplace must ensure that the maintenance, inspection and, if necessary, testing of the plant is carried out by a competent person. Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty.

(2) The maintenance, inspection and testing must be carried out:

(a) in accordance with the manufacturer's recommendations, if any; or   
(b) if there are no manufacturer's recommendations, in accordance with the recommendations of a competent person; or   
(c) in relation to inspection, if it is not reasonably practicable to comply with paragraph (a) or (b), annually.

compliance.reasoning: The POLICY is functionally related because it sets requirements for inspection and testing of workplace electrical equipment (a type of plant) by a competent person, aligning with the PROVISION’s obligation to ensure maintenance/inspection/testing is carried out by a competent person. However, the POLICY does not ensure the PROVISION’s required basis and minimum frequency framework: it mandates testing frequency solely by AS3760 for certain socket-outlet equipment in damaging environments, but it does not (i) require maintenance/inspection/testing to be carried out in accordance with manufacturer recommendations (or competent-person recommendations where none exist), nor (ii) include the fallback requirement that inspection be carried out annually where compliance with (a) or (b) is not reasonably practicable. The POLICY also narrows scope to specific electrical equipment conditions rather than all plant under management/control, and it emphasizes inspection/testing but does not clearly cover 'maintenance' as required by the PROVISION.
compliance.remediation: Amend the POLICY to: (1) explicitly require maintenance, inspection and (if necessary) testing of plant/electrical equipment by a competent person; (2) state that maintenance/inspection/testing must follow manufacturer recommendations, or if none, a competent person’s recommendations; (3) add a fallback that if it is not reasonably practicable to follow (2) for inspection, inspection must occur at least annually; (4) clarify scope to all relevant plant under management/control (not only socket-outlet equipment in harsh environments), while retaining AS3760 as an acceptable method where applicable.

matches.target_title: 151 Untested electrical equipment not to be used
matches.target_text:
A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure, so far as is reasonably practicable, that electrical equipment is not used if the equipment: 

(a) is required to be tested under regulation 150; and (b) has not been tested. 

Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning: The provision prohibits use of electrical equipment that is required to be tested under regulation 150 but has not been tested. The policy establishes a system requiring regular inspection and scheduled testing/tagging by a competent person for socket-outlet supplied equipment used in damaging environments, requires isolation/out-of-service where defects are found, and sets testing frequency in accordance with AS3760. It also clarifies that brand-new equipment need not be tested before first use (consistent with the provision because it would not yet be 'required to be tested' under the relevant trigger), but must be tagged 'new to service' and then tested at the next scheduled interval. Overall, the policy supports ensuring equipment that requires testing is tested before continued use, aligning with the provision’s prohibition on using untested equipment when testing is required.

matches.target_title: 158 Preliminary steps
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure the following before electrical work on energised electrical equipment commences: 

(a) a risk assessment is conducted in relation to the proposed electrical work;   
(b) the area where the electrical work is to be carried out is clear of obstructions so as to allow for easy access and exit;   
(c) the point at which the electrical equipment can be disconnected or isolated from its electricity supply is: (i) clearly marked or labelled; and (ii) clear of obstructions so as to allow for easy access and exit by the worker who is to carry out the electrical work or any other competent person; and (iii) capable of being operated quickly;   
(d) the person authorises the electrical work after consulting with the person with management or control of the workplace. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(a), the risk assessment must be: 

(a) conducted by a competent person; and (b) recorded. 

(3) Subregulation (1)(c) does not apply to electrical work on electrical equipment if: 

(a) the work is to be carried out on the supply side of the main switch on the main switchboard for the equipment; and   
(b) the point at which the equipment can be disconnected from its electricity supply is not reasonably accessible from the work location.

matches.target_title: 165 Testing of residual current devices
matches.target_text:
(1) A person with management or control of a workplace must take all reasonable steps to ensure that residual current devices used at the workplace are tested regularly by a competent person to ensure that the devices are operating effectively. 

Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty. 

(2) The person must keep a record of all testing of a residual current device (other than any testing conducted daily) until the earlier of the following occurs: 

(a) the device is next tested;   
(b) the device is permanently removed from use. 

Maximum penalty: tier I monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning: The provision requires the workplace controller to ensure residual current devices (RCDs) are tested regularly by a competent person and to keep records of all RCD testing (other than daily testing) until the next test or removal from use. The policy focuses on inspection/testing/tagging of electrical equipment supplied via socket outlets in harsh environments and references AS3760 intervals, but it does not specifically address RCD testing requirements or the mandatory recordkeeping for RCD tests. Therefore, it does not fully fulfill the provision’s obligations.
compliance.remediation: Amend the policy to explicitly require: (1) regular testing of all workplace RCDs by a competent person to confirm effective operation (including defining/aligning the testing frequency with applicable requirements), and (2) retention of records for all non-daily RCD testing until the RCD is next tested or permanently removed from use. Include responsibility assignment and a recordkeeping process/template.

matches.target_title: 161 How the work is to be carried out
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical work on energised electrical equipment is carried out: 

(a) by a competent person who has tools, testing equipment and personal protective equipment that: 

(i) are suitable for the work; and (ii) have been properly tested; and (iii) are maintained in good working order; and 

(b) in accordance with a safe work method statement prepared for the work; and   
(c) subject to subregulation (5), with a safety observer present who has the competence and qualifications specified in subregulation (4). 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) The person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure, so far as is reasonably practicable, that the person who carries out the electrical work uses the tools, testing equipment and personal protective equipment properly. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(3) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(b), the safe work method statement must: 

(a) identify the electrical work; and   
(b) specify hazards associated with that electrical work and risks associated with those hazards; and   
(c) describe the measures to be implemented to control the risks; and   
(d) describe how the risk control measures are to be implemented, monitored and reviewed. 

(4) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(c): 

(a) the safety observer must be competent: 

(b) the safety observer must have been assessed in the previous 12 months as competent to rescue and resuscitate a person. 

(5) A safety observer is not required if: 

(a) the work consists only of testing; and   
(b) the person conducting the business or undertaking has conducted a risk assessment under regulation 158(1)(a) that shows that there is no serious risk associated with the proposed work.

matches.target_title: 149 Unsafe electrical equipment
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must ensure that any unsafe electrical equipment at the workplace: 

(a) is disconnected (or isolated) from its electricity supply; and (b) once disconnected (or isolated): 

(i) is not reconnected until it is repaired or tested and found to be safe; or (ii) is replaced or permanently removed from use. 

Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty. 

(2) For the purposes of this regulation, electrical equipment or a component of electrical equipment is unsafe if there are reasonable grounds for believing it to be unsafe.

compliance.reasoning: The provision requires that any electrical equipment that is unsafe (or reasonably believed to be unsafe) must be isolated/disconnected and not reconnected until repaired or tested and found safe, or else replaced/removed. The policy requires visual inspections each time equipment is used and states that if damage/wear/defects are present (even if there is doubt), the equipment must be isolated or taken out of service until repaired and tested as safe. This meets the provision’s core obligations to isolate unsafe equipment and prevent reconnection until it is repaired or tested safe (or removed).

matches.target_title: 147 Risk management
matches.target_text:
A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must manage risks to health and safety associated with electrical risks at the workplace, in accordance with Part 3.1. 

Example 

Electrical risks associated with the design, construction, installation, protection, maintenance and testing of electrical equipment and electrical installations at a workplace. 

Note 

WHS Act—section 19 (see regulation 9).

compliance.reasoning: The provision requires the PCBU to manage risks to health and safety associated with electrical risks at the workplace (per Part 3.1), including risks arising from maintenance and testing of electrical equipment. The policy establishes a risk-control system for electrical equipment used in higher-risk environments: it mandates regular inspection and testing by a competent person when equipment is socket-supplied and exposed to conditions likely to cause damage; requires visual checks each use and isolation of suspect equipment; requires scheduled testing/tagging aligned to AS/NZS 3760; and sets controls for new equipment (new-to-service tagging and inclusion in the next test interval). These measures are consistent with managing electrical risks through inspection, maintenance, and testing controls, satisfying the general duty described in the provision.

matches.target_title: 240 Maintenance, inspection and testing of amusement devices and passenger ropeways
matches.target_text:
(1) The person with management or control of an amusement device or passenger ropeway at a workplace must ensure that the maintenance, inspection and, if necessary, testing of the device or ropeway is carried out:

(a) by a competent person; and (b) in accordance with:

(i) the recommendations of the designer or manufacturer or designer and manufacturer; or

(ii) if a maintenance manual for the device or ropeway has been prepared by a competent person, the requirements of the maintenance manual.

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

(2) A person is not a competent person to carry out a detailed inspection of an amusement device or passenger ropeway that includes an electrical installation unless the person is qualified, or is assisted by a person who is qualified, to inspect electrical installations.

source_title: 9.1 Business Unit and Site Electrical Safety Officers
source_text:
The primary purpose of an ESO is to provide advice and support to the business unit regarding CSIRO electrical work requirements and safe electrical work practices.

The ESO is not responsible for managing or approving electrical work and safety activites on site and/or across a business unit.

A senior manager of a business unit/function MUST appoint a Site Electrical Safety Officer (Site ESO) at each site where recurring electrical work is carried out by staff members or affiliates within the business unit.

A senior manager of a business unit/function may also appoint a Business Unit Electrical Safety Officer (BU ESO) where recurring electrical work is carried out by staff members and affiliates across multiple sites within the business unit.

Note: A Site ESO does not need to be appointed for work on extra low voltage equipment or installations.

matches.target_title: 161 How the work is to be carried out
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical work on energised electrical equipment is carried out: 

(a) by a competent person who has tools, testing equipment and personal protective equipment that: 

(i) are suitable for the work; and (ii) have been properly tested; and (iii) are maintained in good working order; and 

(b) in accordance with a safe work method statement prepared for the work; and   
(c) subject to subregulation (5), with a safety observer present who has the competence and qualifications specified in subregulation (4). 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) The person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure, so far as is reasonably practicable, that the person who carries out the electrical work uses the tools, testing equipment and personal protective equipment properly. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(3) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(b), the safe work method statement must: 

(a) identify the electrical work; and   
(b) specify hazards associated with that electrical work and risks associated with those hazards; and   
(c) describe the measures to be implemented to control the risks; and   
(d) describe how the risk control measures are to be implemented, monitored and reviewed. 

(4) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(c): 

(a) the safety observer must be competent: 

(b) the safety observer must have been assessed in the previous 12 months as competent to rescue and resuscitate a person. 

(5) A safety observer is not required if: 

(a) the work consists only of testing; and   
(b) the person conducting the business or undertaking has conducted a risk assessment under regulation 158(1)(a) that shows that there is no serious risk associated with the proposed work.

compliance.reasoning: The texts are functionally related because both concern governance and safe conduct of electrical work. The provision imposes specific duties on the person conducting a business or undertaking (PCBU) to ensure energized electrical work is performed by a competent person with suitable/tested/maintained tools and PPE, in accordance with a safe work method statement (SWMS) meeting specified content requirements, and (subject to exceptions) with a competent safety observer assessed within the last 12 months for rescue/resuscitation. The policy, however, focuses on appointing Electrical Safety Officers (ESOs) to provide advice/support and explicitly states ESOs are not responsible for managing or approving electrical work and safety activities. It does not state or require the PCBU/business unit to ensure the mandated controls for energized work (competency, equipment/PPE suitability/testing/maintenance, SWMS requirements, safety observer requirements, risk assessment exception). Therefore, the policy does not demonstrate coverage of the provision’s operational compliance obligations and is insufficient on its face.
compliance.remediation: Amend or supplement the policy (or add a linked procedure) to explicitly require that energized electrical work is: (1) performed only by competent persons; (2) performed using suitable tools/testing equipment/PPE that are tested and maintained; (3) conducted under a SWMS that meets the regulation’s required contents; (4) conducted with a competent safety observer assessed within the last 12 months for rescue/resuscitation, unless the testing-only + risk assessment no-serious-risk exception applies; and (5) includes a requirement that the PCBU/management ensures proper use of tools/PPE so far as reasonably practicable. Define accountable roles (management/PCBU) for these controls, since ESOs are advisory only.

matches.target_title: 155 Duty to determine whether equipment is energised
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that, before electrical work is carried out on electrical equipment, the equipment is tested by a competent person to determine whether or not it is energised. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

Note 

Regulation 157 allows electrical testing to be carried out on electrical equipment for the purposes of this regulation. Regulation 161 sets out how the testing is to be carried out. 

(2) The person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that: 

(a) each exposed part is treated as energised until it is isolated and determined not to be energised; and   
(b) each high-voltage exposed part is earthed after being deenergised. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

matches.target_title: 158 Preliminary steps
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure the following before electrical work on energised electrical equipment commences: 

(a) a risk assessment is conducted in relation to the proposed electrical work;   
(b) the area where the electrical work is to be carried out is clear of obstructions so as to allow for easy access and exit;   
(c) the point at which the electrical equipment can be disconnected or isolated from its electricity supply is: (i) clearly marked or labelled; and (ii) clear of obstructions so as to allow for easy access and exit by the worker who is to carry out the electrical work or any other competent person; and (iii) capable of being operated quickly;   
(d) the person authorises the electrical work after consulting with the person with management or control of the workplace. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(a), the risk assessment must be: 

(a) conducted by a competent person; and (b) recorded. 

(3) Subregulation (1)(c) does not apply to electrical work on electrical equipment if: 

(a) the work is to be carried out on the supply side of the main switch on the main switchboard for the equipment; and   
(b) the point at which the equipment can be disconnected from its electricity supply is not reasonably accessible from the work location.

compliance.reasoning: The provision imposes preconditions that must be ensured before energised electrical work commences, including conducting and recording a competent-person risk assessment, ensuring access/egress and isolation points are clear/labelled/operable, and authorising the work after consultation with the person with management or control of the workplace. The policy text focuses on appointing Electrical Safety Officers (ESOs) and explicitly states the ESO is not responsible for managing or approving electrical work and safety activities. It does not assign responsibility to any role (e.g., PCBU/senior manager/authoriser) to ensure the provision’s required controls, nor does it require risk assessments to be conducted/recorded or define an authorisation/consultation process for energised work. Therefore, the policy does not demonstrate coverage of the provision’s obligations and is not compliant as written.
compliance.remediation: Amend the policy (or add a linked procedure) to explicitly require, before any energised electrical work starts: (1) a risk assessment conducted by a competent person and recorded; (2) the work area is clear for access/exit; (3) isolation/disconnection points are labelled, unobstructed, and quickly operable (including addressing the stated exception); and (4) a defined authorisation process that includes consultation with the person with management/control of the workplace. Assign clear accountability (e.g., PCBU/manager/permit issuer) for ensuring these steps occur, while keeping ESO as advisory if intended.

matches.target_title: 148 Electrical equipment and electrical installations to which this Division applies
matches.target_text: In this Division, a reference to electrical equipment or an electrical installation in relation to a person conducting a business or undertaking is a reference to electrical equipment or an electrical installation that is under the person's management or control.

compliance.reasoning: The provision defines the scope of what counts as 'electrical equipment' or an 'electrical installation' for a person conducting a business or undertaking (PCBU): it is equipment/installations under that person's management or control. The policy sets internal governance roles (ESO/Site ESO/BU ESO) for sites where recurring electrical work is carried out, but it does not address or incorporate the 'management or control' concept that determines which equipment/installations fall within the PCBU’s responsibility. Because the provision is definitional and the policy does not make claims about what equipment/installations are under CSIRO’s management/control, a direct compliance determination cannot be made from the provided policy text alone.
compliance.remediation: Clarify in the policy (or a referenced procedure) that ESO advice/support and appointment triggers apply to electrical equipment/installations under CSIRO’s (the PCBU’s) management or control, and define how 'management or control' is determined for shared/leased/client sites.

matches.target_title: 160 Contact with equipment being worked on
matches.target_text:
A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that, while electrical work is being carried out on energised electrical equipment, all persons are prevented from creating an electrical risk by inadvertently making contact with an exposed energised component of the equipment. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

matches.target_title: 147 Risk management
matches.target_text:
A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must manage risks to health and safety associated with electrical risks at the workplace, in accordance with Part 3.1. 

Example 

Electrical risks associated with the design, construction, installation, protection, maintenance and testing of electrical equipment and electrical installations at a workplace. 

Note 

WHS Act—section 19 (see regulation 9).

compliance.reasoning: The provision requires the PCBU to manage electrical health and safety risks at the workplace in accordance with the WHS risk management requirements (Part 3.1). The policy focuses on appointing Electrical Safety Officers (ESOs) to provide advice and support, and explicitly states ESOs are not responsible for managing or approving electrical work and safety activities. It does not state that the business unit/PCBU must manage electrical risks, does not reference Part 3.1 risk management duties, and does not describe required risk management processes (e.g., hazard identification, risk assessment, control implementation, review) for electrical risks. Therefore, the policy does not adequately implement or assure compliance with the regulatory duty to manage electrical risks.
compliance.remediation: Amend the policy to explicitly assign responsibility to the PCBU/business unit management for managing electrical risks and require compliance with WHS risk management (Part 3.1), including processes for identifying electrical hazards, assessing risks, implementing and maintaining controls (e.g., isolation/LOTO, RCDs, competent persons, maintenance/testing), and reviewing control effectiveness. Clarify ESO roles as advisory/support while ensuring accountable managers are designated for risk management and approval/oversight of electrical safety activities.

matches.target_title: 157 Electrical work on energised electrical equipment—when permitted
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical work on energised electrical equipment is not carried out unless: 

(a) it is necessary in the interests of health and safety that the electrical work is carried out on the equipment while the equipment is energised; or Example 

It may be necessary that life-saving equipment remain energised and operating while electrical work is carried out on the equipment.   
(b) it is necessary that the electrical equipment to be worked on is energised in order for the work to be carried out properly; or   
(c) it is necessary for the purposes of testing required under regulation 155; or   
(d) there is no reasonable alternative means of carrying out the work. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) The electrical work that may be carried out under subregulation (1)(a), (b) and (d) may include testing of the energised electrical equipment.

matches.target_title: 146 Meaning of electrical work

compliance.reasoning: The provision defines what constitutes “electrical work” and what is excluded. The policy sets requirements for appointing Electrical Safety Officers (ESOs) when “recurring electrical work” is carried out, but it does not define “electrical work” or align its scope/exclusions with the provision’s definition. The policy’s only explicit exclusion is “extra low voltage equipment or installations,” which is not the same as (and does not cover) the provision’s broader exclusions (e.g., plug-and-socket connection, non-electrical component work without electrical risk, replacing fuses/light bulbs, certain supervised assistance, etc.). This creates a mismatch/ambiguity: the policy may require ESO appointments for activities that are not “electrical work” under the provision, or may fail to clearly scope when appointments are required based on the legally defined term.
compliance.remediation: Amend the policy to incorporate or reference the provision’s definition of “electrical work” and its exclusions, and clarify that ESO appointment triggers apply to “electrical work” as defined (including explicit treatment of excluded activities). Update the extra-low-voltage note to ensure it does not conflict with, and is clearly distinguished from, the provision’s exclusions.

matches.target_title: 156 De-energised equipment must not be inadvertently re-energised
matches.target_text:
A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical equipment that has been de-energised to allow electrical work to be carried out on it is not inadvertently re-energised while the work is being carried out. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

matches.target_title: 166 Duty of person conducting a business or undertaking
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must ensure, so far as is reasonably practicable, that no person, plant or thing at the workplace comes within an unsafe distance of an overhead or underground electric line. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) If it is not reasonably practicable to ensure the safe distance of a person, plant or thing from an overhead or underground electric line, the person conducting the business or undertaking at the workplace must ensure that:
(a) a risk assessment is conducted in relation to the proposed work; and 

(b) control measures implemented are consistent with: 

(i) the risk assessment; and 

(ii) if an electricity supply authority is responsible for the electric line, any requirements of the authority. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

Notes 

1 [Relevant electricity safety legislation] will also apply to the person conducting the business or undertaking.

source_text:
Individuals appointed to a BU or Site ESO role must have a level of training, qualification, licencing, etc that is commensurate to the competencies required for electrical work that is carried out on the site they primarily work at.

Example - ESO competency

CASS conducts electrical work on 2 different sites Marsfield and Narrabri.

At Marsfield they conduct work on portable electrical equipment and at Narrabri they conduct work on fixed and portable electrical equipment. Based on this information, a BU or Site ESO located at:

 Marsfield will need to have completed an electrical competency assessment.   
 Narrabri will need to hold an appropriate electrical licence.

matches.target_title: 161 How the work is to be carried out
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical work on energised electrical equipment is carried out: 

(a) by a competent person who has tools, testing equipment and personal protective equipment that: 

(i) are suitable for the work; and (ii) have been properly tested; and (iii) are maintained in good working order; and 

(b) in accordance with a safe work method statement prepared for the work; and   
(c) subject to subregulation (5), with a safety observer present who has the competence and qualifications specified in subregulation (4). 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) The person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure, so far as is reasonably practicable, that the person who carries out the electrical work uses the tools, testing equipment and personal protective equipment properly. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(3) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(b), the safe work method statement must: 

(a) identify the electrical work; and   
(b) specify hazards associated with that electrical work and risks associated with those hazards; and   
(c) describe the measures to be implemented to control the risks; and   
(d) describe how the risk control measures are to be implemented, monitored and reviewed. 

(4) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(c): 

(a) the safety observer must be competent: 

(b) the safety observer must have been assessed in the previous 12 months as competent to rescue and resuscitate a person. 

(5) A safety observer is not required if: 

(a) the work consists only of testing; and   
(b) the person conducting the business or undertaking has conducted a risk assessment under regulation 158(1)(a) that shows that there is no serious risk associated with the proposed work.

compliance.reasoning: The texts are functionally related because both address competency/qualification requirements for people involved in electrical work. However, the POLICY focuses on training/qualification/licensing requirements for individuals appointed to BU or Site ESO roles, based on the type of electrical work performed at a site. The PROVISION imposes specific duties on the PCBU for electrical work on energised electrical equipment: it must be carried out by a competent person with suitable/tested/maintained tools and PPE, in accordance with a safe work method statement (SWMS), and (subject to exceptions) with a competent safety observer who has been assessed within the last 12 months as competent to rescue and resuscitate. The POLICY does not address energised electrical work controls (SWMS requirement, safety observer requirements, rescue/resuscitation assessment currency, or tool/testing/PPE suitability/testing/maintenance), nor does it explicitly require that energised work be performed only by a competent person with those resources. Therefore, the POLICY does not fully satisfy the obligations in the PROVISION.
compliance.remediation: Update the policy (or add a linked procedure) to explicitly require that any electrical work on energised electrical equipment is: (1) performed only by a competent person; (2) performed using suitable tools/testing equipment/PPE that are properly tested and maintained; (3) performed in accordance with a SWMS meeting the provision’s content requirements; (4) performed with a safety observer unless the testing-only + risk assessment exception applies; and (5) ensure safety observers are competent and have been assessed within the last 12 months as competent to rescue and resuscitate. Also define how the PCBU verifies and records competency, SWMS use, equipment testing/maintenance, and observer/rescue assessment currency.

matches.target_title: 155 Duty to determine whether equipment is energised
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that, before electrical work is carried out on electrical equipment, the equipment is tested by a competent person to determine whether or not it is energised. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

Note 

Regulation 157 allows electrical testing to be carried out on electrical equipment for the purposes of this regulation. Regulation 161 sets out how the testing is to be carried out. 

(2) The person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that: 

(a) each exposed part is treated as energised until it is isolated and determined not to be energised; and   
(b) each high-voltage exposed part is earthed after being deenergised. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning: The POLICY sets requirements for BU/Site ESO training/qualification/licensing commensurate with site electrical work. The PROVISION imposes duties on the PCBU to ensure electrical equipment is tested by a competent person before work, and that exposed parts are treated as energised until isolated/tested, and that high-voltage exposed parts are earthed after de-energising. These are functionally related because ensuring competent testing and safe isolation/earthing practices depends on having appropriately competent/qualified personnel. However, the POLICY does not explicitly require (a) pre-work testing to confirm de-energised status by a competent person, (b) treating exposed parts as energised until isolation and confirmation, or (c) earthing of high-voltage exposed parts after de-energising. It also focuses on ESO role competency rather than ensuring the PCBU’s operational controls for all electrical work. Therefore, the POLICY does not fully address the specific obligations in the PROVISION.
compliance.remediation: Amend the POLICY (or add a linked procedure) to explicitly require that before any electrical work: (1) electrical equipment is tested by a competent person to confirm it is not energised (with reference to applicable testing/isolation procedures), (2) exposed parts are treated as energised until isolated and proven de-energised, and (3) high-voltage exposed parts are earthed after de-energising. Define/verify who qualifies as a 'competent person' for testing, require records of testing/isolation/earthing, and ensure ESO competency requirements include these specific controls (or ensure they are mandated for all workers performing/authorising electrical work).

matches.target_title: 158 Preliminary steps
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure the following before electrical work on energised electrical equipment commences: 

(a) a risk assessment is conducted in relation to the proposed electrical work;   
(b) the area where the electrical work is to be carried out is clear of obstructions so as to allow for easy access and exit;   
(c) the point at which the electrical equipment can be disconnected or isolated from its electricity supply is: (i) clearly marked or labelled; and (ii) clear of obstructions so as to allow for easy access and exit by the worker who is to carry out the electrical work or any other competent person; and (iii) capable of being operated quickly;   
(d) the person authorises the electrical work after consulting with the person with management or control of the workplace. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(a), the risk assessment must be: 

(a) conducted by a competent person; and (b) recorded. 

(3) Subregulation (1)(c) does not apply to electrical work on electrical equipment if: 

(a) the work is to be carried out on the supply side of the main switch on the main switchboard for the equipment; and   
(b) the point at which the equipment can be disconnected from its electricity supply is not reasonably accessible from the work location.

matches.target_title: 150 Inspection and testing of electrical equipment
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must ensure that electrical equipment is regularly inspected and tested by a competent person if the electrical equipment is: 

(a) supplied with electricity through an electrical socket outlet; and   
(b) used in an environment in which the normal use of electrical equipment exposes the equipment to operating conditions that are likely to result in damage to the equipment or a reduction in its expected life span, including conditions that involve exposure to moisture, heat, vibration, mechanical damage, corrosive chemicals or dust. 

Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty. 

(2) In the case of electrical equipment that is new and unused at the workplace, the person conducting the business or undertaking: 

(a) is not required to comply with subregulation (1); and (b) must ensure that the equipment is inspected for obvious damage before being used. 

Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty. 

Note 

However, electrical equipment that is unsafe must not be used (see regulation 149). 

(3) The person must ensure that a record of any testing carried out under subregulation (1) is kept until the electrical equipment is: 

(a) next tested; or (b) permanently removed from the workplace or disposed of. 


Maximum penalty: tier I monetary penalty. 

(4) The record of testing: 

(a) must specify the following: (i) the name of the person who carried out the testing; (ii) the date of the testing; (iii) the outcome of the testing; (iv) the date on which the next testing must be carried out; and   
(b) may be in the form of a tag attached to the electrical equipment tested.

compliance.reasoning: The provision requires the PCBU to ensure that certain electrical equipment is regularly inspected and tested by a competent person, and that records of testing are kept with specified details. The policy addresses training/qualification/licensing requirements for individuals appointed to BU or Site ESO roles, ensuring their competency is commensurate with electrical work performed at the site. While this relates to ensuring competent persons are involved in electrical safety activities, the policy does not explicitly require (a) regular inspection and testing of in-scope electrical equipment (socket-supplied equipment used in damaging environments), (b) that such inspection/testing be performed at required intervals, or (c) that testing records be created and retained with the mandated fields. Therefore, the policy only partially aligns (competency concept) and does not demonstrate compliance with the inspection/testing and recordkeeping obligations.
compliance.remediation: Update the policy (or add a linked procedure) to: (1) require regular inspection and testing of electrical equipment that is socket-supplied and used in harsh/damaging environments; (2) define/mandate inspection and testing intervals consistent with applicable WHS guidance/standards; (3) require testing to be performed by a competent person (and define competency criteria for testers, not only ESOs); (4) require pre-use inspection for new/unused equipment for obvious damage; and (5) require recordkeeping until next test or disposal, including tester name, test date, outcome, and next test due date (e.g., via tags and/or a register).

matches.target_title: 267 When is a person competent to inspect plant
matches.target_text:
A person is a competent person to inspect an item of plant for registration if the person has: 

(a) educational or vocational qualifications in an engineering discipline relevant to the plant to be inspected; or

matches.target_title: 240 Maintenance, inspection and testing of amusement devices and passenger ropeways
matches.target_text:
(1) The person with management or control of an amusement device or passenger ropeway at a workplace must ensure that the maintenance, inspection and, if necessary, testing of the device or ropeway is carried out:

(a) by a competent person; and (b) in accordance with:

(i) the recommendations of the designer or manufacturer or designer and manufacturer; or

(ii) if a maintenance manual for the device or ropeway has been prepared by a competent person, the requirements of the maintenance manual.

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

(2) A person is not a competent person to carry out a detailed inspection of an amusement device or passenger ropeway that includes an electrical installation unless the person is qualified, or is assisted by a person who is qualified, to inspect electrical installations.

compliance.reasoning: The provision requires that maintenance/inspection/testing of amusement devices or passenger ropeways be carried out by a competent person, and specifically that a person conducting a detailed inspection that includes an electrical installation must be qualified (or assisted by a qualified person) to inspect electrical installations. The policy addresses training/qualification/licensing for BU or Site ESO roles based on the type of electrical work performed at a site (e.g., competency assessment vs holding an electrical licence). However, it does not explicitly ensure that (a) maintenance/inspection/testing of amusement devices/ropeways is performed by a competent person in accordance with manufacturer/designer recommendations or a competent-person-prepared maintenance manual, nor (b) that detailed inspections involving electrical installations are performed by a qualified electrical installation inspector or assisted by one. Therefore, the policy only partially aligns and does not fully satisfy the provision’s specific requirements.
compliance.remediation: Update the policy to explicitly cover amusement devices/passenger ropeways by requiring: (1) maintenance, inspection and testing be performed by a defined 'competent person' and in accordance with manufacturer/designer recommendations or an approved maintenance manual; and (2) any detailed inspection involving an electrical installation be performed by a person qualified to inspect electrical installations (or performed with documented assistance/supervision by such a qualified person). Define required qualifications/licences for electrical installation inspection and include verification/recordkeeping processes.

matches.target_title: 157 Electrical work on energised electrical equipment—when permitted
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical work on energised electrical equipment is not carried out unless: 

(a) it is necessary in the interests of health and safety that the electrical work is carried out on the equipment while the equipment is energised; or Example 

It may be necessary that life-saving equipment remain energised and operating while electrical work is carried out on the equipment.   
(b) it is necessary that the electrical equipment to be worked on is energised in order for the work to be carried out properly; or   
(c) it is necessary for the purposes of testing required under regulation 155; or   
(d) there is no reasonable alternative means of carrying out the work. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) The electrical work that may be carried out under subregulation (1)(a), (b) and (d) may include testing of the energised electrical equipment.

matches.target_title: 163 Duty of person conducting business or undertaking
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking that includes the carrying out of construction work must comply with AS/NZS 3012:2019 (Electrical installations—Construction and demolition sites). Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.   
(2) For the purposes of subregulation (1), AS/NZS 3012:2019 (Electrical installations— Construction and demolition sites) applies as if any term that is defined in that Standard and that is also defined in the Act or these Regulations has the same meaning as it has in the Act or these Regulations.   
(3) If any requirement in AS/NZS 3012:2019 (Electrical installations— Construction and demolition sites) deals with the same matter as a requirement under this Part, it is sufficient that the person conducting the business or undertaking complies with the requirement in AS/NZS 3012:2019 as modified by subregulation (2).

compliance.reasoning: The provision imposes a duty on a PCBU carrying out construction work to comply with AS/NZS 3012:2019 for electrical installations on construction/demolition sites. The policy addresses training/qualification/licensing requirements for BU/Site Electrical Safety Officer (ESO) roles based on the type of electrical work performed at a site. While both concern electrical safety competence, the policy does not reference construction work contexts, does not require compliance with AS/NZS 3012:2019, and does not establish controls/processes to ensure the PCBU meets the Standard’s requirements. Therefore, the policy is not sufficient to demonstrate compliance with the provision.
compliance.remediation: Update the policy (or add a linked procedure/standard) to explicitly require compliance with AS/NZS 3012:2019 whenever construction work is undertaken, including: defining when work is 'construction work'; assigning responsibility for ensuring AS/NZS 3012 compliance; specifying competency/licensing requirements for roles performing or supervising electrical installation work on construction sites per the Standard; and implementing verification (audits/records) demonstrating compliance with AS/NZS 3012 requirements.

matches.target_title: 146 Meaning of electrical work

compliance.reasoning: The provision defines what constitutes “electrical work” and, by implication, distinguishes tasks that are not electrical work (and therefore may not require licensed/registered electrical worker competency). The policy sets a general requirement that BU/Site ESOs must have training/qualification/licensing commensurate with the electrical work performed at their primary site, and gives examples (Marsfield: competency assessment; Narrabri: appropriate electrical licence). However, the policy does not explicitly align its competency/licensing requirements to the provision’s definition and exclusions (e.g., plug-and-socket connection is excluded; certain replacements like fuses/light bulbs are excluded; supervised assistance is excluded). Without mapping site activities to whether they are “electrical work” under the provision, the policy may over-require licensing for excluded tasks or under-specify requirements for included tasks (e.g., testing/repair/alteration/maintenance of fixed installations). Therefore, it is not demonstrably compliant with the provision’s scope distinctions and lacks clear criteria to ensure the correct level of licensing/competency is applied based on whether tasks fall inside or outside “electrical work.”
compliance.remediation: Update the policy to explicitly reference and incorporate the provision’s definition of “electrical work” and its exclusions. Add a decision framework that: (1) classifies site tasks as electrical work vs excluded activities; (2) specifies minimum competency/assessment requirements for excluded tasks (if any) and licensing/registration requirements for electrical work; (3) documents how each site’s task profile (portable vs fixed equipment; testing/repair/maintenance) drives ESO qualification/licensing requirements; and (4) requires periodic review when site electrical task scope changes.

matches.target_title: 147 Risk management
matches.target_text:
A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must manage risks to health and safety associated with electrical risks at the workplace, in accordance with Part 3.1. 

Example 

Electrical risks associated with the design, construction, installation, protection, maintenance and testing of electrical equipment and electrical installations at a workplace. 

Note 

WHS Act—section 19 (see regulation 9).

compliance.reasoning: The provision requires the PCBU to manage risks to health and safety associated with electrical risks at the workplace (covering design, installation, protection, maintenance and testing of electrical equipment/installations) in accordance with Part 3.1. The policy addresses only one control element: ensuring BU/Site ESOs have training/qualification/licensing commensurate with the electrical work performed at their primary site (e.g., competency assessment vs electrical licence). While this supports risk management, it does not establish a broader risk management process (hazard identification, risk assessment, implementation of controls, maintenance/testing regimes, and ongoing review) as required by the provision. Therefore, the policy is only partially aligned and does not, on its face, demonstrate compliance with the full obligation to manage electrical risks.
compliance.remediation: Expand the policy (or reference a linked procedure) to explicitly require electrical risk management in accordance with Part 3.1, including: identifying electrical hazards/risks across equipment and installations; assessing risks; implementing and maintaining controls (e.g., isolation/LOTO, RCDs, inspection/testing schedules, competent persons, safe work procedures); documenting responsibilities (including ESO role in the system); and periodic review/audit of controls and incidents.

source_text:
A formal letter of appointment to the position of BU ESO or Site ESO is to be issued by the Business Unit/Function Director.

Prior to the appointment taking place, the proposed candidates completed verification of electrical competency should be reviewed to confirm they meet the applicable competency requirements.

On the appointment of a new BU and/or Site ESO, notify your HSE Manager and/or local HSE Advisor to ensure the ESO contact list on MyCSIRO is updated.

matches.target_title: 177 Appointment of competent person to supervise diving work
matches.target_text:
A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must appoint 1 or more competent persons to: 

(a) supervise general diving work carried out in the business or undertaking; and (b) perform other functions under this Division. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

matches.target_title: 148 Electrical equipment and electrical installations to which this Division applies
matches.target_text: In this Division, a reference to electrical equipment or an electrical installation in relation to a person conducting a business or undertaking is a reference to electrical equipment or an electrical installation that is under the person's management or control.

matches.target_title: 158 Preliminary steps

(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure the following before electrical work on energised electrical equipment commences: 

(a) a risk assessment is conducted in relation to the proposed electrical work;   
(b) the area where the electrical work is to be carried out is clear of obstructions so as to allow for easy access and exit;   
(c) the point at which the electrical equipment can be disconnected or isolated from its electricity supply is: (i) clearly marked or labelled; and (ii) clear of obstructions so as to allow for easy access and exit by the worker who is to carry out the electrical work or any other competent person; and (iii) capable of being operated quickly;

matches.target_title: 159 Unauthorised access to equipment being worked on
matches.target_text:
A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that only persons authorised by the person conducting the business or undertaking enter the immediate area in which electrical work on energised electrical equipment is being carried out. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

matches.target_title: 160 Contact with equipment being worked on
matches.target_text:
A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that, while electrical work is being carried out on energised electrical equipment, all persons are prevented from creating an electrical risk by inadvertently making contact with an exposed energised component of the equipment. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

matches.target_text:

(a) supplied with electricity through an electrical socket outlet; and   
(b) used in an environment in which the normal use of electrical equipment exposes the equipment to operating conditions that are likely to result in damage to the equipment or a reduction in its expected life span, including conditions that involve exposure to moisture, heat, vibration, mechanical damage, corrosive chemicals or dust. 

Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty.
